ref: In the beginning God created the heavens and the earth.
text: A nyini piilimka po ate Wen a nye wenzukku ale tɛŋka la.

ref: The earth was barren, with no form of life; it was under a roaring ocean covered with darkness. But the Spirit of God was moving over the water. The First Day
text: Tɛŋka a jam ka magsa ale chim tɛŋ koruk. Legi nna kpitɔ ale jam a lik nya liuŋtaŋa ate Wen Chiika a yaa bo nyiamu zuk a ja.

ref: God said, “I command light to shine!” And light started shining.
text: Ate Wen yaa weeni ayen, <<Ku be nyaksi a nya,>> ate ku yaa a nya.

ref: God looked at the light and saw that it was good. He separated light from darkness
text: Wen a nya ayen, ku la a nya la a nala, ate wa gaari nyaka legini po.

ref: and named the light “Day” and the darkness “Night.” Evening came, then morning—that was the first day. The Second Day
text: Wen yaa wi ku le nyaksi a nya la ayen kantueŋ ale a wi legini ayen yok. Ate saluik ale junɔi yaa deri boro. Piilimka danni jam le la.

ref: God said, “I command a dome to separate the water above it from the water below it.”
text: Wen yaa ŋman weeni ayen, <<Basi ate peelim a bo nyaataŋa suŋsuŋ a gaari ti tɔgatɔga.>>

ref: and named it “Sky.” Evening came, then morning—that was the second day. The Third Day
text: Wen yaa wi ŋmazukku ayen wen. Ate saluik ale junɔi a ŋman boro. Da dila jam saari ka daa ŋaye.

ref: God said, “I command the water under the sky to come together in one place, so there will be dry ground.” And that's what happened.
text: Wen yaa ŋman weeni ayen, <<Basi ate nya buui ale bo tɛŋka la a chuiri ga duek jigi yeŋ ate tɛŋ kosik a nya.>> Ate ku nye dila.

ref: God named the dry ground “Land,” and he named the water “Ocean.” God looked at what he had done and saw that it was good.
text: Wen yaa wi tɛŋ kosikka ayen tɛŋ alege a wi nya tii ale chuiri dua jigi yeŋ ka la ayen nyakpiem. Wen yaa nya ate ku nala te wa.

ref: God said, “I command the earth to produce all kinds of plants, including fruit trees and grain.” And that's what happened.
text: Wen yaa weeni ayen, <<Basi ate ŋanta tɔgatɔga ale tiisa a nyini tɛŋka zuk ate ba a yɔni yɔna a ta bie a va ba buuni ale ka diila.>> Ate ku nye dila.

ref: The earth produced all kinds of vegetation. God looked at what he had done, and it was good.
text: Ate ŋanta tɔgatɔga ale tiisa a nyini tɛŋka zuk a yɔni yɔna a ta bie a va ba buuni ale ka diila. Wen yaa nya ate ku nala te wa.

ref: Evening came, then morning—that was the third day. The Fourth Day
text: Ate saluik ale junɔi a ŋman boro. Da dila jam saari ka daa ŋata.

ref: God said, “I command lights to appear in the sky and to separate day from night and to show the time for seasons, special days, and years.
text: Wen yaa ŋman weeni ayen, <<Basi ate ŋan nyaksinta a bo wenzukku a gaari yok ale kantueŋ. Ŋan bala mɛ ale ba gaari wenkarik ale yoei ale daa ale bena.

ref: I command them to shine on the earth.” And that's what happened.
text: Bala mɛ ale ba bo wenzukku a nyaksi ate tɛŋka zuk a nya.>> Ate ku nye dila.

ref: God made two powerful lights, the brighter one to rule the day and the other to rule the night. He also made the stars.
text: Wen a nye ka ŋan nyaksin kpienta baye wenbinni ale chiika. Wenbinni ale soa kantueŋka ate chiika a soa yokku. Wa nye chiŋmarisaŋa mɛ.

ref: Then God put these lights in the sky to shine on the earth,
text: Wen a nye ba kama a basi wenzukku ayen tɛŋka zuk a nya,

ref: to rule day and night, and to separate light from darkness. God looked at what he had done, and it was good.
text: wenbinni a nya kantueŋ abe chiika mɛ a nya yokku. Wenbinni le chiika ale poori legini. Wen yaa nya ayen ku nala a te wa.

ref: Evening came, then morning—that was the fourth day. The Fifth Day
text: Ate saluik ale junɔi a ŋman boro. Da dila a saari ka daa ŋanaansi.

ref: God said, “I command the ocean to be full of living creatures, and I command birds to fly above the earth.”
text: Wen yaa ŋman weeni ayen, <<Basi ate nyiamu a ta ŋanvuta abe nuinsa a yiri wenzukku.>>

ref: So God made the giant sea monsters and all the living creatures that swim in the ocean. He also made every kind of bird. God looked at what he had done, and it was good.
text: Dila Wen yaa nye nyiam po ŋan kpienta ale ŋan fiisa ate ba bo nyiamu po a cheŋ, ba jam ka ŋanta tɔgatɔga a bo nyiamu po. Wa nye nuinsa mɛ tɔgatɔga. Wen yaa nya ate ku nala a te wa.

ref: Then he gave the living creatures his blessing—he told the ocean creatures to increase and live everywhere in the ocean and the birds to increase everywhere on earth.
text: Wen yaa niak ŋantaŋa ale weeni ayen ba biak abe ba wɔgri a sueri nyiamu po meena abe nuinsaŋa a wɔgri a sueri tɛŋka.

ref: Evening came, then morning—that was the fifth day. The Sixth Day
text: Ate saluik ale junɔi a ŋman boro. Da dila a saari ka daa ŋanu.

ref: God said, “I command the earth to give life to all kinds of tame animals, wild animals, and reptiles.” And that's what happened.
text: Wen ŋman weeni ayen, <<Basi ate ŋanvuta tɔgatɔga a bo tɛŋka zuk, yeri duŋsa, ŋan baai meena ale vuuri a cheŋ ale gɔi duŋsa ba meena ba tɔga ba tɔga.>> Ate ku nye dila.

ref: God made every one of them. Then he looked at what he had done, and it was good.
text: Wen a nye gɔi duŋsa buuni ale yeri duŋsa buuni ale ŋan baai ale vuuri a cheŋ la buuni. Ate Wen nya ate ku nala te wa nna yegayega.

ref: God said, “Now we will make humans, and they will be like us. We will let them rule the fish, the birds, and all other living creatures.”
text: Wen yaa ŋman weeni ayen, <<Ni te ti nye nuru ase tidek la ate wa a neensi ti ate wa a nya nyakpiemu jumaŋa ale nuinsaŋa, ale bo wenzukku la ale duŋsaŋa ale bo tɛŋka zuk ale ŋan baai meena ale vuuri a cheŋ la zuk.>>

ref: So God created humans to be like himself; he made men and women.
text: Dila Wen yaa nye nuru ase wadek la, wadek ale ka diila ate wa nye wa, nidoa ale nipok ate wa nye.

ref: God gave them his blessing and said: Have a lot of children! Fill the earth with people and bring it under your control. Rule over the fish in the ocean, the birds in the sky, and every animal on the earth.
text: Wen a niak ba ale weeni ba ayen, <<Ni biak a sueri tɛŋka a nya ka zuk. Ayen ba a nya jumaŋa ale bo nyakpiemu po la, ale nuinsaŋa ale bo wenzukku la, ale ŋanvutaŋa meena ale a cheŋ tɛŋka zuk la.>>

ref: And I have given the green plants as food for everything else that breathes, including animals, both wild and tame, and birds. And so it was.
text: M pai duŋsaŋa ale nuinsa ale ŋan baai meena ale bo tɛŋka zuk a cheŋ ate ba ta nyuvuri la te ni. M pai va sobta meena a te ba ayen ba chim ba ŋandiinta.>> Ate ku nye dila.

ref: God looked at what he had done. All of it was very good! Evening came, then morning—that was the sixth day.
text: Wen yaa nya wa ale nye dii meena la ate ku nala te wa. Ate saluik ale junɔi a ŋman boro. Da dila a saari ka daa ŋayuebi.

ref: So the heavens and the earth and everything else were created. The Seventh Day
text: Dila la Wen yaa nye jaab meena tɔgatɔga ale bo wenzukku ale tɛŋka la a nueri.

ref: By the seventh day God had finished his work, and so he rested.
text: Daa ŋayopɔiwa danni ale Wen nueri tuim ŋaai meena ate wa tom la kama, dila daa ŋayopɔiwa danni wa yaa vuusi.

ref: God blessed the seventh day and made it special, because on that day he rested from his work.
text: Wen yaa niak dai dila danni a basi ate di chim tuilum dai, dii nyiŋla da dila wa vuusi kama, wa an ŋman tom tuini.

ref: That's how God created the heavens and the earth. The Garden of Eden When the Lord God made the heavens and the earth,
text: Dii meena ale magsi chaab ale wenzukku ale tɛŋka nyeka la ale la. Naawen dii ale ka Wen la ale nye tɛŋka ale wenzukku la

ref: no grass or plants were growing anywhere. God had not yet sent any rain, and there was no one to work the land.
text: tiisa ale wuuta an diem jam nyini ya, diila Naawen dii ale ka Wen la an basi ate ŋmoruk a ni ya, nuru mɛ abe karo a tom.

ref: But streams came up from the ground and watered the earth.
text: Alege nyiam ale nyini tɛŋ jueli a yɔk tɛŋka ŋmazuk meena.

ref: The Lord God took some soil from the ground and made a man. God breathed life into the man, and the man started breathing.
text: Naawen dii ale ka Wen la yaa nye tantaŋa a mɛ nuru a yaa vuusi basi wa nyueŋa po ate wa chim nuru vuk.

ref: The Lord made a garden in a place called Eden, which was in the east, and he put the man there.
text: Yɔgyɔgla de Naawen dii ale ka Wen la yaa nye talim kpiem Eden po kori geŋka a yaa basi ate nuruwa ate wa nye la a bo du.

ref: The Lord God filled the garden with all kinds of beautiful trees and fruit trees. Two other trees were in the middle of the garden. One of these gave life—the other gave the wisdom to know the difference between right and wrong.
text: Naawen dii ale ka Wen la a yaa basi ate tiisa tɔgatɔga a nyini. Sii ale nala nina po ale magsi dɛka. Talimu suŋsuŋ nyuvuri tiib ale jam za du. Nalim sebika ale biem sebika tiib ale maa za du.

ref: From Eden a river flowed out to water the garden, then it divided into four rivers.
text: Beli ale jam bo Eden a chali a zaŋti talimu. Di jam poori ka beli naŋsa sinaansi.

ref: The first one is the Pishon River that flows through the land of Havilah,
text: Dii ale dɛ niŋ la yuei ale Pison, di chali va ka Yavila tɛŋka po a taam. Ligmɔniŋ ale jam bo du la.

ref: The second is the Gihon River that winds through Ethiopia.
text: Beli dii ale jam nye ŋayewa la yuei ale Giyon, di chali va ka Kus tɛŋka po a taam.

ref: The Tigris River that flows east of Assyria is the third, and the fourth is the Euphrates River.
text: Beli dii ale jam nye ŋatawa la yuei ale Tigris, di jam chali va ka Assur kori geŋka a taam. Beli dii ale nye ŋanaansiwa la yuei jam le Yufiretis.

ref: The Lord God put the man in the Garden of Eden to take care of it and to look after it.
text: Naawen dii ale ka Wen la yaa pai nuruwa a basi Eden Talimu po ayen wa a tom abe wa a nya bu zuk.

ref: But the Lord told him, “You may eat fruit from any tree in the garden,
text: Naawen dii ale ka Wen la yaa weeni nuruwa ayen fi ta suik a dɛ tiib buui yɔna kama,

ref: except the one that has the power to let you know the difference between right and wrong. If you eat any fruit from that tree, you will die before the day is over!”
text: alege kan dɛ nalim ale biem sebika tiimu yɔnaŋa, diila fi dan dɛ fi sum a kpi kama.

ref: The Lord God said, “It isn't good for the man to live alone. I will make a suitable partner for him.”
text: Naawen dii ale ka Wen la yaa ŋman weeni ayen, <<Ku an nala ayen nidoawa a boro wadek wa nyiini. M le nye ka maarɔ magsi a te wa.>>

ref: So the Lord God made him fall into a deep sleep, and he took out one of the man's ribs. Then after closing the man's side,
text: Dila Naawen dii ale ka Wen la yaa nye ate nidoawa a lo goom po nna yegayega. Wa ale a goa la, ate Wen yaa yeeri wa nyipik kobini dinyi alege lik kauk kula.

ref: the Lord made a woman out of the rib. The Lord God brought her to the man,
text: Naawen dii ale ka Wen la yaa pai nidoawa nyipik kobini ate wa yeeri la a nye nipok a pai te nidoawa.

ref: and the man exclaimed, “Here is someone like me! She is part of my body, my own flesh and bones. She came from me, a man. So I will name her Woman!”
text: Nidoawa yaa weeni ayen, <<Yɔgyɔgla de ka m dek kobi ale nna. Ka m dek nyiŋ lam ale nna, ba le wi wa ayen ka nipok, diila wa nyini ka mi jigi.>>

ref: That's why a man will leave his own father and mother. He marries a woman, and the two of them become like one person.
text: Dila nyiŋla, ku a fɛ kama ate nidoa basi wa ko ale wa ma, abe wa gum chaab ale wa powa, ate bayewa a chim nyiŋ yeŋ.

ref: Although the man and his wife were both naked, they were not ashamed.
text: Nidoawa ale nipowa a jam ta ka chaboala alege kan chali chivie.

ref: The snake was sneakier than any of the other wild animals that the Lord God had made. One day it came to the woman and asked, “Did God tell you not to eat fruit from any tree in the garden?”
text: Duŋsaŋa meena ate Naawen dii ale ka Wen la jam nye la po, waamu ale jam seba a gaam si meena. Bu yaa weeni nipowa ayen, <<Wen sum weeni kama ayen, <Ni kan dɛ tiisaŋa ale bo talimu po la yɔnaŋa?> >>

ref: The woman answered, “God said we could eat fruit from any tree in the garden,
text: Nipowa yaa weeni waamu ayen, <<Ti baga a dɛ tiisaŋa yɔnaŋa kama,

ref: except the one in the middle. He told us not to eat fruit from that tree or even to touch it. If we do, we will die.”
text: alege Wen weeni kama ayen ti kan dɛ buui ale za talimu suŋsuŋ la yaase ti tiri bu. Ti dan nye dila, ti le kpi.>>

ref: “No, you won't!” the snake replied.
text: Waamu yaa weeni nipowa ayen, <<Ni kan kpi.

ref: “God understands what will happen on the day you eat fruit from that tree. You will see what you have done, and you will know the difference between right and wrong, just as God does.”
text: Wen sebi kama ayen ni dan dɛ ni ninaŋa ale lagri ate ni a nye ase Wen la, a yaa seba nalim ale biem.>>

ref: The woman stared at the fruit. It looked beautiful and tasty. She wanted the wisdom that it would give her, and she ate some of the fruit. Her husband was there with her, so she gave some to him, and he ate it too.
text: Nipowa yaa nya ayen yɔnni a nala nina po a magsi dɛka. Ku be ŋman magsi mɛ ayen wa ta yam. Wa yaa gbesi yɔnni dɛ alege te wa chorɔwa le bo ale wa la ate wa mɛ dɛ.

ref: At once they saw what they had done, and they realized they were naked. Then they sewed fig leaves together to cover themselves.
text: Ba nɔi bayewa nina deri lagri ate ba deri miŋ ayen ba ta ka chaboala. Ate ba ya yaali kiŋkaŋ vaata a baali tom chaab a lik badek.

ref: Late in the afternoon, when the breeze began to blow, the man and woman heard the Lord God walking in the garden. So they hid behind some trees. Sin Brings a Curse
text: Dai dila danni dek junɔi ate ku yɔk nna chorototo la, nidoawa ale wa powa yaa wom Naawen dii ale ka Wen la a cheŋ talimu po, ba yaa suk tiisaŋa po.

ref: The Lord God called out to the man and asked, “Where are you?”
text: Naawen dii ale ka Wen la yaa wi nidoawa ayen, <<Fi bo ka bɛ?>>

ref: The man answered, “I was naked, and when I heard you walking through the garden, I was frightened and hid!”
text: Nidoawa yaa tulisi ayen, <<Mi wom ka fi le a tabi la yaa chali yɔgsum, a yaa suk, diila mi ta ka chaboala.>>

ref: “How did you know you were naked?” God asked. “Did you eat any fruit from that tree in the middle of the garden?”
text: Naawen yaa bek ayen, <<Ka wana ale weeni fu ayen fi ta chaboala? Fi dɛ tiib buui ate mi a weeni ayen fi kan dɛ bu yɔnni la kama?>>

ref: “It was the woman you put here with me,” the man said. “She gave me some of the fruit, and I ate it.”
text: Nidoawa yaa tulisi ayen, <<Nipowa ate fi pai te mu la, ale pai tiimu yɔnni a te mi ate mi dɛ.>>

ref: The Lord God then asked the woman, “What have you done?” “The snake tricked me,” she answered, “and I ate some of that fruit.”
text: Naawen dii ale ka Wen la yaa bek nipowa ayen, <<Ka boa ale la ate fi nye?>> Nipowa yaa tulisi ayen, <<Waamu ale paasi mi ate mi dɛ yɔnni.>>

ref: So the Lord God said to the snake: “Because of what you have done, you will be the only animal to suffer this curse— For as long as you live, you will crawl on your stomach and eat dirt.
text: Dila Naawen dii ale ka Wen la yaa weeni waamu, <<Fi ale nye nnala la nyiŋ, m yeeri fu kama yeri duŋsa ale gɔi duŋsa po a ka a te fu. Fi ale kasim a vuuri ka fi lɔgni zuk a cheŋ abe fi a dɛ tanta fi boka meena po.

ref: You and this woman will hate each other; your descendants and hers will always be enemies. One of hers will strike you on the head, and you will strike him on the heel.”
text: Abe m basi ate dachaani a bo fi ale nipowa suŋsuŋ abe di bo fi buuni ale nipowa buuni suŋsuŋ. Fi ale dom wa nankpanni ate wa mɛ tabi fi zukku a ŋɔriŋ.>>

ref: Then the Lord God said to the woman, “You will suffer terribly when you give birth. But you will still desire your husband, and he will rule over you.”
text: Alege weeni nipowa ayen, <<Fi dan boro a biak biik fi nyiŋdommaŋa ale zuak nna yegayega. Fi le kasim a biak bisa ale ka nyiŋdomma. Fi le kasim a yaali fi chorɔwa abe wa a nya fi zuk.>>

ref: The Lord said to the man, “You listened to your wife and ate the fruit I told you not to eat. And so, the ground will be under a curse because of what you did. As long as you live, you will have to struggle to grow enough food.
text: Alege a weeni nidoawa ayen, <<Fi le wom fi powa nɔi a siak dɛ tiimu ate m weeni ayen fi kan dɛ bu yɔnni la, fi nyiŋ m ka basi tɛŋka zuk. Fi le kasim a cheri ka wulum abe fi nya ŋandiinta a dɛ fi boka meena po.

ref: Your food will be plants, but the ground will produce thorns and thistles.
text: M le basi ate tɛŋka a ta wuuk ale munsa ate fi nam a nya ŋanta a dɛ.

ref: You will sweat all your life to earn a living; you were made out of soil, and you will once again turn into soil.”
text: Wulum ale fi za fi cheri a nya ŋandiinta a dɛ ate fi pilim ŋman siŋ tɛŋ, fi le tɔlim nyini ka tanta po la. Fi ka tanta, fi be le ŋman cheŋ ka tantaŋa po.>>

ref: The man Adam named his wife Eve because she would become the mother of all who live.
text: Nidoawa yuei jam ale Adam ate wa yaa te wa powa yuei ayen Iiv, dii nyiŋla wa za wa chim ka saalɔ meena ma.

ref: Then the Lord God made clothes out of animal skins for the man and his wife.
text: Naawen dii ale ka Wen la a yaa pai duŋsa gbaŋsa a nye gatta a te Adam ale Iiv ate ba lik badek.

ref: The Lord said, “They now know the difference between right and wrong, just as we do. But they must not be allowed to eat fruit from the tree that lets them live forever.”
text: Naawen dii ale ka Wen la yaa weeni ayen, <<Nuruwa yaa nye se ka tama la, a yaa seba nalim ale biem. Wa kan ŋman laari nisa a gbesi nyuvuri tiimu yɔnni a dɛ ate wa kan kpi.>>

ref: So the Lord God sent them out of the Garden of Eden, where they would have to work the ground from which the man had been made.
text: Dila Naawen dii ale ka Wen la yaa yiak wa Eden Talimu po ayen wa nyini ga a kpa tɛŋ kaai ate Wen nye tantaŋa a nye wa la.

ref: Then God put winged creatures at the entrance to the garden and a flaming, flashing sword to guard the way to the life-giving tree.
text: Wa le yiak wa nueri la, wa yaa basi ate ŋanvuta a za Eden Talimu kori geŋ ale gebi kpieŋ a yuk ayen suik kan boro a taam nyuvuri tiimu jigini.

ref: Adam and Eve had a son. Then Eve said, “I'll name him Cain because I got him with the help of the Lord.”
text: Adam yaa goa ale wa powa Iiv ate wa yik lɔgi a biak Keen. Iiv yaa weeni ayen Naawen maarika nyiŋ wa biak ka nidoa.

ref: Later she had another son and named him Abel. Abel became a sheep farmer, but Cain farmed the land.
text: Ku ŋaaŋnyiŋ wa yaa biak Keen yoawa Abel. Abel a jam a wuuk ka piisa alege Keen a kpa.

ref: One day, Cain gave part of his harvest to the Lord,
text: Ku yaa jam paari diipo ate Keen yaa ta wa kpanni ŋanta gela jam ayen wa te Naawen.

ref: and Abel also gave an offering to the Lord. He killed the first-born lamb from one of his sheep and gave the Lord the best parts of it. The Lord was pleased with Abel and his offering,
text: Abel mɛ ko wa pai ŋaai ate wa piisaŋa a liŋ biak la a yaa ta kab tii ale ta kpaam la jam ayen wa te Naawen. Naawen yaa tuesi Abel tiirimu

ref: but not with Cain and his offering. This made Cain so angry that he could not hide his feelings.
text: alege zɛri Keen tiirimu. Dila nyiŋ Keen sui yaa puuri ale kaasi nina.

ref: The Lord said to Cain: What's wrong with you? Why do you look so angry?
text: Naawen yaa bek Keen ayen, <<Ka boa ale soa ate fi sui a puuri? Ka boa le soa ate fi kaasi nina?

ref: If you had done the right thing, you would be smiling. But you did the wrong thing, and now sin is waiting to attack you like a lion. Sin wants to destroy you, but don't let it!
text: Fi dan nye dii ale magsi la, m ti a siak kama, alege fi dan kan nye dii ale magsi la, be seba ale biem ale a yaali ayen bu jo fu. Bu a yaali ayen bu nya ka pagrim fi zuk, alege ku a fɛ ate fi nya pagrim biem zuk.>>

ref: Cain said to his brother Abel, “Let's go for a walk.” And when they were out in a field, Cain attacked and killed him.
text: Keen yaa weeni wa yoawa Abel ayen, te ti nyini a cheŋ peelim. Ba ale cheŋ la Keen yaa nak wa yoawa Abel a ko.

ref: Afterwards the Lord asked Cain, “Where is Abel?” “How should I know?” he answered. “Am I supposed to look after my brother?”
text: Naawen a yaa bek Keen ayen, <<Fi yoawa Abel bo ka bɛ?>> Keen yaa tulisi ayen, <<Mi a zɛ. Ka mi ale chim yoawa nyaarɔ?>>

ref: Then the Lord said: Why have you done this terrible thing? You killed your own brother, and his blood flowed onto the ground. Now his blood is calling out for me to punish you.
text: Naawen yaa weeni ayen, <<M nya fi ale ko fi yoawa la kama. Wa ziimu ale siŋ tɛŋka la a weeni mu kama ayen m namsi fu.

ref: When you try to farm the land, it won't produce anything for you. From now on, you'll be without a home, and you'll spend the rest of your life wandering from place to place.
text: Fi dan kpa fi kan nya jaaboa. Fi le kasim a nyiem tɛŋka meena, fi kan nya jigi a vuusi.>>

ref: “You're making me leave my home and live far from you. I will have to wander about without a home, and just anyone could kill me.”
text: Jinla fi yiak mu a basi kama, fi be kan bo ale mu. M le kasim a nyiem ka tɛŋka meena a ka jigiya a vuusi. Waai a dan nya mu wa a ko mu kama.>>

ref: “No!” the Lord answered. “Anyone who kills you will be punished seven times worse than I am punishing you.” So the Lord put a mark on Cain to warn everyone not to kill him.
text: Alege Naawen yaa weeni wa ayen, <<Daa di, waai a dan ko fu wa le nya ku tutuna naŋtuok nɔi buyopɔi.>> Naawen yaa dueni daana wa nyiŋ ate waai a dan nya wa abe wa kan ko wa.

ref: But Cain had to go far from the Lord and live in the Land of Wandering, which is east of Eden. More and More People
text: Keen yaa basi Naawen ale cheŋ ga bo Nod tɛŋka. Nod a bo ka Eden kori geŋ.

ref: Later, Cain and his wife had a son named Enoch. At the time Cain was building a town, and so he named it Enoch after his son.
text: Keen yaa goa ale wa powa ate wa yik lɔgi a biak Ɛnok. Dila po Keen jam a sɛ ka tɛŋ kpieŋ ate wa yaa pai wa nidoabinni yuenni a te tɛŋka a wi ayen Ɛnok.

ref: Then Enoch had a son named Irad, who had a son named Mehujael, who had a son named Methushael, who had a son named Lamech.
text: Ɛnok yaa jam biak Irad, ate Irad a biak Meyujael, ate Meyujael a biak Metusael, ate Metusael a biak Lamek.

ref: Lamech and Adah had two sons, Jabal and Jubal. Their son Jabal was the first to live in tents and raise sheep and goats. Jubal was the first to play harps and flutes.
text: Wa yua yuei jam le Jubal. Jubal jam ka baai ale a yaali kpanta nakka ale paampuuŋta peeka la ko.

ref: Lamech and Zillah had a son named Tubal Cain who made tools out of bronze and iron. They also had a daughter, whose name was Naamah.
text: Zilla mɛ a jam biak ka nidoabili a te wa yuei ayen, Tubal Keen. Tubal Keen a jam ka choabiik. Wa jam ta ka toa, wa yuei ale Naama.

ref: Anyone who tries to get even with me will be punished ten times more than anyone who tries to get even with Cain.”
text: Keen a dan a nya tutuna naŋtuok nɔi buyopɔi, dan ka di Lamek le nya ka naŋtuok nɔi pisiyopɔi ale buyopɔi.

ref: Adam and his wife had another son. They named him Seth, because they said, “God has given us a son to take the place of Abel, who was killed by his brother Cain.”
text: Adam yaa ŋman goa ale wa powa ate wa biak nidoabili ate ba te wa yuei ayen Sɛti. Ba jam weeni kama ayen Wen ŋman te ba ka biik ate wa tagri Abel ate Keen ko la zuk.

ref: Later, Seth had a son and named him Enosh. About this time people started worshiping the Lord.
text: Sɛti mɛ a biak nidoabili a te wa yuei ayen Enos. Dila powa ate nuruba yaa jam piilim a puusi Naawen yuei.

ref: God created men and women to be like himself. He gave them his blessing and called them human beings. The following is a list of the descendants of Adam, the first man:
text: Wa jam nye ka nidoa le nipok a yaa niak ba. Wa le nye ba la, wa jam a wi ba ayen ka nuruba.

ref: and died at the age of
text: Adam jam paari ka bena kobsaneok ale pisita alege kpi.

ref: and died at the age of
text: Sɛti benaŋa meena jam ka kobsaneok ale pi ale ŋaye alege kpi.

ref: and died at the age of
text: Enos benaŋa meena jam ka kobsaneok ale ŋanu alege kpi.

ref: and died at the age of
text: Kenan benaŋa meena jam ka bena kobsaneok ale pi alege kpi.

ref: and died at the age of
text: Metusela benaŋa meena jam ka kobsaneok ale pisiyuebi ale neok alege kpi.

ref: Lamech said, “I'll name him Noah because he will give us comfort, as we struggle hard to make a living on this land that the Lord has put under a curse.”
text: Wa jam te wa yuei ayen ka Nowa. Wa jam weeni kama ayen Naawen ale ka te tɛŋka ate ti a tom ale ti nisima a nam diila, biika de ale yɔk ti sue.

ref: and died at the age of
text: Lamek benaŋa meena jam ka kobsiyopɔi ale pisiyopɔi le ŋayopɔi alege kpi.

ref: More and more people were born, until finally they spread all over the earth. Some of their daughters were so beautiful that supernatural beings came down and married the ones they wanted.
text: Wen nidoabilisaŋa a yaa nya ba ale nala diila ba yaa lueri a faari baai ate ba a yaali la.

ref: Then the Lord said, “I won't let my life-giving breath remain in anyone forever. No one will live for more than
text: Naawen yaa weeni ayen mi Chiik kan bo ale nurubiik taam wuu, diila wa ka waai ale a kpi. Wa daaŋa tɛŋzuk de ka bena kook le pisiye.

ref: The children of the supernatural beings who had married these women became famous heroes and warriors. They were called Nephilim and lived on the earth at that time and even later.
text: Nefilim demma ale jam bo tɛŋka zuk da ŋala po. Ate ku ŋaaŋnyiŋ Wen nidoabilisaŋa yaa ga goa ale nuruba lieba ate ba biak bisa. Ba jam ka ganduata, baai ale ta yuei.

ref: The Lord saw how bad the people on earth were and that everything they thought and planned was evil.
text: Naawen yaa nya ayen nurubiik biem yaa zuak ka nna yegayega. Wa popolaŋa ale wa suinummu biem kasim sueri ba ka daaŋa meena.

ref: and he said, “I'm going to destroy every person on earth! I'll even wipe out animals, birds, and reptiles. I'm sorry I ever made them.”
text: Dila Naawen yaa weeni ayen, wa le ko nurubiik meena ate wa nye la, nuruba ale duŋsa, ŋan baai ale a cheŋ ba lɔga zuk, ale wenzuk nuinsa, diila m sui kaasi ka yegayega m le nye ba la.

ref: But the Lord was pleased with Noah,
text: Alege Nowa nyeka jam nala Naawen nina po.

ref: and this is the story about him. Noah was the only person who lived right and obeyed God.
text: Nowa ale jam ka diila ale nna. Nowa jam ka nuru maŋ, da ŋala po nuru an baga a dak wa kaasuŋŋoa, wa jam a cheŋ ale Wen kama.

ref: He had three sons: Shem, Ham, and Japheth.
text: Nowa jam ta ka nidoabilisa bata. Ba yue jam ale Sɛm ale Yɛm ale Jafet.

ref: God knew that everyone was terribly cruel and violent.
text: Wen yaa nya tɛŋka kaasi nna yegayega diila nuruma nyeka an bola nala.

ref: Get some good lumber and build a boat. Put rooms in it and cover it with tar inside and out.
text: Be nye ŋaaruŋ ale daata a magsi nidek. Abe fi nye diina ku po a pai kotaali a lik paaŋa ŋaaŋ ale popo meena.

ref: Noah did everything God told him to do.
text: Nowa yaa nye dii meena ti Wen weeni wa la.

ref: I'm going to send a flood that will destroy everything that breathes! Nothing will be left alive.
text: <<M le basi ate nyiam jam lik tɛŋka meena a ko javuk meena ale bo tɛŋka zuk la. Jaabuuri meena ale ta nyuvuri la. Jaab meena tɛŋka zuk ale kpi.

ref: But I solemnly promise that you, your wife, your sons, and your daughters-in-law will be kept safe in the boat.
text: Alege mi ale fi ale nye nɔiyeŋ ate fi ale fi nidoabilisa ale fi powa ale fi nidoabilisaŋa pooma a jo ŋaaruŋku po.

ref: Take into the boat with you a male and a female of every kind of animal and bird, as well as a male and a female of every reptile. I don't want them to be destroyed.
text: Nuinsa buuri siye siye, ale duŋsa buuri siye siye, ale ŋan baai ale duiri tɛŋ a cheŋ baye baye, ale jam fi jigi ate fi ta ba ate ba vua.

ref: Store up enough food both for yourself and for them.
text: Pa ŋandiin tii buuri ale magsi dɛka a ta jo magsi fi ale ba.>>

ref: The Lord told Noah: Take your whole family with you into the boat, because you are the only one on this earth who pleases me.
text: Naawen yaa weeni Nowa ayen, <<Jo ŋaaruŋku po, fi ale fi dɔkdemma meena dii nyiŋla m nya kama ayen fi ale wayɔrisima nyɔnɔ nuruma de po.

ref: Take seven pairs of every kind of animal that can be used for sacrifice and one pair of all others.
text: Lueri duŋ sii buuri ale magsi Wen tiirim teka la siyopɔi, jaduok ale jasari ale sii ale kan magsi Naawen tiirim teka la siye, jaduok ale jasari,

ref: Also take seven pairs of every kind of bird with you. Do this so there will always be animals and birds on the earth.
text: ale nuinsa buuni siyopɔi, jaduok ale jasari ate si buuni a vua a bo tɛŋka meena.

ref: He obeyed God and took a male and a female of each kind of animal and bird into the boat with him.
text: ŋanduata ale ŋansaa yaa jam Nowa jigi a jo ŋaaruŋku po ase Wen ale weeni Nowa diila.

ref: Seven days later a flood began to cover the earth.
text: Daa ŋayopɔiwa ale taam la nyiamu yaa jam.

ref: On that day Noah and his wife went into the boat with their three sons, Shem, Ham, and Japheth, and their wives.
text: Da dila danni dek Nowa ale wa nidoabilisaŋa Sɛm, ale Yɛm ale Jafet ale wa powa le wa nidoabilisa batawa pooma yaa jo ŋaaruŋku po.

ref: Noah took a male and a female of every living creature with him,
text: Ŋanta buuri meena ale ta nyuvuri la baye baye yaa jam Nowa jigi a jo ŋaaruŋku po.

ref: just as God had told him to do. And when they were all in the boat, the Lord closed the door.
text: Duŋ sii ale jam a jo la ka jaduok ale jasari a va ale Wen ale weeni Nowa diila. Naawen yaa lik wa basi du.

ref: Finally, the mighty flood was so deep that even the highest mountain peaks were about seven meters below the surface of the water.
text: Bu jam jueli kama a lik guutaŋa meena. A zaani guutaŋa zuk a jueli ku limisa ase ka naŋtuok magsika pisiye la.

ref: Not a bird, animal, reptile, or human was left alive anywhere on earth.
text: Javuk meena ale bo tɛŋka zuk yaa kpi. Nuinsa ale gɔi duŋsa ale yeri duŋsa ale ŋan yirinta ale nurubiik meena a jam kpi kama.

ref: A hundred fifty days later, the water started going down.
text: Nyiamu jam lik tɛŋka ka daa kook ale pisinu.

ref: God did not forget about Noah and the animals with him in the boat. So God made a wind blow, and the water started going down.
text: Alege Wen yaa teeri Nowa wari ale gɔi duŋsaŋa ale yeri duŋsaŋa ale bo ŋaaruŋku po ale wa la wari. Wa yaa basi ate viok yaa fuk ate nyiamu yaa siŋ.

ref: God stopped up the places where the water had been gushing out from under the earth. He also closed up the sky, and the rain stopped.
text: Yɔgyɔgla de Wen yaa lik wenzukku ate ŋmorukku basi nika. Tɛŋka mɛ nyiamu basi nyini ka.

ref: Then on the seventeenth day of the seventh month of the year, the boat came to rest somewhere in the Ararat mountains.
text: Chiisa siyopɔiwa po da pi ale ŋayopɔiwa danni ŋaaruŋku yaa siŋ zaani Ararat guutaŋa zuk.

ref: The water kept going down, and the mountain tops could be seen on the first day of the tenth month.
text: Nyiamu yaa kasim a siŋ a ta ga paari chiisa piwa po piilimka danni ate guutaŋa yaa a nya.

ref: Forty days later Noah opened a window to send out a raven, but it kept flying around until the water had dried up.
text: a yaa tom tagaaruk ate ku nyini a gilim de ale de ate nyiamu ga kpeeri tɛŋka zuk.

ref: Noah wanted to find out if the water had gone down, so he sent out a dove.
text: Ate wa ŋman tom nangbaŋ ayen ka nyini nya ase nyiamu a siŋ alege tɛŋka ya.

ref: Deep water was still everywhere, and when the dove could not find a place to land, it flew back to the boat. Then Noah held out his hand and helped it back in.
text: Alege nangbaŋka an nya jigi a kali ya dii nyiŋla nyiamu diem jam lik tɛŋka kama. Ka yaa ŋmani cheŋ Nowa jigi ŋaaruŋku po. Wa yaa laari wa nisaŋa a yik ti ka jo wadek jigi ŋaaruŋku po.

ref: Seven days later Noah sent the dove out again.
text: Wa yaa limisi paari daa ŋayopɔi a ŋman tom nangbaŋka ate ka nyini ŋaaruŋku po.

ref: It returned in the evening, holding in its beak a green leaf from an olive tree. Noah knew the water was finally going down.
text: Nangbaŋka ale pilim ŋman wa jigi junɔinni la, wa yaa nya ka ta tiib viok yɔgsuk ka nɔnni po. Ate Nowa yaa seba ayen nyiamu siŋ kama.

ref: He waited seven more days before sending the dove out again, and this time it did not return.
text: Wa ŋman limisi paari daa ŋayopɔi a tom ka ate ka nyini, alege ka an ŋman ga wa jigi ya.

ref: By the twenty-seventh day of the second month, the earth was completely dry.
text: Benni po chiik kaai ale a nye siye la, daa pisiye ale ŋayopɔiwa danni tɛŋka yaa ko cheksegi.

ref: God said to Noah,
text: Wen yaa weeni Nowa ayen,

ref: “You, your wife, your sons, and your daughters-in-law may now leave the boat.
text: <<Nyini ŋaaruŋku po nyini ale fi powa ale fi nidoabilisaŋa ale fi nidoabilisaŋa pooma.

ref: Let out the birds, animals, and reptiles, so they can mate and live all over the earth.”
text: Ta javuk meena ale bo ŋaaruŋku po ale fu la nyini. Nuinsa, duŋsa, ale ŋan baai meena ale a cheŋ tɛŋzuk ate ba biak abe ba wɔgri a piisi a sueri tɛŋka meena.>>

ref: After Noah and his family had left the boat,
text: Nowa ale wa powa, ale wa nidoabilisaŋa, ale wa nidoabilisaŋa pooma yaa nyini.

ref: the living creatures left in groups of their own kind. The Lord's Promise for the Earth
text: Duŋsaŋa meena ale ŋan baai meena ale a vuuri tɛŋ la, ale nuinsa, ŋan baai meena, ale a cheŋ tɛŋka zuk la, yaa nyini ŋaaruŋku po ba beŋ ba beŋ.

ref: Noah built an altar where he could offer sacrifices to the Lord. Then he offered on the altar one of each kind of animal and bird that could be used for a sacrifice.
text: Ate Nowa yaa sɛ kuŋkuŋ a pai duŋ sii ale magsi Naawen teka ale nuin sii ale magsi Naawen teka la a ko a ju te wa.

ref: The smell of the burning offering pleased the Lord, and he said: Never again will I punish the earth for the sinful things its people do. All of them have evil thoughts from the time they are young, but I will never destroy everything that breathes, as I did this time.
text: Naawen yaa wom nyummu le masa la a yaa weeni wa suinum po ayen, <<Wa kan ŋman ka te tɛŋka diipo diipo nurubiik nyiŋŋa, dila wa suinummu po poom ka biem nyiini, nyini wa biik po. M kan ŋman kaasi ŋanvuta meena ase m ni nye dii nna.

ref: As long as the earth remains, there will be planting and harvest, cold and heat; winter and summer, day and night.
text: Ase tɛŋka ale kasim boro nna, borik daa ale cheka daa ŋoota ale wulum, yoei ale wenkarik yok ale kantueŋ a kan maa kpegli.>>

ref: God said to Noah and his sons: I am giving you my blessing. Have a lot of children and grandchildren, so people will live everywhere on this earth.
text: Dila Wen yaa niak Nowa ale wa nidoabilisaŋa, ale weeni ayen, <<Ni biak a piisi a sueri tɛŋka.

ref: All animals, birds, reptiles, and fish will be afraid of you. I have placed them under your control,
text: Fi yɔgsum popola ale jam ka duŋsa ale wenzuk nuinsa zuk, ale ŋan baai meena ale vuuri a cheŋ la zuk, ale jumaŋa ale bo nyiam po la. M pai ba nyo fi nisa po.

ref: and I have given them to you for food. From now on, you may eat them, as well as the green plants that you have always eaten.
text: Jaab meena ale boro a cheŋ la, m pai te fu ayen ba chim ŋandiinta. Ase m le pai jaab meena ale ta vaata a te fu la, m yaa pai jaab meena a te fu.

ref: But life is in the blood, and you must not eat any meat that still has blood in it.
text: Alege m kan yaali ayen waai ŋɔbi jaab ate ziim wari wa po.

ref: I want you and your descendants to have many children, so people will live everywhere on earth.
text: Alege nama, ni be biak a piisi tɛŋka zuk.>>

ref: Again, God said to Noah and his sons:
text: Wen yaa weeni Nowa ale wa nidoabilisaŋa le bo ale wa la ayen,

ref: I am going to make a solemn promise to you and to everyone who will live after you.
text: <<Yɔgyɔgla de m nyeka nɔiyeŋ ale fi ale fi ŋaaŋsa

ref: I promise every living creature that the earth and those living on it will never again be destroyed by a flood.
text: M nye nɔiyeŋ le fu. M kan ŋman kaasi nyuvuri ale nyiamoa. Nyiam kan ŋman kaasi tɛŋka diipo diipo.>>

ref: The rainbow that I have put in the sky will be my sign to you and to every living creature on earth. It will remind you that I will keep this promise forever.
text: M pai ka nagoritom a basi chiŋmaaŋa po ate bu a sak nɔiyeŋ kaai ale bo mi ale tɛŋka suŋsuŋ la.

ref: When I send clouds over the earth, and a rainbow appears in the sky,
text: M dan basi ate chiŋmaaŋa lik ate nagoritommu a nyini chiŋmaaŋa po nyini,

ref: I will remember my promise to you and to all other living creatures. Never again will I let floodwaters destroy all life.
text: M le teeri nɔiyeŋka ale bo mi ale fi ale ŋanvuta meena tɔgatɔga la suŋsuŋ. Nyiam kan ŋman lik tɛŋka a ko ŋanvuta meena.

ref: When I see the rainbow in the sky, I will always remember the promise that I have made to every living creature.
text: Diipo meena nagoritommu dan nyini chiŋmaaŋa po nyini, m le nya bu a teeri nɔiyeŋ kaai ale kan ta nuerika a bo Wen ale ŋanvuta tɔgatɔga tɛŋka zuk la.>>

ref: The rainbow will be the sign of that solemn promise. Noah and His Family
text: Dila Wen yaa weeni Nowa ayen, <<Jabuui ale a sak nɔiyeŋka ate mi nye mi ale fi ale ŋanvuta meena tɛŋka zuk la ale nna.>>

ref: Noah and his sons, Shem, Ham, and Japheth, left the boat. Ham later had a son named Canaan.
text: Nowa nidoabilisaŋa ale jam nyini ŋaaruŋku po a nyini la ale, Sɛm ale Yɛm ale Jafet. Yɛm jam ka Keenan ko.

ref: All people on earth are descendants of Noah's three sons.
text: Nowa nidoabilisa batawa jam le la. Bala abe le biak ate nuruma waaŋ tɛŋka meena.

ref: Noah farmed the land and was the first to plant a vineyard.
text: Nowa waai le ka kpaarɔ la yaa vuk tiisa kaduk po.

ref: One day he got drunk and was lying naked in his tent.
text: Wa le gbesi yɔnaŋa a nye daam nyu la wa yaa buk doa chaboala wa viuku po.

ref: Ham entered the tent and saw him naked, then went back outside and told his brothers.
text: Keenan kowa Yɛm yaa nya wa kowa chaboalaŋa a yaa weeni wa suataŋa bayewa ale bo peelim la.

ref: Shem and Japheth put a robe over their shoulders and walked backwards into the tent. Without looking at their father, they placed it over his body.
text: Alege Sɛm ale Jafet yaa pai garuk a vi ba ŋmankpala zuk a cheŋ tuntula a ga lik ba kowa chaboalaŋa. Ba jam tulim ba ninaŋa a basi ka nakpiak ayen ba kan nya ba kowa chaboalaŋa.

ref: When Noah sobered up and learned what his youngest son had done,
text: Nowa daamu ale yiinti ate wa yiti a wom wa bibaaŋka ale nye wa diila,

ref: he said, “I now put a curse on Canaan! He will be the lowest slave of his brothers.
text: Wa yaa weeni ayen, <<Kikaasa va Keenan! Wa le chim ka yɔma te wa suataŋa.>>

ref: I ask the Lord my God to bless Shem and make Canaan his slave.
text: Wa ŋman weeni ayen, <<Niauŋ a bo Naawen jigi, Sɛm Wenni. Keenan ale chim ka Sɛm yɔma.

ref: I pray God will give Japheth more and more land and let him take over the territory of Shem. May Canaan be his slave.”
text: Wen abe lagri Jafet tɛŋ banni, Jafet za wa bo ka Sɛm viitaŋa po, abe Keenan chim wa yɔma.>>

ref: and died at the age of
text: Nowa benaŋa meena jam ka kobsaneok ale pisinu alege ate wa kpi.

ref: After the flood Shem, Ham, and Japheth had many descendants. The Descendants of Japheth
text: Sɛm ale Yɛm ale Jafet buuni wie ale nna. Ba jam ka Nowa nidoabilisa. Ba mɛ jam biak ka nidoabilisa nyiamu ale jam tagli tɛŋka la ŋaaŋnyiŋ.

ref: Japheth's descendants had their own languages, tribes, and land. They were Gomer, Magog, Madai, Javan, Tubal, Meshech, and Tiras. Gomer was the ancestor of Ashkenaz, Riphath, and Togarmah. Javan was the ancestor of Elishah, Tarshish, Kittim, and Dodanim, who settled along the coast. The Descendants of Ham
text: Baai ale jam kali gilim nyakpiemu la a jam nyini ka Jafet yezukku buuni. Ba yaa jam piisi a chim tɛŋ kpieŋsa a ta biisa tɔgatɔga.

ref: This completes the list of Noah's descendants. After the flood their descendants became nations and spread all over the world.
text: Nowa ŋaaŋbisaŋa ale nna. Nyiamu ale jam lik tɛŋka la ŋaaŋnyiŋ ba yaa jam chim ka tɛŋ kpieŋsa a waaŋ tɛŋka meena zuk.

ref: but after some of them moved from the east and settled in Babylonia,
text: Ase nuruma ale jam torisi a cheŋ kori geŋ la, ba yaa nya jigi nna jassi Sinar a yaa kali dula.

ref: they said: Let's build a city with a tower that reaches to the sky! We'll use hard bricks and tar instead of stone and mortar. We'll become famous, and we won't be scattered all over the world.
text: Ba yaa weeni chaab ayen, <<Ni jam, ate ti sɛ tɛŋ a te tidek ate ka ta kiari a jueli wenŋmazuk. Ate ti nye yuei a te tidek ate ti kan waaŋ tɛŋka meena zuwa.>>

ref: But when the Lord came down to look at the city and the tower,
text: Alege Naawen a yaa siŋ nya tɛŋ nummu ale kiani ate saalɔ bisaŋa a sɛ la.

ref: he said: These people are working together because they all speak the same language. This is just the beginning. Soon they will be able to do anything they want.
text: Naawen a yaa weeni ayen, <<Ba dan chim nuru yeŋsa a biisi bi yeŋ a yaa piilim a nye nna, wari ka ate ba dan zɔk ayen ba nye di tua te ba.

ref: Let's go down and confuse them! We'll make them speak different languages, and they won't be able to understand each other.
text: Ni jam, ate ti siŋ tɛŋ a butim ba biika ate ba kaa miŋ chaaba.

ref: So the people had to stop building the city, because the Lord confused their language and scattered them all over the earth. That's how the city of Babel got its name. The Descendants of Shem
text: Dila ale soa ate ba jam a wi ka ayen Babel la. Ku kiri ale Naawen a butim tɛŋka meena biika. A nyini dula jigini Naawen a basi ate ba waaŋ tɛŋka meena zuk.

ref: Serug had more children and died at the age of
text: Wa le biak Nayo la wa goa ka bena kobsiye. Wa jam biak nidoabilisa ba chaab kama ale lieba.

ref: Terah decided to move from Ur to the land of Canaan. He took along Abram and Sarai and his grandson Lot, the son of Haran. But when they came to the city of Haran, they settled there instead.
text: Tɛra yaa pai wa nidoabinni Abram ale wa ŋaaŋka Lɔti, Yaran nidoabinni ale wa sampowa Sarai, Abram powa. Ba meena yaa basi Ur ale bo Kaldiya po la ayen ba cheŋ Keenan. Alege ba le jam paari Yaran la, ba yaa kali dula.

ref: The Lord said to Abram: Leave your country, your family, and your relatives and go to the land that I will show you.
text: Naawen a jam weeni Abram ayen, <<Basi fi tɛŋka, ale fi nuruma, ale fi kowa dɔkdemma abe fi cheŋ tɛŋ kaai ate mi ale dak a sak fu la.

ref: I will bless you and make your descendants into a great nation. You will become famous and be a blessing to others.
text: <<M le basi ate fi chim tɛŋ kpieŋ abe m niak fu, m le basi ate fi yuenni a yiti ate fi chim niauŋ.

ref: I will bless those who bless you, but I will put a curse on anyone who puts a curse on you. Everyone on earth will be blessed because of you.
text: M le niak baai ale a niak fu la, abe waai ale ka a te fu la m le ka a te wa. Tɛŋzuk nuruma meena ale nya niauŋ fi nyiŋ.

ref: Abram went as far as the sacred tree of Moreh in a place called Shechem. The Canaanites were still living in the land at that time,
text: Abram jam cheŋ kama a ga paari More ti kpiemu ale bo Sikiim la. Dila po Keenan demma a jam bo tɛŋka po kama.

ref: but the Lord appeared to Abram and promised, “I will give this land to your family forever.” Abram then built an altar there for the Lord.
text: Naawen yaa pai wadek a sak Abram, a yaa weeni wa ayen, <<M le pai tɛŋka de a te fi ŋaaŋbisaŋa.>> Wa yaa sɛ kuŋkuŋ dula a te Naawen dii ale pai wadek a sak wa la.

ref: Abram traveled to the hill country east of Bethel and camped between Bethel and Ai, where he built another altar and worshiped the Lord.
text: Wa yaa nyini dula a va guutaŋa jigini Betel kori geŋsa a ga ti wa viuku, ate Betel a bo yeniŋ geŋsa alege Ai a bo kori geŋsa. Dula jigini wa yaa sɛ kuŋkuŋ a te Naawen, a yaa wi Naawen yuenni.

ref: Later, Abram started out toward the Southern Desert. Abram in Egypt
text: Abram ŋman basi dula ale cheŋ va Negev geŋsa.

ref: The crops failed, and there was no food anywhere in Canaan. So Abram and his wife Sarai went to live in Egypt for a while. But just before they got there, Abram said, “Sarai, you are really beautiful!
text: Ba le jam moata Ejipiti tɛŋka la, wa yaa weeni wa powa Sarai ayen, <<Mi seba fi ale ka nipok naliŋ diila kama.

ref: When the Egyptians see you, they will murder me because I am your husband. But they won't kill you.
text: Ejipiti demma a dan nya fu, ba le weeni ayen, <Nya wa pok ale nna.> Ba le ko mu alege basi ate fi boro.

ref: As soon as Abram and Sarai arrived in Egypt, the Egyptians noticed how beautiful she was.
text: Abram ale ga paari Ejipiti la, Ejipiti demma a yaa nya ayen nipowa a ŋmasi nala ka yega.

ref: The king's officials told him about her, and she was taken to his house.
text: Faaro nisɔmma a yaa nya nipowa a paali wa wie a sak Faaro, ate ba ta wa jo naawa yenni po.

ref: The king was good to Abram because of Sarai, and Abram was given sheep, cattle, donkeys, slaves, and camels.
text: Wa jam nye Abram a magsi kama nipowa nyiŋ ate Abram paari piisa ale niiga ale bon duata ale bon niima, yɔm nidoaba ale nipooba ale laalaakomiba.

ref: Because of Sarai, the Lord struck the king and everyone in his palace with terrible diseases.
text: Alege Naawen yaa basi ate nyiŋtui kpiema a yik Faaro ale wa yenni demma Abram powa Sarai nyiŋ.

ref: Finally, the king sent for Abram and said to him, “What have you done to me? Why didn't you tell me Sarai was your wife?
text: Faaro yaa wi Abram ayen, <<Ka boan le la ate fi nye mu?>> Wa ŋman weeni ayen, <<Ka boa ale soa ate fi an weeni mi ayen wa ka fi powa?

ref: Why did you make me believe she was your sister? Now I've married her. Take her and go! She's your wife.”
text: Ka boa ale soa ate fi weeni ayen, wa ka m toa, ate mi yaa pai wa chim m powa? Yɔgyɔgla de fi powa ale nna. Ta wa cheŋ.>>

ref: So the king told his men to let Abram and Sarai take their possessions and leave.
text: Faaro yaa weeni wa nuruma ate ba te ti Abram a cheŋ ale wa powa ale wa ŋantaŋa meena ate wa ta la.

ref: Abram and Sarai took everything they owned and went to the Southern Desert. Lot went with them.
text: Abram yaa basi Ejipiti alege cheŋ Negev ale wa powa ale jaabuuri meena ate wa jam ta la. Lɔti mɛ jam va wa kama a cheŋ.

ref: Abram was very rich. He owned many cattle, sheep, and goats, and had a lot of silver and gold.
text: Abram jam jigsi kama ale duŋsa ale kpana ale ligmɔniŋ.

ref: Abram moved from place to place in the Southern Desert. And finally, he went north and set up his tents between Bethel and Ai,
text: Wa jam zaani Negev kama a cheŋ de ale de a yaa ga paari Betel kauk kuui ale bo Betel ale Ai suŋsuŋ la. Dula ale wa jam liŋ ti wa viuku la.

ref: where he had earlier camped and built an altar. There he worshiped the Lord.
text: Dula mɛ ale wa liŋ a sɛ kuŋkuŋku la. Dula jigini Abram yaa wi Naawen yuenni.

ref: Lot, who was traveling with him, also had sheep, goats, and cattle, as well as his own family and slaves.
text: Lɔti ale jam va Abram nyiem a cheŋ la a jam ta piisa ale niiga ale viita kama.

ref: At this time the Canaanites and the Perizzites were still living in the same area, and so there wasn't enough pastureland left for Abram and Lot with all of their animals. Besides this, the men who took care of Abram's animals and the ones who took care of Lot's animals started quarreling.
text: Kpaliŋ ale jam bo baai ale a nya Abram duŋsaŋa zuk la ale baai ale a nya Lɔti duŋsaŋa zuk la suŋsuŋ. Keenan demma ale Perizzi demma mɛ jam maa bo ka tɛŋ kala dila po.

ref: Abram said to Lot, “We are close relatives. We shouldn't argue, and our men shouldn't be fighting one another.
text: Abram yaa jam weeni Lɔti ayen, <<Kan te ti kpaliŋ a bo mi ale fi suŋsuŋŋa yaase baai ale a nya mi duŋsaŋa zuk la ale fi duŋsaŋa zuk la suŋsuŋŋa, diila ti ka suata.

ref: There is plenty of land for you to choose from. Let's separate. If you go north, I'll go south; if you go south, I'll go north.”
text: Tɛŋka an doa daa mi ale fi meena niŋŋa? Basi ate ti gaari chaab. Fi dan cheŋ gala, mi ale cheŋ juga, fi be dan cheŋ juga, mi ale cheŋ gala.>>

ref: This happened before the Lord had destroyed the cities of Sodom and Gomorrah. And when Lot looked around, he saw there was plenty of water in the Jordan Valley. All the way to Zoar the valley was as green as the garden of the Lord or the land of Egypt.
text: Lɔti yaa zak zuk nya Jodan ale dua dii jassi ale yɔk a nye ase Naawen tiisa jigi la. Ku jam a nye ase ka Ejipiti tɛŋka la a tuak Zoar. Ku le jam ka dii alege ate Naawen a kaasi Sodom ale Gomora tɛŋsaŋa la.

ref: So Lot chose the whole Jordan Valley for himself, and as he started out toward the east, he and Abram separated.
text: Lɔti yaa lueri Jodan meena ale dua jassi la a te wadek a va kori geŋ. Nuruba bayewa yaa jam poori chaab.

ref: Abram stayed in the land of Canaan. But Lot settled near the cities of the valley and put up his tents not far from Sodom,
text: Abram jam bo ka Keenan tɛŋka alege Lɔti jam bo tɛŋ sii ale jam dua nna jassi la a ti wa viitaŋa a moata Sodom.

ref: where the people were evil and sinned terribly against the Lord. Abram Moves to Hebron
text: Yɔgyɔgla de Sodom nuruma nyeka jam tua kama ale a nye biem nna yegayega a kaasi Naawen.

ref: After Abram and Lot had gone their separate ways, the Lord said to Abram: Look around to the north, south, east, and west.
text: Abram ale Lɔti ale poori chaab la ŋaaŋnyiŋ, Naawen yaa weeni wa ayen, <<Kauk kuui ate fi za la zak zuk a nya wiiluk geŋ ale yeseok geŋ ale kori geŋ ale yeniŋ geŋ.

ref: I will give you and your family all the land you can see. It will be theirs forever!
text: Tɛŋka meena ate fi a nya la m le pai te fi ale fi ŋaaŋbisaŋa taam wuu.

ref: I will give you more descendants than there are specks of dust on the earth, and someday it will be easier to count those specks of dust than to count your descendants.
text: M le basi ate fi ŋaaŋbisaŋa a piisi ase tintain busuŋ la, ate waai a dan baga a chiini tintain busuŋku, wa le baga a chiini fi ŋaaŋbisaŋa.

ref: Now walk back and forth across the land, because I am going to give it to you.
text: Cheŋ, cheŋ tɛŋka woglim ale ka labsimu meena, diila m pai a te fu kama.>>

ref: Abram took down his tents and went to live near the sacred trees of Mamre at Hebron, where he built an altar in honor of the Lord.
text: Dila nyiŋ Abram yaa torisi wa viitaŋa a cheŋ moata Mamre ale bo Yebron la tiib kpieŋsaŋa jigini. Dula jigini, wa yaa sɛ kuŋkuŋ a te Naawen.

ref: About this time, King Amraphel of Babylonia, King Arioch of Ellasar, King Chedorlaomer of Elam, and King Tidal of Goiim
text: Dila po Sinar naawa Amrapel ale Ellasar naawa Ariok ale Iilam naawa Chedorlaomer ale Goyim naawa Tidal,

ref: attacked King Bera of Sodom, King Birsha of Gomorrah, King Shinab of Admah, King Shemeber of Zeboiim, and the king of Bela, a city also known as Zoar.
text: yaa cheŋ ba nye kpaliŋ ale Sodom naawa Bera ale Gomora naawa Birsa ale Adma naawa Sinab ale Zeboiim naawa Sɛmeber ale Bela naawa. Zoar jam le la.

ref: King Chedorlaomer and his allies had ruled these last five kings for twelve years, but in the thirteenth year the kings rebelled and joined forces in Siddim Valley, which is now covered by the southern part of the Dead Sea.
text: Bena pi ale ŋaye nalimaŋa de jam siak te Chedorlaomer kama, alege bena pi ale ŋatawa po ba zɛri wa nɔi.

ref: A year later King Chedorlaomer and his allies attacked and defeated the Rephaites in Ashteroth-Karnaim, the Zuzites in Ham, and the Emites in Shaveh-Kiriathaim.
text: Bena pi ale ŋanaansiwa po, Chedorlaomer ale nalimaŋa yaa nye nɔiyeŋ a cheŋ ga nak dɛ Arefa demma ale bo Asterot Karnaim la ale Zuzi demma ale bo Yɛm la ale Emi demma ale bo Saveya Kiriataim la

ref: They also defeated the Horites in the hill country of Edom, as far as El-Paran, near the desert.
text: ale Yori demma ale bo Seir ate ka ta guuta la, a ta ga paari El Paran ale moata gɔnni la.

ref: They went back to the city of Enmishpat, better known as Kadesh. Then they captured all the land that belonged to the Amalekites, and they defeated the Amorites who were living in Hazazon-Tamar.
text: Ba yaa ŋman viriŋ ŋaaŋ a cheŋ En Mispat yaase Kades, a nak dɛ Amalek demma tɛŋsaŋa meena ale Amori demma ale jam bo Yazazon Tamara la.

ref: At Siddim Valley, the armies of the kings of Sodom, Gomorrah, Admah, Zeboiim, and Bela fought the armies of King Chedorlaomer of Elam, King Tidal of Goiim, King Amraphel of Babylonia, and King Arioch of Ellasar. The valley
text: Ayen ba siti Iilam naawa Chedorlaomer ale Goyim naawa Tidal ale Sinar naawa Amrapel ale Ellasar naawa Ariok. Nalima ŋanaansi ale ta chaab ale nalima ŋanu.

ref: was full of tar pits, and when the troops from Sodom and Gomorrah started running away, some of them fell into the pits. Others escaped to the hill country.
text: Siddim Viaka jam ta ka gola ate ŋa sueri ale kotaali. Sodom ale Gomora nalimaŋa ale jam a chali la, nuruma gela yaa lo ŋa po alege nuru mbala chali jueli guutaŋa zuima.

ref: Their enemies took everything of value from Sodom and Gomorrah, including their food supplies.
text: Nalima ŋanaansiwa yaa vari Sodom ale Gomora kɔlimaŋa meena ale ba ŋandiintaŋa meena a ta cheŋ.

ref: They also captured Abram's nephew Lot, who lived in Sodom. They took him and his possessions and then left.
text: Ba jam ta Abram suoku nidoabinni Lɔti ale wa ŋantaŋa a cheŋ kama. Wa le tɔlim jam bo Sodom la.

ref: At this time Abram the Hebrew was living near the oaks that belonged to Mamre the Amorite. Mamre and his brothers Eshcol and Aner were Abram's friends. Someone who had escaped from the battle told Abram
text: Ba wanyi ale jam chali bɛ yaa jam weeni dii le nye la a sak Yiburu denɔwa Abram. Abram le jam bo juijui la a moata ka Mamre, Amori denɔwa tiib kpieŋsaŋa. Mamre jam ka Eskol ale Aner suok. Bala meena jam nye ka nɔiyeŋ ale Abram.

ref: that his nephew Lot had been taken away. Three hundred and eighteen of Abram's servants were fighting men, so he took them and followed the enemy as far north as the city of Dan.
text: Abram le wom ayen wa suoku nidoabinni ba yik ta cheŋ la, wa yaa wi nuruma kobsita ale pi ale naanika ate ba biak ba wa yenni po a sak ba la a nyini ta ba a ga paari Dan.

ref: That night, Abram divided up his troops, attacked from all sides, and won a great victory. But some of the enemy escaped to the town of Hobah north of Damascus,
text: Yɔkku Abram yaa poori wa nuruma a siti ba a vi a nak ba dɛ. Wa vi ba ga paari ka Yoba, Damasikus wiiluk geŋsa.

ref: and Abram went after them. He brought back his nephew Lot, together with Lot's possessions and the women and everyone else who had been captured. Abram Is Blessed by Melchizedek
text: Wa tuesi wa suoku nidoabinni Lɔti ale wa ŋantaŋa meena ale nipooma ale nuru yontaŋa.

ref: Abram returned after he had defeated King Chedorlaomer and the other kings. Then the king of Sodom went to meet Abram in Shaveh Valley, which is also known as King's Valley.
text: Abram ale nak Chedorlaomer ale nalimaŋa ale nye nɔiyeŋka ale wa a dɛ la, Sodom naawa a yaa nyini ayen wa tu wa Saveya Viaka po. Viaka jam ka Naawa Viak le la.

ref: King Melchizedek of Salem was a priest of God Most High. He brought out some bread and wine
text: Salem naawa Melekizedek yaa ta boroborok ale daam a nyini. Wa jam ka Wen Dii Ale Bo Wenŋmazuk La kaabteerɔ.

ref: and said to Abram: “I bless you in the name of God Most High, Creator of heaven and earth.
text: Ate wa yaa niak Abram ayen, <<Wen Dii Ale Bo Wenŋmazuk La a niak Abram, wa ka waai ale nye wenzuk ale tɛŋ.

ref: All praise belongs to God Most High for helping you defeat your enemies.” Then Abram gave Melchizedek a tenth of everything.
text: Abe niauŋ a bo Wen Dii Ale Bo Wenŋmazuk La jigi, wa ka waai ale pai fi dachaasaŋa a nyo fi nisima po.>> Abram yaa pai wa ŋantaŋa piika po bunyi a te wa.

ref: The king of Sodom said to Abram, “All I want are my people. You can keep everything else.”
text: Sodom naawa yaa weeni Abram ayen, <<Te mu nuruma abe fi tanla kɔlimaŋa fidek.>>

ref: Abram answered: The Lord God Most High made the heavens and the earth. And I have promised him
text: Alege Abram a weeni Sodom naawa ayen, mi zak ka nisa a sak Naawen, Wen Dii Ale Bo Wenŋmazuk La, a nye wenzuk ale tɛŋ la alege a pɛ,

ref: that I won't keep anything of yours, not even a sandal strap or a piece of thread. Then you can never say that you are the one who made me rich.
text: ayen mi kan tuesi jabuui ale ka fi jaab la, daa garuk miik yaase nuensiri gbain mɛ, ate fi kan baga a weeni ayen fi ale basi ate Abram jigsi.

ref: Let my share be the food that my men have eaten. But Aner, Eshcol, and Mamre went with me, so give them their share of what we brought back.
text: M kan siak a tuesi jaab fi jigiya alege ka mi nuruma ale dɛ ŋan baai la ale nuru baai ale va mu cheŋ la charuku. Bala ale Aner ale Eskol ale Mamre. Bala tuesi ba charuku.>>

ref: Later the Lord spoke to Abram in a vision, “Abram, don't be afraid! I will protect you and reward you greatly.”
text: Kude ŋaaŋnyiŋ Naawen wanni yaa jam Abram jigi ase gɔdaasima la ayen, <<Abram, kan chali yɔgsummoa. Mi ka waai ale a lik fu, a chim fi tutun kpiema. >>

ref: But Abram answered, “Lord All-Powerful, you have given me everything I could ask for, except children. And when I die, Eliezer of Damascus will get all I own.
text: Alege Abram yaa weeni ayen, <<M Nyɔnɔwa waai ale ka Naawen la, fi le te mu ka boa mi ale kan ta biik nna? Waai ale wa pai m nitalimu la ale Ɛlieza ale nyini Damasikus la.>>

ref: You have not given me any children, and this servant of mine will inherit everything.”
text: Abram weeni ayen, <<Fi an te mu bisa. Dila nyiŋ m yenni po yɔmma wanyi ale wa pai m nitalimu.>>

ref: The Lord replied, “No, he won't! You will have a son of your own, and everything you have will be his.”
text: Ate Naawen wanni yaa jam Abram jigi ayen, <<Nuruwa de a kan pai fi nitalimu alege ka nidoabi dii ale wa chim fidek ziim la ale wa pai fi nitalimu.>>

ref: Then the Lord took Abram outside and said, “Look at the sky and see if you can count the stars. That's how many descendants you will have.”
text: Naawen yaa ta wa nyini peelim a weeni ayen, <<Zak zuk a nya wenŋmazuk a chiini chiŋmarisaŋa, fi dan baga a chiini si.>> Wa yaa weeni wa ayen, <<Fi ŋaaŋbisaŋa le piisi ka dila.>>

ref: Abram believed the Lord, so the Lord was pleased with him and accepted him. The Lord Makes Another Promise to Abram
text: Abram yaa ta siaka ale Naawen, ate wa a wi wa ayen wayɔrisima nyɔnɔ.

ref: The Lord said to Abram, “I brought you here from Ur in Chaldea, and I gave you this land.”
text: Wa ŋman weeni wa mɛ ayen, <<Mi ka Naawen, mi ale ta fu nyini Ur, Kaldiya demma tɛŋka a nyini ayen m te fu tɛŋka de ate fi soa.>>

ref: Then the Lord told him, “Bring me a three-year-old cow, a three-year-old female goat, a three-year-old ram, a dove, and a young pigeon.”
text: Naawen yaa weeni Abram ayen, <<Ta bena ŋata naasari ale bena ŋata buuk ale bena ŋata padiak a jam. Maa ta nangbaŋ takuriŋ ale nangbaŋ kawuruk a jam.>>

ref: Abram obeyed the Lord. Then he cut the animals in half and laid the two halves of each animal opposite each other on the ground. But he did not cut the doves and pigeons in half.
text: Abram yaa ta ŋantaŋa de meena a jam wa jigi, a gebi ŋa gela buye a yaa pai dueni a tuk chaab. Alege nangbaŋsaŋa wa an gebi ya.

ref: And when birds came down to eat the animals, Abram chased them away.
text: Nuin sii ale a dɛ lanta la a yaa jam lantaŋa zuk, alege Abram a yiak ti si yiti cheŋ.

ref: As the sun was setting, Abram fell into a deep sleep, and everything became dark and frightening.
text: Wenni ale a siŋ la goom yaa chiak Abram nna vilim, ate legi nna kpitɔ yaa jam Abram zuk.

ref: Four generations later, your descendants will return here and take this land, because only then will the people who live here be so sinful that they deserve to be punished.
text: Fi ŋaaŋbiik sii ale a saari bunaansiwa la ale ŋman jam de la, diila Amori demma biemu an diem zuak ya.>>

ref: Sometime after sunset, when it was very dark, a smoking cooking pot and a flaming fire passed between the two halves of each animal.
text: Wenbinni ale siŋ la ate tɛŋka a sob la, samɔniŋ ale nyuisa ale bolim ŋiak a yaa jam va lam gelaŋa suŋsuŋ a taam.

ref: At that time the Lord made an agreement with Abram and told him: I will give your descendants the land east of the Shihor River on the border of Egypt as far as the Euphrates River.
text: Dai dila danni Naawen yaa nye nɔiyeŋ ale Abram ayen, <<M le pai tɛŋka de a te fi ŋaaŋbisaŋa a zaani Ejipiti benni a ga paari beli kpienni Yufiretis.

ref: They will possess the land of the Kenites, the Kenizzites, the Kadmonites,
text: M le pai Ken demma ale Kenizzi demma ale Kadmon demma

ref: the Hittites, the Perizzites, the Rephaites,
text: ale Yit demma ale Perizzi demma ale Arefa demma,

ref: the Amorites, the Canaanites, the Girgashites, and the Jebusites.
text: ale Amori demma ale Keenan demma ale Girgas demma ale Jebus demma tɛŋsaŋa a te ba.>>

ref: Abram's wife Sarai had not been able to have any children. But she owned a young Egyptian slave woman named Hagar,
text: Yɔgyɔgla de Abram powa Sarai an jam biak bisa a te wa. Alege wa jam ta ka dɔklie ate wa nyini Ejipiti. Wa yuei jam le Aga.

ref: and Sarai said to Abram, “The Lord has not given me any children. Sleep with my slave, and if she has a child, it will be mine.” Abram agreed,
text: Wa yaa weeni wa chorɔwa Abram ayen, <<Naawen a kasi mu kama ale bisa biam. Be goa ale m dɔkliewa ate m nya dɔkdem a nyini wa jigi.>> Abram yaa siak wa powa Sarai ale weeni diila.

ref: and Sarai gave him Hagar to be his wife. This happened after Abram had lived in the land of Canaan for ten years.
text: Abram le bo Keenan tɛŋka bena pi la ŋaaŋnyiŋ, wa powa Sarai yaa pai wa dɔkliewa Ejipiti denɔwa Aga a te wa chorɔwa ayen wa chim wa pok.

ref: Later, when Hagar knew she was going to have a baby, she became proud and treated Sarai hatefully.
text: Wa yaa goa le Aga ate wa yik lɔgi. Wa le miŋ ayen wa yik lɔgi la, wa kan ŋman nye wa mawa Sarai a magsi.

ref: Then Sarai said to Abram, “It's all your fault! I gave you my slave woman, but she has been hateful to me ever since she found out she was pregnant. You have done me wrong, and you will have to answer to the Lord for this.”
text: Sarai yaa weeni Abram ayen, <<Fi ale soa kaasuŋ kuui ate mi a namu nna. M pai m dɔkliewa a nyo fi nisima po, ate yɔgyɔgla de wa yaa le nya le wa ta lɔgi la wa kan nye mi a magsi. Naawen a bo mi ale fi buusa.

ref: Abram said, “All right! She's your slave—do whatever you want with her.” Then Sarai began treating Hagar so harshly that she finally ran away.
text: Abram yaa weeni ayen, <<Fi dɔkliewa a bo fi nisima po. Dii ate fi a poli ayen ku nala magsi fi nye wa la, be nye.>> Sarai yaa jam kan nye Aga a magsi, ate wa chali ale ge wa.

ref: Hagar stopped to rest at a spring in the desert on the road to Shur. While she was there, the angel of the Lord came to her
text: Naawen maarɔ yaa nya Aga ale wa kali moata bulik gɔnni po. Ka ka bulik kaai ale jam bo suik kuui ale a cheŋ Sur la nakpiak.

ref: and asked, “Hagar, where have you come from, and where are you going?” She answered, “I'm running away from Sarai, my owner.”
text: Ate maarɔwa yaa bek wa ayen, <<Aga, Sarai dɔkliewa fi nyini ka bɛ a jam, fi be a cheŋ ka bɛ?>> Wa yaa tulisi ayen, <<M chali ale a ge ka m mawa Sarai.>>

ref: The angel said, “Go back to Sarai and be her slave.
text: Naawen maarɔwa yaa weeni ayen, <<Ŋman cheŋ fi mawa jigi a siak te wa.>>

ref: I will give you a son, who will be called Ishmael, because I have heard your cry for help. And someday I will give you so many descendants that no one will be able to count them all.
text: Naawen maarɔwa ŋman weeni mɛ ayen, <<Yɔgyɔgla de fi ta ka lɔgi fi be le biak nidoabili a te wa yuei ayen Ismael, diila Naawen a wom fi le a tɔk fi niniŋŋa la kama.

ref: But your son will live far from his relatives; he will be like a wild donkey, fighting everyone, and everyone fighting him.”
text: Fi biika a za wa a nye ase ka boniŋ kaai ale waara, wa le zak nisa ta chaab le waai meena, abe waai meena zak nisa ta chaab le wa, ate wa ta dachaani ale wa suataŋa meena.>>

ref: Hagar thought, “Have I really seen God and lived to tell about it?” So from then on she called him, “The God Who Sees Me.”
text: Ate wa yaa pai yuenni de a te Naawen dii ale biisi le wa la ayen, <<Fi ka Wen dii ale a nya mu.>> Wa weeni kama ayen, <<Yɔgyɔgla de mi nya Waai ale a nya mi la.>>

ref: That's why people call the well between Kadesh and Bered, “The Well of the Living One Who Sees Me.”
text: Dila ale soa ate ba a wi vinni ayen, Beer Layai Aroi. Di diem bo ka Kades ale Bered suŋsuŋ po.

ref: I will keep my solemn promise to you and give you more descendants than can be counted.”
text: M le basi ate fi ŋaaŋbisaŋa a piisi ate nɔiyeŋ kaai ate mi ale fi nye la a chim wensie.>>

ref: Abram bowed with his face to the ground, and God said:
text: Abram yaa koori a pai diri a kpi tɛŋ, ate Wen weeni wa ayen,

ref: I promise that you will be the father of many nations. So now I'm changing your name from Abram to Abraham.
text: Ba kan ŋmaa wi fu ayen Abram. Fi yuenni yaa le chim ka Abrayam. Diila m basi kama ate fi chim tɛŋsa yega ko.

ref: I will give you a lot of descendants, and they will become great nations. Some of them will even be kings.
text: M le basi ate biam jam fi jigi. Tɛŋsa ale nyini fi jigi ate nalima mɛ a nyini fi jigi.

ref: I will always keep the promise I have made to you and your descendants, because I am your God and their God.
text: M le basi ate m nɔiyeŋka a chim nɔiyeŋ kaai ale kaa kpeglim mi ale fi ale fi ŋaaŋbisaŋa suŋsuŋ la. Ka le kasim boro kama ale fi ŋaaŋ sii ale cheen la. M le chim fi Wen ale fi ŋaaŋsaŋa Wen.

ref: I will give you and them the land in which you are now a foreigner. I will give the whole land of Canaan to your family forever, and I will be their God.
text: Keenan tɛŋka meena ate fi boro yɔgyɔgla de a chim nichaanɔ nna, m le basi ate ka chim fi ale fi ŋaaŋbisaŋa tɛŋ kasim a cheŋ. M le chim ba Wen.>>

ref: Abraham, you and all future members of your family must promise to obey me.
text: Wen yaa weeni Abrayam ayen, <<Ku a fɛ kama ayen fi ale fi ŋaaŋsaŋa a ta m nɔiyeŋka a magsi baai meena ale bo ŋaaŋ a cheen la.

ref: As the sign that you are keeping this promise, you must circumcise every man and boy in your family.
text: Ni gebi ni yoaŋa ate ku a sak nɔiyeŋka ale bo mi ale fi suŋsuŋ la.

ref: Any man who isn't circumcised hasn't kept his part of the promise and cannot be one of my people.

text: wa yaa weeni Abrayam ayen, <<Basi ate nipok yɔmawa ale wa nidoabinni a cheŋ, nipok yɔma wala nidoabinni a kan maa ta charuk ale mi nidoabinni Aizik.>>

ref: But God said, “Abraham, don't worry about your slave woman and the boy. Just do what Sarah tells you. Isaac will inherit your family name,
text: Alege Wen a weeni wa ayen, kan te fi nidoabinni ale fi dɔkliewa wari a daani fu. Wom Saara ale weeni fu dii meena la, diila ka Aizik nyiŋ ate m teeri fi buuni wari.

ref: but the son of the slave woman is also your son, and I will make his descendants into a great nation.”
text: M le basi ate dɔkliewa nidoabinni a chim tɛŋ mɛ dii nyiŋla wa ka fi buuri.>>

ref: Early the next morning Abraham gave Hagar an animal skin full of water and some bread. Then he put the boy on her shoulder and sent them away. They wandered around in the desert near Beersheba,
text: Vari ale veenti la saluik yokyokwa Abrayam yaa pai ŋandiinta ale bunlɔk ale nyiam a te Aga. Wa pai ba a vi wa ŋmankpala a basi ate wa cheŋ ale nidoabinni. Wa yaa cheŋ a ga bo Beerseba gɔnni po a tuinti.

ref: and after they had run out of water, Hagar put her son under a bush.
text: Bunlɔkku po nyiamu ale nueri la, wa yaa pai nidoabinni a dueni ti fiisa tɛŋ.

ref: Then she sat down a long way off, because she could not bear to watch him die. And she cried bitterly.
text: Wa yaa cheŋ niŋ nna maga a ga kala. Wa yaa poli ayen, <<M kan baga a nya nidoabinni ate wa a kpi.>> Wa le kala a moata dula la wa yaa piilim kumsa.

ref: When God heard the boy crying, the angel of God called out to Hagar from heaven and said, “Hagar, why are you worried? Don't be afraid. I have heard your son crying.
text: Wen yaa wom nidoabinni ale a kum la, ate Wen maarɔ yaa wi Aga nyini wenŋmazuk ayen, <<Ka boan wari, Aga? Kan chali yɔgsummoa, Wen a wom nidoabinni ale doa dula a kum la kama.

ref: Help him up and hold his hand, because I will make him the father of a great nation.”
text: Yik nidoabinni nisa a zak wa ate wa yiti, diila m le basi kama ate wa chim tɛŋ kpieŋ.>>

ref: Then God let her see a well. So she went to the well and filled the skin with water, then gave some to her son.
text: Wen yaa lak wa nina ate wa nya vili ale nyiam. Wa yaa cheŋ a ga sueri bunlɔkku ale nyiam a yaa te nidoabinni ate wa nyu.

ref: About this time Abimelech and his army commander Phicol said to Abraham, “God blesses everything you do!
text: Dila po Abimelek ale wa tugurik tɔŋdɔma kpagni Fikol yaa weeni Abrayam ayen, <<Wen a bo le fu kama wari meena ate fi a nye la po.

ref: Now I want you to promise in the name of God that you will always be loyal to me and my descendants, just as I have always been loyal to you in this land where you have lived as a foreigner.”
text: Yɔgyɔgla de pɛ Wen niŋ ayen fi kan nye venta nyeka ale mi yaase mi bisaŋa yaase m ŋaaŋsa. Sak mi ale tɛŋ kaai ate fi boro ase nichaanɔ la jiirim buui dek ate mi sak ale fu la.>>

ref: And so, Abraham promised he would.
text: Abrayam yaa weeni ayen, <<M pɛ ya.>>

ref: One day, Abraham told Abimelech, “Some of your servants have taken over one of my wells.”
text: Abrayam yaa fɔbili te Abimelek wa vinni ate Abimelek tomteerɔma a vari la.

ref: “This is the first I've heard about it,” Abimelech replied. “Why haven't you said something before? I don't have any idea who did it.”
text: Alege Abimelek yaa weeni ayen, <<Mi zɛ waai ale nye nnala la. Fi an weeni mi, ka jinla nyiini ate mi a wom.>>

ref: Abraham gave Abimelech some sheep and cattle, then the two men made a peace treaty.
text: Abrayam yaa ta piisa ale niiga a jam te Abimelek ate ba nɔi bayewa a nye nɔiyeŋ.

ref: Abraham separated seven female lambs from his flock of sheep,
text: Abrayam yaa gaari pasaa ŋayopɔi piisaŋa po,

ref: and Abimelech asked, “Why have you done this?”
text: ate Abimelek a bek Abrayam ayen, <<Pasaa ŋayopɔiwa de ate fi gaari la kiri ale boa?>>

ref: Abraham replied, “I want you to accept these seven lambs as proof that I dug this well.”
text: Wa yaa tulisi ayen, <<Siak a tuesi pasaa ŋayopɔiwa de mi nisa po ate ku chim seerɔ ayen ka mi ale tu vinni de.>>

ref: So they called the place Beersheba, because they made a treaty there.
text: Ate ba yaa wi dula ayen Beerseba, dii nyiŋla nuruba baye ale pɛ dula jigini.

ref: When the treaty was completed, Abimelech and his army commander Phicol went back to the land of the Philistines.
text: Nɔiyeŋka ale nye la ŋaaŋnyiŋ, Abimelek ale wa tugurik tɔŋdɔma kpagni Fikol yaa ŋman cheŋ Filistia demma tɛŋka.

ref: Abraham planted a tamarisk tree in Beersheba and worshiped the eternal Lord God.
text: Abrayam yaa vuk ti fiik Beerseba. Dula jigini wa yaa wi Naawen yuei, Wen Dii Ale Kan Ta Kpeglimka la.

ref: Then Abraham lived a long time as a foreigner in the land of the Philistines.
text: Abrayam yaa jam bo Filistia demma tɛŋka ate ku benni.

ref: Some years later God decided to test Abraham, so he spoke to him. Abraham answered, “Here I am, Lord.”
text: Ku yaa jam paari diipo ate Wen a nye Abrayam a nya. Wa weeni wa ayen, <<Abrayam.>> Abrayam tulisi ayen, <<Mi ale nna.>>

ref: The Lord said, “Go get Isaac, your only son, the one you dearly love! Take him to the land of Moriah, and I will show you a mountain where you must sacrifice him to me on the fires of an altar.”
text: Wen yaa weeni ayen, <<Pai fi nidoabinni, fi bi yeŋka, Aizik waai ate fi a yaali la, a ta cheŋ Moria tɛŋka a kaab wa dula ase juka kaabka la guutaŋa kunyi zuk ate m le weeni fu la.

ref: So Abraham got up early the next morning and chopped wood for the fire. He put a saddle on his donkey and set out with Isaac and two servants for the place where God had told him to go.
text: Vari ale veenti la saluik yokyokwa, Abrayam yaa yiti a gomsi wa boniŋ. Wa yaa basi ate yɔmma baye ale wa nidoabinni Aizik a va wa. Wa le yaali daata a magsi juka kaabka la, wa yaa lo suik po a cheŋ juijui ate Wen a weeni wa la.

ref: Three days later Abraham looked off in the distance and saw the place.
text: Daa ŋatawa danni Abrayam yaa zak zuk a nya jigini nna niŋniŋŋa.

ref: He told his servants, “Stay here with the donkey, while my son and I go over there to worship. We will come back.”
text: Wa yaa weeni wa yɔmma ayen, <<Ni wari dela ale bonika alege ate mi ale biika a cheŋ dela. Ti le puusi abe ti ŋman jam ni jigi.

ref: Abraham put the wood on Isaac's shoulder, but he carried the hot coals and the knife. As the two of them walked along,
text: Abrayam yaa pai juka kaabka daataŋa a ji wa nidoabinni Aizik, alege wadek a yik bolimu ale gebika. Ase bayewa ale a cheŋ ale chaab la,

ref: Isaac said, “Father, we have the coals and the wood, but where is the lamb for the sacrifice?” “My son,” Abraham answered, “God will provide the lamb.” The two of them walked on, and
text: Abrayam yaa tulisi ayen, <<Wen dek ale te pabiik a magsi juka kaabka, m biiga.>> Ate bayewa a va chaab a cheŋ.

ref: when they reached the place that God had told him about, Abraham built an altar and placed the wood on it. Next, he tied up his son and put him on the wood.
text: Ba le paari juijui ate Wen a weeni la, Abrayam yaa sɛ kuŋkuŋ dula a dueni daataŋa ku zuk a magsi. Wa yaa bobi wa nidoabinni Aizik a dueni kuŋkuŋku zuk daataŋa zuk.

ref: He then took the knife and got ready to kill his son.
text: Wa yaa laari nisa a pai gebika ayen wa ko Aizik.

ref: But the Lord's angel shouted from heaven, “Abraham! Abraham!” “Here I am!” he answered.
text: Alege Naawen maarɔwa a wi nyini wenŋmazuk ayen, <<Abrayam, Abrayam>> ate wa tulisi ayen, <<Nya mi ale nna.>>

ref: “Don't hurt the boy or harm him in any way!” the angel said. “Now I know that you truly obey God, because you were willing to offer him your only son.”
text: Wa yaa weeni Abrayam ayen, <<Kan pai nisa a tiri biika, kan nye wa jaaboa. Yɔgyɔgla de mi seba ayen fi a chali Wen kama, dii nyiŋla fi an peri fi bi yeŋka mi te ka.

ref: Abraham looked up and saw a ram caught by its horns in the bushes. So he took the ram and sacrificed it instead of his son.
text: Abrayam yaa zak zuk a nya padiak ale ka pai ka nyiila a saksi tikiba po a za. Wa yaa cheŋ a ga yik ka a kaabi ase juka kaabka la, alege basi nidoabinni.

ref: Abraham named that place “The Lord Will Provide.” And even now people say, “On the mountain of the Lord it will be provided.”
text: Abrayam yaa wi dula jigini ayen, <<Naawen Ale Te.>> A zaani dila a jam paari jinla ba weeni kama ayen, <<Naawen guuku zuk wa le te.>>

ref: The Lord's angel called out from heaven a second time:
text: Naawen maarɔwa yaa wi Abrayam a nyini wenŋmazuk a nye buye

ref: You were willing to offer your only son to the Lord, and so he makes you this solemn promise,
text: a weeni ayen, <<Mi a pɛ ka m dek nyiŋ, Naawen weeni, fi le nye nna la nyiŋ alege an peri fi nidoabinni, fi bi yeŋka mi te ka la,

text: M le sum niak fu abe m basi ate fi ŋaaŋsaŋa a piisi ase chiŋmarisaŋa ale bo wenŋmazukku ale tintain busuŋ ale bo beli po la. Fi ŋaaŋsaŋa ale tuesi ba dachaasaŋa tɛŋsaŋa a tara.

ref: You have obeyed me, and so you and your descendants will be a blessing to all nations on earth.”
text: M le va ka fi ŋaaŋbisaŋa nyiŋ a niak waai meena tɛŋzuk, dii nyiŋla fi zuli mu kama.

ref: Abraham and Isaac went back to the servants who had come with him, and they returned to Abraham's home in Beersheba. The Children of Nahor
text: Ate Abrayam yaa ŋmani wa tomteerɔba jigi, ate ba meena a lo suik po a cheŋ Beerseba. Abrayam yaa jam bo Beerseba.

ref: Nahor also had another wife. Her name was Reumah, and she had four sons: Tebah, Gaham, Tahash, and Maacah.
text: Wa nɔŋku waai yuei jam le Areuma la mɛ a biak ka nidoabilisa. Ba yue jam le Teba ale Gayam ale Tayas ale Maaka.

ref: he went to the Hittites and said,
text: Abrayam yaa yiti wa powa tɛŋ a biisi ale Yit demma. Wa weeni ayen,

ref: “I live as a foreigner in your land, and I don't own any property where I can bury my wife. Please let me buy a piece of land.”
text: <<Mi ka nichaanɔ a bo ni po. Ni pa tɛŋ a da te mu dela ate m gu m powa.

ref: “Sir,” they answered, “you are an important man. Choose the best place to bury your wife. None of us would refuse you a resting place for your dead.”
text: <<Wom ti le a weeni diila. Fi ka nabiik kaai ale zuak a bo le ti. Lueri tama vieŋa bunyi a gu fi powa. Ti waai a kan zɛri ayen fi kan gu fi kpiewa, wa vɔrup po.>>

ref: and replied, “If you are willing to let me bury my wife here, please ask Zohar's son Ephron
text: Wa weeni ba ayen, <<Ni daa siak ayen m gu m kpiewa, ni be wom mu a biisi ale Efron ale ka Zoyar biik la a te mu

text: ate wa pa Makpela vɔrumu ate wa soa la ate bu bo talimu kpeglimka jigini la a da te mu. Ni weeni wa ate wa pai da bu le magsi diila a te mu ate ku chim guka jigi ni tɛŋka po.>>

ref: Ephron was sitting there near the city gate, when Abraham made this request, and he answered,

ref: “Sir, the whole field, including the cave, is yours. With my own people as witnesses, I freely give it to you as a burial place for your dead.”
text: Wa weeni ayen, <<Aaya, m nyɔnɔ. Wom mi m pai talimu a te fu, ale pai vɔrumu ale bo talimu po la a te fu. M pai a te fu m nuruma niŋ. Gu fi kpiewa.>>

ref: Once again, Abraham bowed down
text: Abrayam ŋman koori tɛŋ, tɛŋka demma niŋ

ref: and said to Ephron, “In front of these witnesses, I offer you the full price, so I can bury my wife. Please accept my offer.”
text: a weeni Efron nuruma tue po ayen, <<Wom mi, ase fi le a yaali. M le tuni talimu ale ka diila. Siak a tuesi m jigi ate m gu m kpiewa dula.>>

ref: “But sir,” the man replied, “the property is worth only
text: <<Wom dɛ, m nyɔnɔ, tɛŋka ka kpana kobsinaansi, alege kula ka boa mi ale fi suŋsuŋŋa? Gu fi kpiewa.>>

ref: Abraham accepted Ephron's offer and paid him the
text: Abrayam ase wa nitalim la, Yit demma ale jam wa tɛŋka nansuiŋku nɔi la niŋ.

ref: So Abraham buried his wife Sarah in Machpelah Cave that was in the field
text: Ku ŋaaŋnyiŋ Abrayam yaa gu wa powa Saara vɔrumu ale bo Makpela a moata Mamre ale ka Yebron a bo Keenan tɛŋka po la.

ref: Abraham was now a very old man. The Lord had made him rich, and he was successful in everything he did.
text: Yɔgyɔgla de Abrayam yaa jam kpaŋ ate wa benaŋa a cheŋ. Naawen a jam niak wa kama nna yegayega.

ref: One day, Abraham called in his most trusted servant and said to him, “Solemnly promise me
text: Wa yaa weeni tomteerɔma meena kpagni waai ale a nya wa ŋantaŋa meena zuk la ayen, <<Pai fi nisaŋa a yik m kɔsukku tɛŋka.

ref: in the name of the Lord, who rules heaven and earth, that you won't choose a wife for my son Isaac from the people here in the land of Canaan.
text: Mi a yaali ka fi pɛ Naawen yuenni, Wen dii ale soa wenzuk ale tɛŋka la nyiŋ ayen fi kan yaali nipok Keenan demma ate mi bo ale ba la liema po a te m nidoabinni,

ref: But the servant asked, “What if the young woman I choose refuses to leave home and come here with me? Should I send Isaac there to look for a wife?”
