ref: When I came to the well today, I silently prayed, “You, Lord, are the God my master Abraham worships, so please lead me to a wife for his son
text: <<Mi ale jam paari bulika jinla la, m yaa weeni ayen, <Naawen, mi kpagni Abrayam Wenni, fi daa yaali, niak m chelimu ate mi cheŋ jam nna.

ref: while I am here at the well. When a young woman comes out to get water, I'll ask her to give me a drink.
text: Nya, mi za ka bulika de tɛŋ. Nipokbili a dan jam ayen wa dari nyiam ate mi weeni wa ayen, <<Mi a saalim te mu nyiam maga fi liikka po,>>

ref: If she gives me a drink and offers to get some water for my camels, I'll know she is the one you have chosen.”
text: ate wa weeni mi ayen, <<Be ŋoa a nyu, m le dari nyiam a te fi laalaakomima mɛ,>> Basi ate wa chim waai ate Naawen a lueri magsi mi kpagni nidoabinni la.>

ref: Even before I had finished praying, Rebekah came by with a water jar on her shoulder. When she had filled the jar, I asked her for a drink.
text: <<Alege ate mi nueri puusaŋa mi suinummu po la, Aribeka a deri nyini ale liik wa ŋmankpala zuk. Wa yaa cheŋ siŋ bulika a dari nyiam, ate mi a weeni wa ayen, <Mi a saalim te mu nyiam ate m nyu.>

ref: She quickly lowered the jar from her shoulder and said, “Have a drink. Then I'll get water for your camels.” So I drank, and after that she got some water for my camels.
text: <<Ate wa nye nwuli a te nyiamu nyini wa ŋmankpaluku zuk a siŋ tɛŋ ayen, <Be nyu, m le te fi laalaakomima mɛ a nyu.> Ate mi nyu, ate wa te laalaakomima mɛ a nyu.

ref: I asked her who her father was, and she answered, “My father is Bethuel the son of Nahor and Milcah.” At once I put the ring in her nose and the bracelets on her arms.
text: <<Mi yaa bek wa ayen, <Ka wana lie ale fi?> Wa yaa tulisi ayen, <Mi ka Nayo nidoabinni Betuel lie. Milka ale biak Betuel a te wa.> Ate mi yaa pai nifelinni a su wa nyuenni ale pai baŋsaŋa a su wa nisimaŋa,

ref: Then I bowed my head and gave thanks to the God my master Abraham worships. The Lord had led me straight to my master's relatives, and I had found a wife for his son.
text: ate mi yaa koori tɛŋ a puusi Naawen, mi pak Naawen, mi kpagni Abrayam Wenni, waai ale basi ate mi nya suik maŋ a ga yaali mi kpagni suoku ŋaaŋ a te wa nidoabinni la.

ref: Now please tell me if you are willing to do the right thing for my master. Will you treat him fairly, or do I have to look for another young woman?
text: Yɔgyɔgla de ni daa sak jiirim ale wensie ale mi kpagni, ni weeni mu be dan kaa daa di ni weeni mu, ate m seba mi nin va suik kuui la.>>

ref: Laban and Bethuel answered, “The Lord has done this. We have no choice in the matter.
text: Laban ale Betuel yaa tulisi ayen, <<Kude nyini ka Naawen jigi, ti an baga a weeni wari ayen kan nna yaa ku daa di.

ref: Take Rebekah with you; she can marry your master's son, just as the Lord has said.”
text: Aribeka ale nna, ta wa a cheŋ ate wa chim fi kpagni nidoabinni pok, a va chaab ale Naawen ale dak diila.

ref: Abraham's servant bowed down and thanked the Lord.
text: Abrayam tomteerɔwa ale wom ba le weeni diila, wa yaa koori tɛŋ Naawen niŋ.

ref: Then he gave clothing, as well as silver and gold jewelry, to Rebekah. He also gave expensive gifts to her brother and her mother.
text: Tomteerɔwa yaa yeeri ligmɔniŋ ale kpana ŋanta ale gatta a te Aribeka. Wa pai ŋan baai diak ale pagra la tiirita a te wa toawa ale wa mawa.

ref: “Let Rebekah stay with us for a week or ten days,” they answered. “Then she may go.”
text: Alege wa toawa ale wa mawa yaa tulisi ayen, Basi ate nipokbinni a wari bo ale ti daa pi abe fi cheŋ.

ref: But he said, “Don't make me stay any longer. The Lord has already helped me find a wife for my master's son. Now let us return.”
text: Alege weeni ba ayen, ni kan peri mu, yɔgyɔgla de ate Naawen a niak m chelimu la. Ni basi ate m cheŋ m kpagni jigi.

ref: They answered, “Let's ask Rebekah what she wants to do.”
text: Ba yaa weeni ayen, ni te ti wi nipokbinni a bek wa ku wari.

ref: They called her and asked, “Are you willing to leave with this man at once?” “Yes,” she answered.
text: Ba yaa wi Aribeka a bek wa ayen, <<Fi le cheŋ ale nuruwa de?>> Wa yaa weeni ayen, <<Mi le cheŋ ale wa.>>

ref: So they agreed to let Rebekah and an old family servant woman leave immediately with Abraham's servant and his men.
text: Ba yaa basi Aribeka suik ale wa dɔkliewa ale Abrayam tomteerɔwa ale wa nuruma.

ref: They gave Rebekah their blessing and said, “We pray that God will give you many children and grandchildren and that he will help them defeat their enemies.”
text: Ba yaa niak Aribeka ale weeni wa ayen, <<Ti toa, be nya niaka a paari tusa abe fi ŋaaŋsaŋa a tuesi ba dachaasaŋa nansuiŋtaŋa a tara.>>

ref: Afterwards, Rebekah and the young women who were to travel with her prepared to leave. Then they got on camels and left with Abraham's servant and his men.
text: Aribeka ale wa dɔkliema yaa gomsi badek a do laalaakomima a cheŋ ale nuruwa. Tomteerɔwa yaa ta Aribeka a cheŋ.

ref: At that time Isaac was living in the southern part of Canaan near a place called “The Well of the Living One Who Sees Me.”
text: Yɔgyɔgla de Aizik yaa nyini Beer Layai Aroi a jam, diila wa jam bo ka Negev.

ref: One evening he was walking out in the fields, when suddenly he saw a group of people approaching on camels. So he started toward them. Rebekah saw him coming; she got down from her camel, and asked, “Who is that man?” “He is my master Isaac,” the servant answered. Then Rebekah covered her face with her veil.
text: a bek tomteerɔwa ayen, <<Ka nuru boa le la a nyini peelimmu po a cheena ayen wa tu ti?>> Tomteerɔwa yaa tulisi ayen wa ka m kpagni. Wa yaa pai wa garukku a lik wadek.

ref: The servant told Isaac everything that had happened.
text: Tomteerɔwa yaa weeni Aizik wa le nye dii meena la.

ref: Isaac took Rebekah into the tent where his mother had lived before she died, and Rebekah became his wife. He loved her and was comforted over the loss of his mother.
text: Aizik yaa ta wa jam jo wa mawa Saara viuku po a faari Aribeka. Wa yaa chim wa pok, ate wa a yaali wa. Aizik sui a jam yɔk kama wa mawa kummu ŋaaŋnyiŋ.

ref: and they had six sons: Zimran, Jokshan, Medan, Midian, Ishbak, and Shuah.
text: Wa jam biak ka Zimran ale Joksan ale Medan ale Midian ale Isbak ale Suya a te Abrayam.

ref: Later, Jokshan became the father of Sheba and Dedan, and when Dedan grew up, he had three sons: Asshurim, Letushim, and Leummim.
text: Joksan jam ka Siiba ale Dedan ko. Dedan ŋaaŋbisa jam le Assur demma ale Letus demma ale Leummi demma.

ref: Midian also had five sons: Ephah, Epher, Hanoch, Abida, and Eldaah.
text: Midian nidoabilisaŋa jam le Efa ale Efer ale Yanok ale Abida ale Eldaa. Bade meena jam ka Ketura ŋaaŋbisa.

ref: While Abraham was still alive, he gave gifts to the sons of Hagar and Keturah. He also sent their sons to live in the east far from his son Isaac, and when Abraham died, he left everything to Isaac. The Death of Abraham
text: Alege wa le jam diem vua la wa yaa te wa pooma nidoabilisaŋa tiirita a yaa basi ate ba va kori geŋka alege Aizik.

ref: Abraham died at the ripe old age of
text: Abrayam jam kpaŋ kama a nya bena zuak alege kpi a cheŋ wa nuruma jigi.

ref: His sons Isaac and Ishmael buried him east of Hebron in Machpelah Cave that was part of the field Abraham had bought from Ephron son of Zohar the Hittite. Abraham was buried there beside his wife Sarah.
text: talim buui ate Abrayam a da Yit demma jigi la. Dula jigini ate ba gu Abrayam ale wa powa.

ref: God blessed Isaac after this, and Isaac moved to a place called “The Well of the Living One Who Sees Me.” Ishmael's Descendants
text: Abrayam kummu ŋaaŋnyiŋ Wen yaa niak wa nidoabinni Aizik waai ale jam bo juijui ale moata Beer Layai Aroi la.

ref: Ishmael was the son of Abraham and Hagar, the slave woman of Sarah.
text: Abrayam nidoabinni Ismael waai ate Saara dɔkliewa Aga ate wa nyini Ejipiti a biak a te Abrayam la buuni ale nna.

ref: Mishma, Dumah, Massa,
text: ale Misma ale Duma ale Massa

ref: Hadad, Tema, Jetur, Naphish, and Kedemah.
text: ale Yadad ale Tema ale Jetur ale Nafis ale Kedema.

ref: Each of Ishmael's sons was a tribal chief, and a village was named after each of them.
text: Ismael nidoabilisaŋa a jam le nna, ba buura pi ale ŋayewa yuei ale nna, a ta va chaab ale ba le kali diila.

ref: Ishmael had settled in the land east of his brothers, and his sons settled everywhere from Havilah to Shur, east of Egypt on the way to Asshur. Ishmael was
text: Wa ŋaaŋbisaŋa a jam kali ka Yavila tɛŋka a ga paari Sur, a moata Ejipiti banni, ase fi nin tuak Assur geŋka. Ba jam ta ka nyeka tuem ale ba suataŋa ba boka po meena.

ref: Isaac was the son of Abraham,
text: Abrayam nidoabinni Aizik buuni jam le nna. Abrayam jam ka Aizik ko.

ref: Rebekah still had no children. So Isaac asked the Lord to let her have a child, and the Lord answered his prayer.
text: Aizik yaa zaani wa powa nankula po a puusi a te Naawen dii nyiŋla wa powa jam ka kirik. Naawen yaa tulisi wa puusaŋa ate wa powa Aribeka a yik lɔgi.

ref: Before Rebekah gave birth, she knew she was going to have twins, because she could feel them inside her, fighting each other. She thought, “Why is this happening to me?” Finally, she asked the Lord why her twins were fighting,
text: Bisaŋa yaa jaa tusi chaab wa ponni po, ate wa yaa weeni ayen, <<Ka boa ale soa ate wanni de a nye mi nna?>> Wa yaa cheŋ ayen wa bek Naawen a nya.

ref: and he told her: “Your two sons will become two separate nations. The younger of the two will be stronger, and the older son will be his servant.”
text: Naawen yaa weeni wa ayen, <<Tɛŋsa siye ale bo fi ponni po, nuruba bayewa ale bo fi po la ale poori chaab. Nuru wanyi ale pagra a gaam nwala, ate kpagni ale tom a te baaŋka.>>

ref: the first baby was covered with red hair, so he was named Esau.
text: Waai ale dɛ niŋ a nyini la jam ka jamɔniŋ, ate nyiŋka meena a nye ase garuk kobtuk la. Ba yaa te wa yuei ayen Iisɔ.

ref: As Jacob and Esau grew older, Esau liked the outdoors and became a good hunter, while Jacob lived the quiet life of a shepherd.
text: Nidoabilisaŋa yaa chim ate Iisɔ a chim waai ale seba yaaliŋka. Wa jam ka waai ale bo peelim. Alege Jekob a chim waai ale kaa yaa biiga, a bo viitaŋa po.

ref: Esau would take the meat of wild animals to his father Isaac, so Isaac loved him more, but Jacob was his mother's favorite son.
text: Aizik ale jaa yaali gɔi lanta la, a yaali Iisɔ alege Aribeka a yaali Jekob.

ref: One day, when Jacob was cooking some stew, Esau came home hungry
text: Diipo nna ate Jekob yaa jam boro a dik jenta. Iisɔ yaa nyini peelim a jam ate kɔm a ta wa nna yegayega.

ref: and said, “I'm starving to death! Here and now give me some of that red stew!” That's how Esau got the name “Edom.”
text: Wa yaa weeni Jekob ayen, <<Nye nwuli a te mu jen muntaŋa, kɔm ale ta mu.>> Dila ale soa ate ba jam a wi wa mɛ ayen Edom la.

ref: Jacob replied, “Sell me your rights as the first-born son.”
text: Jekob yaa tulisi ayen, <<De niŋ a pai fi biam kpaŋtaŋa a da te mu.>>

ref: “I'm about to die,” Esau answered. “What good will those rights do me?”
text: Iisɔ yaa weeni ayen, <<Nya, mi a nye ayen m kpi kama, biam kpaŋta a ta boan jaab wamaŋ a te mu?>>

ref: But Jacob said, “Promise me your birthrights, here and now!” And that's what Esau did.
text: Alege Jekob a weeni ayen, <<De niŋ a pɛ.>> Dila nyiŋ wa yaa pɛ, a pai wa biam kpaŋtaŋa a da a te Jekob.

ref: Jacob then gave Esau some bread and some of the bean stew, and when Esau had finished eating and drinking, he just got up and left, showing how little he thought of his rights as the first-born.
text: Jekob yaa te Iisɔ boroborok ale jentaŋa. Wa yaa dɛ ale nyu alege a yiti a cheŋ. Wa an pai zula a te wa biam kpaŋtaŋa.

ref: Once during Abraham's lifetime, the fields had not produced enough grain, and now the same thing happened. So Isaac went to King Abimelech of the Philistines in the land of Gerar,
text: Yɔgyɔgla de kɔm ale jam bo tɛŋka po, daa kɔm buui ale jam boro Abrayam daaŋa po la. Aizik yaa cheŋ Filistia demma naawa Abimelek jigi Gerar.

ref: because the Lord had appeared to Isaac and said: Isaac, stay away from Egypt! I will show you where I want you to go.
text: Naawen yaa pai wadek a sak Aizik a weeni wa ayen, <<Kan cheŋ Ejipiti, bo tɛŋ kaai po ate mi a weeni ayen fi boro la.

ref: You will live there as a foreigner, but I will be with you and bless you. I will keep my promise to your father Abraham by giving this land to you and your descendants.
text: Bo tɛŋka de po magadega, ate m bo ale fu abe m niak fu. Fi ale fi ŋaaŋbisaŋa m le pai tɛŋsaŋa de a te abe m sak m le ta pɛ kaai ate m pɛ ale fi kowa Abrayam la.

ref: I will give you as many descendants as there are stars in the sky, and I will give your descendants all of this land. They will be a blessing to every nation on earth,
text: M le basi ate fi ŋaaŋbisaŋa a piisi ase chiŋmarisaŋa ale bo wenzukku la. M le te ba tɛŋsaŋa de meena. Fi buuni nyiŋ tɛŋsa meena ale nya niaka,

ref: Isaac moved to Gerar
text: Aizik yaa jam bo Gerar.

ref: After Isaac had been there a long time, King Abimelech looked out a window and saw Isaac hugging and kissing Rebekah.
text: Aizik ale bo dula ate ku beni la, Abimelek Filistia demma naawa yaa va tuokparuk a nya Aizik ale wa a gbaari wa powa Aribeka.

ref: Abimelech called him in and said, “Rebekah must be your wife! Why did you say she is your sister?” “Because I thought someone would kill me,” Isaac answered.
text: Abimelek yaa wi Aizik a weeni ayen, <<Wa sum ka fi pok. Ka boa ale soa ate fi weeni ayen, <Wa ka m toa?> >> Aizik yaa tulisi wa ayen, <<Dii nyiŋla mi poli kama ayen mi ni jɔk m nyuvunni wa nyiŋ.>>

ref: “Don't you know what you've done?” Abimelech exclaimed. “If someone had slept with her, you would have made our whole nation guilty!”
text: Abimelek yaa weeni ayen, <<Ka boa ate fi nye ti di? Nuruma wanyi ate baga kama a goa ale fi powa, ate fi ta nye kaasuŋ a jam ti zuk.>>

ref: Then Abimelech warned his people that anyone who even touched Isaac or Rebekah would be put to death.
text: Abimelek yaa te nɔi nuruma meena, <<Nuru waai a dan namsi nuruwa de yaase wa powa, ba le ko wa.>>

ref: Isaac planted grain and had a good harvest that same year. The Lord blessed him,
text: Aizik yaa bori ŋanta tɛŋ kala bein dila dek a che nna yegayega dii nyiŋla Naawen a niak wa kama.

ref: and Isaac was so successful that he became very rich.
text: Nuruwa yaa jam jigsi, ate wa nitalimu kasim a zuak, ate wa jam jigsi nna yegayega.

ref: In fact, the Philistines were jealous of the large number of sheep, goats, and slaves that Isaac owned,
text: Wa jam ta ka piisa ale niiga nna yegayega ale yɔm ate Filistia demma a de nyuri ale wa.

ref: and they stopped up the wells that Abraham's servants had dug before his death.
text: Vila ŋaai meena ate wa kowa tomteerɔma jam tu diipo ate wa kowa boro la, Filistia demma a jam kasi ŋa kama ale si ŋa ale tanta.

ref: Finally, Abimelech said, “Isaac, I want you to leave our country. You have become too powerful to stay here.”
text: Abimelek yaa weeni Aizik ayen, <<Cheŋ abe fi ge ti, fi pagra ka nna yega a te ti.>>

ref: Isaac left and settled in Gerar Valley,
text: Aizik yaa basi dula ale ga kali Gerar Viaka po.

ref: where he cleaned out those wells that the Philistines had stopped up. Isaac also gave each of the wells the same name that Abraham had given to them.
text: Aizik yaa ŋman guti vila ŋaai ate ba tu wa kowa daaŋa po ate Filistia demma a jam si ŋa Abrayam kummu ŋaaŋnyiŋ la. Wa yaa te ŋa yue ŋaai dek ate wa kowa jam te la.

ref: While his servants were digging in the valley, they found a spring-fed well.
text: Aizik tomteerɔma a jam tu ka viak po a nya nyiam paalik dula.

ref: But the shepherds of Gerar Valley quarreled with Isaac's shepherds and claimed the water belonged to them. So this well was named “Quarrel,” because they had quarreled with Isaac.
text: Alege Gerar naapeerisaŋa a kpaliŋ ale Aizik naapeerisaŋa a weeni ayen, <<Nyiamu ka tama ale soa.>> Wa yaa te vinni yuei ayen Esek, dii nyiŋla ba nye ka kpaliŋ ale wa.

ref: Isaac's servants dug another well, and the shepherds also quarreled about it. So that well was named “Jealous.”
text: Ba ŋman tu dichoa, alege ba ŋman kpaliŋ vili dila mɛ nyiŋ ate wa te di yuei ayen Sitna.

ref: Finally, they dug one more well. There was no quarreling this time, and the well was named “Lots of Room,” because the Lord had given them room and would make them very successful.
text: Wa basi dula ale ga tu dichoa, alege waai an nye kpaliŋ di nyiŋŋa. Ate wa te di yuei ayen Areyobot, ayen, <<Yɔgyɔgla de Naawen a te ti vayɔgsum ti le nya tidek tɛŋka po.>>

ref: Isaac went on to Beersheba,
text: A zaani dula wa yaa cheŋ Beerseba.

ref: where the Lord appeared to him that night and told him, “Don't be afraid! I am the God who was worshiped by your father Abraham, my servant. I will be with you and bless you, and because of Abraham I will give you many descendants.”
text: Yok kula Naawen yaa pai wadek a sak wa a weeni ayen, <<Mi ka fi kowa Abrayam Wenni. Kan chali yɔgsum, ale mi a bo le fu kama, m le niak fu abe m basi ate fi ŋaaŋbisaŋa a piisi mi tomteerɔwa Abrayam nyiŋ.>>

ref: Isaac built an altar there and worshiped the Lord. Then he set up camp, and his servants started digging a well.
text: Aizik yaa sɛ kuŋkuŋ dula a wi Naawen yuenni. Dula wa yaa ti wa viuku, ate wa tomteerɔma a tu vili.

ref: Meanwhile, Abimelech had left Gerar and was taking his advisor Ahuzzath and his army commander Phicol to see Isaac.
text: Abimelek yaa nyini Gerar tɛŋka jam dula ale Ayuzzat waai ale a sak wa wie la ale Fikol wa tugurik tɔŋdɔma kpagni.

ref: When they arrived, Isaac asked, “Why are you here? Didn't you send me away because you hated me?”
text: Aizik yaa bek ba ayen, <<Ka boa ate nama a jam mi jigi ya, nama ale jam ta nyeka tuem ale mi a yiak mi la?>>

ref: They answered, “We now know for certain that the Lord is with you, and we have decided there needs to be a peace treaty between you and us. So let's make a solemn agreement
text: Ba yaa tulisi ayen, <<Tama nya kama cheri ayen Naawen a jam bo le fu kama, tama yaa weeni ayen, <Ku magsi ka pɛka nɔiyeŋ a bo ti suŋsuŋ, tama ale nama suŋsuŋ.> Basi ate ti nye nɔiyeŋ ale fu,

ref: not to harm each other. Remember, we have never hurt you, and when we sent you away, we let you go in peace. The Lord has truly blessed you.”
text: ate ni kan nye ti biemmoa, ase tama ale kan jam namsi ni alege nye ni a magsi ale basi ate ni cheŋ ale suiyɔgni la. Ate yɔgyɔgla de Naawen a niak fu la.

ref: Isaac gave a big feast for them, and everyone ate and drank.
text: Aizik yaa nye ŋandiinta tigi a te ba, ate ba dɛ ale nyu.

ref: Early the next morning Isaac and the others made a solemn agreement, then he let them go in peace.
text: Vari ale veenti la, saluik yokyokwa nidoama yaa pɛ a te chaab. Aizik yaa basi ba suik, ate ba basi wa ale suiyɔgni.

ref: Later that same day Isaac's servants came and said, “We've struck water!”
text: Dai dila danni Aizik tomteerɔma yaa jam weeni wa vinni ate ba tu la. Ba weeni ayen, <<Ti nya nyiam kama.>>

ref: So Isaac named the well Shibah, and the town is still called Beersheba. Esau's Foreign Wives
text: Wa yaa a wi di ayen Siba. A ta jam paari jinla tɛŋka yuei ale Beerseba.

ref: But these two women brought a lot of grief to Esau's parents Isaac and Rebekah.
text: Ba jam chim ka sui domsik a te Aizik ale Aribeka.

ref: Isaac was old and almost blind, when he called in his first-born son Esau, who asked him, “Father, what can I do for you?”
text: Aizik ale kpaŋ ate wa ninaŋa paam ate wa an ŋman baga a nya la, wa yaa wi wa bikpagni Iisɔ a weeni wa ayen, <<M biiga.>> Ate wa tulisi wa ayen, <<Nya mi ale nna.>>

ref: Isaac replied, “I am old and might die at any time.
text: Aizik yaa weeni ayen, <<Mi yaa ka nisɔmma a zɛ diipo ate mi ale kpi la.

ref: So go hunting with your bow and arrows and kill a wild animal.
text: Yɔgyɔgla de pai fi yaalim ŋantaŋa, fi namarika ale fi tommu a nyini peelim a yaalim a ko jaab a ta jam te mu.

ref: Cook some of that tasty food that I love so much and bring it to me. I want to eat it once more and give you my blessing before I die.”
text: Nye ŋandiin tii ale masa ate mi a yaali la a ta jam te mu ate m dɛ, abe m niak fu abe m ge a kpi.

ref: she said to Jacob, “I heard your father tell Esau
text: Aribeka yaa weeni wa nidoabinni Jekob ayen, <<Nya mi wom fi kowa a boro a weeni fi suoku ayen,

ref: to kill a wild animal and cook some tasty food for him before he dies. Your father said this because he wants to bless your brother with the Lord as his witness.
text: <Ta gɔi jaab a jam nye ŋandiin tii ale masa ate m dɛ, abe m niak fu Naawen niŋ abe m ge kpi.>

ref: Now, my son, listen carefully to what I want you to do.
text: Yɔgyɔgla de, m biiga, chiib wom nalimnyiini a nye dii ate mi a weeni fu la.

ref: Go and kill two of your best young goats and bring them to me. I'll cook the tasty food that your father loves so much.
text: Nyini cheŋ booŋa jigini a lueri bo paala ŋaye a ta jam, ate m nye ŋandiin masa a te fi kowa, ase wa le a yaali diila.

ref: Then you can take it to him, so he can eat it and give you his blessing before he dies.”
text: Ate fi yaa cheŋ ga te fi kowa ate wa dɛ, abe wa te fu wa niauŋku abe wa ge kpi.>>

ref: “My brother Esau is a hairy man,” Jacob reminded her. “And I am not.
text: Jekob yaa weeni wa mawa Aribeka ayen, <<Alege mi suoku ka waai ale chim kɔbta nuru la, ate mi chim nuru waai ale ta nyiŋ saalik la.

ref: If my father touches me and realizes I am trying to trick him, he will put a curse on me instead of giving me a blessing.”
text: Alege m kowa a nin tiri mu? Ku le nye ase mi a paasi wa kama ate m ta kikaasa a jam m dek zuk alege daa niauŋŋoa.>>

ref: Rebekah insisted, “Let his curse fall on me! Just do what I say and bring me the meat.”
text: Wa mawa yaa weeni wa ayen, <<M biiga, basi ate kikaasaŋa a jam mi zuk. Nye dii ate mi a weeni la, ga ta ŋa jam te mu.>>

ref: So Jacob brought the meat to his mother, and she cooked the tasty food that his father liked.
text: Wa yaa ga ta ŋa jam te wa mawa ate wa nye ŋandiin masa tii ate wa kowa a yaali la.

ref: Then she took Esau's best clothes and put them on Jacob.
text: Aribeka yaa pai Iisɔ wa bikpagni garuk naluŋ ate wa ta yenni po la a jo wa bi baaŋka Jekob.

ref: She also covered the smooth part of his hands and neck with goatskins
text: Wa maa lik wa nisimaŋa ale wa ŋinni ale saala la ale buuk gbana.

ref: and gave him some bread and the tasty food she had cooked.
text: Wa yaa pai ŋandiin tii ale masa la ale boroborok ate wa nye la a te wa nidoabinni Jekob.

ref: Jacob went to his father and said, “Father, here I am.” “Which one of my sons are you?” his father asked.
text: Wa yaa cheŋ wa kowa jigi a weeni wa ayen, <<M koa.>> Wa kowa yaa tulisi wa ayen, <<M biiga, ka wana?>>

ref: Jacob replied, “I am Esau, your first-born, and I have done what you told me. Please sit up and eat the meat I have brought. Then you can give me your blessing.”
text: Jekob yaa tulisi wa kowa ayen, <<Mi ka Iisɔ fi bikpagni. Fi le weeni diila, mi nye kama. Mi a saalim yiti kali a dɛ ŋan baai ate mi yaalim la abe fi te mu fi niauŋku.>>

ref: Isaac asked, “My son, how did you find an animal so quickly?” “The Lord your God was kind to me,” Jacob answered.
text: Aizik yaa bek wa nidoabinni ayen, <<Fi nye ka sɛ a nya nna nwuli, m biiga?>> Wa yaa tulisi ayen, <<Naawen fi Wenni ale maari mu.>>

ref: “My son,” Isaac said, “come closer, where I can touch you and find out if you really are Esau.”
text: Aizik yaa weeni Jekob ayen, <<Forisi jam ate m tiri fu m biiga, ate m seba ase fi sum ka m nidoabinni Iisɔ yaase daa wa.>>

ref: Jacob went closer. His father touched him and said, “You sound like Jacob, but your hands feel hairy like Esau's.”
text: Jekob yaa forisi paari wa kowa Aizik, ate wa tiri wa a weeni ayen, <<Lueluku a nye ase ka Jekob lueluk la, alege nisimaŋa a nye ase Iisɔ nisima la.>>

ref: Isaac asked, “Are you really my son Esau?” “Yes, I am,” Jacob answered.
text: Wa yaa bek ayen, <<Fi sum ka m nidoabinni?>> Ate wa tulisi ayen, <<Ka mi.>>

ref: So Isaac told him, “Serve me the wild meat, and I can give you my blessing.” Jacob gave him some meat, and he ate it. He also gave him some wine, and he drank it.
text: Wa yaa weeni ayen, <<M biiga, ta fi le yaalim jabuui la a jam ate m dɛ, ate m te fu m niauŋku.>> Jekob yaa ta jam te wa ate wa dɛ, ale ta daam mɛ jam te wa ate wa nyu.

ref: Then Isaac said, “Son, come over here and kiss me.”
text: Wa kowa Aizik yaa weeni wa ayen, <<Jam dela, m biiga, a puusi mu.>>

ref: While Jacob was kissing him, Isaac caught the smell of his clothes and said: “The smell of my son is like a field the Lord has blessed.
text: Wa yaa cheŋ wa jigi a puusi wa. Aizik ale wom wa gattaŋa nyummu la, wa yaa niak wa a weeni ayen, <<Aa, m biiga nyummu a nye ase ka talim ate Naawen a niak la.

ref: God will bless you, my son, with dew from heaven and with fertile fields, rich with grain and grapes.
text: Mi a yaali ayen Wen a te fu wenzuk maglim ale tɛŋzuk jigsika kpari ŋanta ale da paalik.

ref: Nations will be your servants and bow down to you. You will rule over your brothers, and they will kneel at your feet. Anyone who curses you will be cursed; anyone who blesses you will be blessed.”
text: Mi a yaali ayen tɛŋsa a tom te fu abe nuruba a koori tɛŋ a zuli fu. Chim kpagi fi suataŋa zuk, abe fi mawa bisaŋa a koori tɛŋ a zuli fu. Baai ale ka a te fu la ale nya kikaasa abe baai ale a niak fu la a nya niaka.>>

ref: Right after Isaac had given Jacob his blessing and Jacob had gone, Esau came back from hunting.
text: Aizik ale niak wa a nueri la, Jekob an basi wa kowa ate ku beni ya, wa suoku Iisɔ deri nyini yaalim jam.

ref: He cooked the tasty food, brought it to his father, and said, “Father, please sit up and eat the meat I have brought you, so you can give me your blessing.”
text: Wa mɛ yaa nye ŋandiin tii ale masa a ta jam te wa kowa. A yaa weeni wa ayen, <<M koa yiti kali a dɛ m yaalimu ŋantaŋa, abe fi te mu fi niaka.>>

ref: “Who are you?” Isaac asked. “I am Esau, your first-born son.”
text: Wa kowa Aizik yaa bek ayen, <<Fi ka wana?>> Wa yaa tulisi ayen, <<Mi ka fi nidoabinni, fi bikpagni Iisɔ.>>

ref: Isaac started trembling and said, “Then who brought me some wild meat right before you came in? I ate it and gave him a blessing that cannot be taken back.”
text: Aizik nyiŋ a deri a gɔk nna yegayega ate wa weeni ayen, <<Be ka wana ale yaalim a ta jam te mi? Ate mi dɛ alege ate fi jam ate mi niak wa, wa be za wa nya niaka kama.

ref: Isaac answered, “Your brother tricked me and stole your blessing.”
text: Alege Aizik a weeni ayen, <<Fi suoku a jam kama ale pimpaasima a tuesi niaka.>>

ref: Esau replied, “My brother deserves the name Jacob, because he has already cheated me twice. The first time he cheated me out of my rights as the first-born son, and now he has cheated me out of my blessing.” Then Esau asked his father, “Don't you have any blessing left for me?”
text: Iisɔ yaa weeni ayen, <<Daa wa yuenni sum ale Jekob ya? Wa paasi mu nna wa ka buye. Wa pa m biamu kpaŋtaŋa, yɔgyɔgla a pa m niaka.>> Wa yaa bek ayen, <<Fi an tali niaka a magsi mi?>>

ref: “My son,” Isaac answered, “I have made Jacob the ruler over you and your brothers, and all of you will be his servants. I have also promised him all the grain and grapes that he needs. There's nothing left that I can do for you.”
text: Aizik yaa tulisi ayen, <<Mi basi ate wa chim ka kpagi fi zuk ale basi ate wa mabisaŋa meena a chim wa tomteerɔba, ale niak wa ale kpari ŋanta ale da paalik ayen ba maari wa. M baga a nye ka boa a te fu, m biiga?>>

ref: “Father,” Esau asked, “don't you have more than one blessing? You can surely give me a blessing too!” Then Esau started crying again.
text: Iisɔ a weeni wa kowa ayen, <<Fi ta niaka bunyi dekki m koa? Niak mi mɛ, m koa. Iisɔ yaa a kaari.

ref: So his father said: “Your home will be far from that fertile land, where dew comes down from the heavens.
text: Wa kowa Aizik yaa tulisi wa ayen, <<Fi boka jigi ale yalima kama ale tɛŋka jigsika, ale yalima ale wenzuk maglimu.

ref: You will live by the power of your sword and be your brother's slave. But when you decide to be free, you will break loose.”
text: Fi le boro ale ka gebik abe fi tom a te fi suoku. Alege fi dan kaa nya vuusum fi le yeeri wa niiga duoku a yuk basi.>>

ref: Esau hated his brother Jacob because he had stolen the blessing that was supposed to be his. So he said to himself, “Just as soon as my father dies, I'll kill Jacob.”
text: Iisɔ yaa jam ta chaab ale Jekob dii nyiŋla niaka ate wa kowa a te wa la. Wa yaa weeni wadek ayen, <<Mi kowa kummu daaŋa a moata kama, m le ko m suoku Jekob.>>

ref: When Rebekah found out what Esau planned to do, she sent for Jacob and told him, “Son, your brother Esau is just waiting for a chance to kill you.
text: Ba le weeni Aribeka wa bikpagni ale weeni diila, wa yaa tom wi wa bi baaŋka Jekob a weeni wa ayen, <<Fi suoku ale ta popola ŋaai ale ŋa yɔk wa suinni la ale wa ko fu.

ref: Now listen carefully and do what I say. Go to the home of my brother Laban in Haran
text: Yɔgyɔgla de, m biiga, nye dii ate mi a weeni la. Chali nwuli a cheŋ mi toawa Laban jigi Yaran.

ref: and stay with him for a while. When Esau stops being angry
text: Bo dula ale wa ate fi suoku suinni a yɔk.

ref: and forgets what you have done to him, I'll send for you to come home. Why should I lose both of my sons on the same day?”
text: Fi suoku sui dan kan ŋmaa puuri ate wa baŋ fi le nye wa diila, m le tom te fi nɔi ate fi nyini dula jam. Ka boa ate maa jɔk ni nɔi niyewa dai yeŋŋa?>>

ref: Rebekah later told Isaac, “Those Hittite wives of Esau are making my life miserable! If Jacob marries a Hittite woman, I'd be better off dead.”
text: Aribeka yaa weeni Aizik ayen, <<Boka an masa te mu Yit nipooma de nyiŋ. Jekob a dan faari nipok tɛŋka de nipooma po, a nyini Yit nipooma de nna po, mi boka a kan ta kiri ya.>>

ref: Isaac called in Jacob, then gave him a blessing, and said: Don't marry any of those Canaanite women.
text: Dila nyiŋ Aizik yaa wi Jekob a niak wa ale ŋman weeni wa ayen, <<Kan faari Keenan lie.

ref: Go at once to your mother's father Bethuel in northern Syria and choose a wife from one of the daughters of Laban, your mother's brother.
text: Cheŋ Paddan Araam, fi mawa kowa Betuel yenni. Faari nipok a nyini Laban liema po wanyi, fi mawa toawa.

ref: I pray that God All-Powerful will bless you with many descendants and let you become a great nation.
text: Mi a yaali ayen ka Wen Dii Ale A Niak La abe niak fu abe wa nye ate fi buuni a piisi ate fi ŋaaŋsa a chim tɛŋ dem.

ref: May he bless you with the land he promised Abraham, so that you will take over this land where we now live as foreigners.
text: Mi a yaali ayen wa abe te fi ale fi ŋaaŋsaŋa niak kaai ate wa jam te Abrayam la, ate ni soa tɛŋ kaai ate ni bo ka po yɔgyɔgla de a chim nicham la, tɛŋ kaai ate Wen a jam pai te Abrayam la.

ref: Isaac then sent Jacob to stay with Rebekah's brother Laban, the son of Bethuel the Aramean. Esau Marries the Daughter of Ishmael
text: Ate Aizik yaa jam basi Jekob suik, ate wa cheŋ Paddan Araam, Laban ale ka Betuel biik la ate wa chim Araam denɔ la, Iisɔ ale Jekob mawa Aribeka toawa la jigi.

ref: Esau found out that his father Isaac had blessed Jacob and had warned him not to marry any of the Canaanite women. He also learned that Jacob had been sent to find a wife in northern Syria
text: Yɔgyɔgla de, Iisɔ yaa miŋ kama ayen Aizik a niak Jekob kama ale te ate wa cheŋ Paddan Araam ayen wa ga faari nipok dula, ate wa ale niak wa la, wa weeni wa kama ayen, <<Kan faari nipok waai ale nyini Keenan la,

ref: Esau already had several wives, but he now realized how much his father hated the Canaanite women.
text: Iisɔ ale jam miŋ Keenan nipooma ale jam a nye ate wa kowa Aizik sui a jam a puuri diila,

ref: So he married Ishmael's daughter Mahalath, who was the sister of Nebaioth and the granddaughter of Abraham. Jacob's Dream at Bethel
text: wa yaa cheŋ Ismael a ga faari Mayalat, ale ka Nebaiot suok la, Abrayam nidoabinni Ismael lie a gum wa pooma ate wa poom a ta la po.

ref: Jacob left the town of Beersheba and started out for Haran.
text: Jekob a jam basi Beerseba ale nyini a cheŋ Yaran.

ref: At sunset he stopped for the night and went to sleep, resting his head on a large rock.
text: Wa ga paari ka jigi, a yaa zaani dii nyiŋla wenbinni a siŋ wa, ate wa yaa pai tain a pai chim dafieluk a kpagli ayen wa goa.

ref: In a dream he saw a ladder that reached from earth to heaven, and God's angels were going up and down on it.
text: Wa yaa gɔdaasim a nya tiili ale zaani tɛŋ a paari wenzukku, ate Wen maarɔma a jueli ale a siŋ di zuk.

ref: The Lord was standing beside the ladder and said: I am the Lord God who was worshiped by Abraham and Isaac. I will give to you and your family the land on which you are now sleeping.
text: Naawen yaa bo wenzuk a weeni ayen, <<Mi ka Naawen, fi kowa Abrayam Wenni ale Aizik Wenni. M le pai tɛŋ kaai ate fi doa ka zuk la a te fu ale fi ŋaaŋbisaŋa.

ref: Your descendants will spread over the earth in all directions and will become as numerous as the specks of dust. Your family will be a blessing to all people.
text: Fi ŋaaŋbisaŋa ale nye ase ka tɛŋka tantaŋa la, ate ni ale teeri a nyini yeniŋka a ga paari konni po, a ŋman nyini wiiluk a ga paari yeseoku po. Fi ale fi ŋaaŋbisa nyiŋ, nuruba meena ale bo tɛŋka po la ale nya niaka.

ref: Wherever you go, I will watch over you, then later I will bring you back to this land. I won't leave you—I will do all I have promised.
text: M bo le fu kama ate jigi meena ate fi a cheŋ la, m le nya fi zuk, ate m ŋman ta fu a jam tɛŋka de po. M kan basi fu, ka m nin nye m le puulim fu diila.>>

ref: Jacob woke up suddenly and thought, “The Lord is in this place, and I didn't even know it.”
text: Jekob ale yiti wa goomu po la, wa yaa poli ayen, <<Chekki Naawen a bo dela jigini kama alege mi a zɛ.>>

ref: Then Jacob became frightened and said, “What a frightening place! It must be the house of God and the gateway to heaven.”
text: Yɔgsum yaa ta wa ate wa weeni ayen, <<Jigi kperikaliik ale nna! Dela daa jigi yoŋ alege ka Wen yeri. Wenŋmazuk nansuiŋku ale nna.>>

ref: When Jacob got up early the next morning, he took the rock that he had used for a pillow and stood it up as a place of worship. Then he poured olive oil on the rock to dedicate it to God,
text: Varivientini ku saluik yokyok Jekob yaa pai buntain dii ate wa pai kpagli la, a feri di a zaani a yaa kpiiri kpaam a basi di zuk.

ref: and he named the place Bethel. Before that it had been named Luz.
text: Wa te jigini yuei ayen Betel, alege ba jam a wi tɛŋ kala ayen ka Luz.

ref: Jacob solemnly promised God, “If you go with me and watch over me as I travel, and if you give me food and clothes
text: Ate Jekob yaa nye pɛka ayen, <<Ase Wen a dan bo ale mu a nya m zuk a ta va chaab ale m nyeerimu de po, a te mu ŋandiinta ate mi a dɛ ale a te mu kɔlima ate mi a jɔ,

ref: and bring me safely home again, you will be my God.
text: ate mi a ŋman pilim jam m kowa yenni ale nyiŋyɔgsa, Naawen ale chim mi Wen

ref: This rock will be your house, and I will give back to you a tenth of everything you give me.”
text: ate buntanni de ate mi a ti zaani ase zaŋi la, ale chim Wen yeri, ate fi ale a te mi jaabuuri meena la, m le te ku piika po ku bunyiwa.>>

ref: As Jacob continued on his way to the east,
text: Jekob yaa tom wa nyeerimu du a cheŋ ga paari nuru baai ale bo konni po la.

ref: he looked out in a field and saw a well where shepherds took their sheep for water. Three flocks of sheep were lying around the well, which was covered with a large rock.
text: Dula wa yaa nya vili ale bo talimu po, ate piisa beŋsa buta doa a moata vinni dii nyiŋla ŋa a nyu nyiam a nyini ka dula. Buntain dii ale jam lik vinni nɔnni la a jam zuak kama.

ref: Shepherds would roll the rock away when all their sheep had gathered there. Then after the sheep had been watered, the shepherds would roll the rock back over the mouth of the well.
text: Piisaŋa meena a dan tigsi dula, naapeerisaŋa nyiem bilim buntanni vinni nɔi a basi kama alege yaa ŋari nyiamu a te piisaŋa. Ba dan nueri ba ŋman pai buntanni a ŋman lik vinni nɔnni.

ref: Jacob asked the shepherds, “Where are you from?” “We're from Haran,” they answered.
text: Jekob yaa bek naapeerisaŋa ayen, <<M suata, nama nyini ka bɛ?>> Ba yaa tulisi ayen, <<Tama nyini ka Yaran.>>

ref: Then he asked, “Do you know Nahor's grandson Laban?” “Yes we do,” they replied.
text: Wa yaa weeni ba ayen, <<Nama seba Laban, Nayo ŋaaŋbiika?>> Ate ba tulisi wa ayen, <<Mm ti seba wa kama.>>

ref: “How is he?” Jacob asked. “He's fine,” they answered. “And here comes his daughter Rachel with the sheep.”
text: Ate Jekob yaa bek ba ayen, <<Wa ta nyiŋyɔgsa?>> Ba yaa tulisi wa ayen, <<Mm wa ta nyiŋyɔgsa kama. Be nya wa liewa Arekel ale la cheena ale piisa.>>

ref: Jacob told them, “Look, the sun is still high up in the sky, and it's too early to bring in the rest of the flocks. Water your sheep and take them back to the pasture.”
text: Jekob yaa weeni ayen, <<Ni nya kantueŋka a diem pagra kama. Ku an diem paari piisaŋa naakuui jam ka. Ni te ŋa nyiam abe ni ta ŋa a cheŋ wuuk po ate ŋa dɛ.>>

ref: But they replied, “We can't do that until they all get here, and the rock has been rolled away from the well.”
text: Ba yaa tulisi ayen, <<Tama an baga a nye dila nin ka piisaŋa meena a nin tigsi jigi yeŋ ate tama yaa bilim buntanni vinni nɔi a basi. Abe ti yaa te piisaŋa nyiam.>>

ref: While Jacob was still talking with the men, his cousin Rachel came up with her father's sheep.
text: Wa le boro a biisi ale ba la, Arekel yaa ta wa kowa piisaŋa a cheŋ ba jigi, dii nyiŋla wa jam ka naapeerik.

ref: When Jacob saw her and his uncle's sheep, he rolled the rock away and watered the sheep.
text: Jekob ale nya wa ŋesawa Laban liewa Arekel ale Laban piisaŋa la, wa yaa cheŋ ga bilim buntanni vinni nɔi a basi ale te wa ŋesawa piisaŋa nyiam.

ref: He then kissed Rachel and started crying because he was so happy.
text: Ate Jekob yaa ga gbaari Arekel ale kum nna yegayega.

ref: He told her that he was the son of her aunt Rebekah, and she ran and told her father about him.
text: Wa weeni Arekel ayen wa ale wa kowa a moata chaab kama ale ŋman chim Aribeka biik. Ate wa yaa chali a ga weeni wa kowa.

ref: As soon as Laban heard the news, he ran out to meet Jacob. He hugged and kissed him and brought him to his home, where Jacob told him everything that had happened.
text: Laban ale ga wom Jekob wa toa nidoabinni wari la, wa yaa nye nwuli a ga tu wa suik. Wa yaa gbaari wa ale ta wa a kuli wa yenni, ate Jekob a yaa weeni wa wieŋa de meena.

ref: Laban said, “You are my nephew, and you are like one of my own family.” Jacob Marries Leah and Rachel After Jacob had been there for a month,
text: Ate Laban yaa weeni wa ayen, <<Fi ka mi dek nyiŋlam ale ziim.>> Jekob ale kali ale wa a paari chi yeŋ la,

ref: Laban said to him, “You shouldn't have to work without pay, just because you are a relative of mine. What do you want me to give you?”
text: Laban a yaa weeni wa ayen, <<M le fu ale moata chaab la, a kan sak kama ayen fi a tom a te mu kinla nyiŋ ŋa? Weeni mu fi tutunaŋa ale magsi ayen ŋa chim diila.>>

ref: Laban had two daughters. Leah was older than Rachel, but her eyes didn't sparkle, while Rachel was beautiful and had a good figure.
text: Leya a jam ta ka nin baasima alege Arekel nyiŋka a jam boka jigi yeŋ ale nala mɛ.

ref: Since Jacob was in love with Rachel, he answered, “If you will let me marry Rachel, I'll work seven years for you.”
text: Jekob a jam ta yaalika ale ka Arekel ate wa yaa weeni ayen, <<Mi ale tom bena ŋayopɔi a te fu a magsi chaab ale fi lie baaŋka Arekel nyiŋ.>>

ref: Laban replied, “It's better for me to let you marry Rachel than for someone else to have her. So stay and work for me.”
text: Laban yaa weeni ayen, <<M dan pai wa a te fi a zunchoŋŋa nuru yoŋ. Kali dela ale mu.>>

ref: Jacob worked seven years for Laban, but the time seemed like only a few days, because he loved Rachel so much.
text: Dila nyiŋ Jekob a jam tom ka bena ŋayopɔi Arekel nyiŋ, alege ku poom a nye wa jigi nna se ka daa maga la, diila wa ale ta yaalika ale wa la nyiŋ.

ref: Jacob said to Laban, “The time is up, and I want to marry Rachel now!”
text: Ate Jekob yaa weeni Laban ayen, <<Pai m powa a te mu. M le dak diipo la a paari kama, ate mi a yaali ayen m faari wa.

ref: So Laban gave a big feast and invited all their neighbors.
text: Ate Laban a yaa ta nuruma meena ale bo jigini la a jam jigi yeŋ a nye tigi.

ref: But that evening he brought Leah to Jacob, who married her and spent the night with her.
text: Alege ku ale paari junɔinni la wa pai wa liewa Leya a te Jekob, ate wa duak ale wa.

ref: The next morning Jacob found out that he had married Leah, and he asked Laban, “Why did you do this to me? Didn't I work to get Rachel? Why did you trick me?”
text: Vanni ale veenti la, wa nya ka Leya ale bo ale wa. Ate Jekob yaa weeni Laban ayen, <<Ka boa ale la ate fi a pai nye mu? Ka Arekel nyiŋ ate mi tom a te, yaa daa di? Ka boan nyiŋ ate fi paasi mu?>>

ref: Laban replied, “In our country the older daughter must get married first.
text: Laban yaa tulisi ayen, <<Tama jigi ti kan pai baaŋka a dɛ niŋ a te yalika alege ate kpagni a kala.

ref: After you spend this week with Leah, you may also marry Rachel. But you will have to work for me another seven years.”
text: Dɛ liewa de pok fiaka bakoanni a nueri. Ate ti pai baaŋka a te fu, abe fi ŋman tom bena ŋayopɔi magsi wa nyiŋ.>>

ref: At the end of the week of celebration, Laban let Jacob marry Rachel, and he gave her his servant woman Bilhah. Jacob loved Rachel more than he did Leah, but he had to work another seven years for Laban.
text: Jekob yaa jam ŋman duak ale Arekel, ate wa jam ta yaalika ale Arekel a gaam Leya. Ate wa jam tom bena ŋayopɔi ŋman gum du a te Laban.

ref: The Lord knew that Jacob loved Rachel more than he did Leah, and so he gave children to Leah, but not to Rachel.
text: Naawen ale nya ayen wa ka yaalika ale Leya la, Wa yaa nye ate Leya a biak alege ate Arekel a chim kirik.

ref: Leah gave birth to a son and named him Reuben. Then she said, “The Lord has taken away my sorrow. Now my husband will love me more than he does Rachel.”
text: Leya a jam yik lɔgi a biak nidoabili. Wa te biika yuei ayen Aruben, diila wa weeni kama ayen, <<Ka Naawen ale nya m ninammu la nyiŋ, chekki m chorɔwa a yaa le ta yaalika ale mu.>>

ref: Rachel was very jealous of Leah for having children, and she said to Jacob, “I'll die if you don't give me some children!”
text: Arekel ale nya ayen wa kan biak bisa a te Jekob la, wa yaa jam a dɛ nyuri ale wa suoku. Ate wa yaa weeni Jekob ayen, <<Te mu bisa, dan kan daa di m le kpi.>>

ref: But Jacob became upset with Rachel and answered, “Don't blame me! I'm not God.”
text: Jekob sui yaa puuri ale wa ate wa weeni wa ayen, <<Mi ka Wen yaa, ate m nye ate fi a kan biagi?>>

ref: “Here, take my servant Bilhah,” Rachel told him. “Have children by her, and I'll let them be born on my knees to show that they are mine.”
text: Ate wa yaa weeni Jekob ayen, <<M dɔkliewa Bilya ale nna, goa ale wa ate wa biak bisa a te mu ate ku va wa nyiŋ ate mi mɛ a nya dɔkdem.>>

ref: and they had a son.
text: ate wa yik lɔgi a biak nidoabili a te wa.

ref: Rachel named him Dan, because she said, “God has answered my prayers. He judged in my favor and has given me a son.”
text: Ate Arekel a weeni ayen, <<Naawen a biisi te mu. Wa wom m juisim a te mu nidoabili.>> Dila nyiŋ wa te biika yuei ayen Dan.

ref: When Bilhah and Jacob had a second son,
text: Bilya Arekel dɔkliewa a ŋman yik lɔgi a biak nidoabili a nye buye a te Jekob.

ref: Rachel said, “I've struggled hard with my sister, and I've won!” So she named the boy Naphtali.
text: Ate Arekel yaa weeni ayen m nye ka gigelik nna yegayega ale m suoku, m be nya nakdeka wa zuk kama.>> Ate wa yaa te biika yuei ayen Nafitali.

ref: When Leah realized she could not have any more children, she let Jacob marry her servant Zilpah,
text: Leya ale ga nya ayen wa gebiri bisa biam la, wa yaa pai Zilpa ale ka wa dɔklie la a te Jekob ayen wa chim wa pok.

ref: “I'm really lucky,” Leah said, and she named the boy Gad.
text: Ate Leya yaa weeni ayen, <<Ka boan zumasuk ale nna!>> Ate wa te biika yuei ayen Gad.

ref: Leah exclaimed, “I'm happy now, and all the women will say how happy I am.” So she named him Asher. Love Flowers
text: Ate wa yaa weeni ayen, <<Nya m sui ale peenti a soa dii yaa! Nipooma ale wi mu ayen suipeentik nyɔnɔ.>> Ate wa te biika yuei ayen Asɛ.

ref: During the time of the wheat harvest, Reuben found some love flowers and took them to his mother Leah. Rachel asked Leah for some of them,
text: Ŋankpaantaŋa cheka vaaŋa po, ate Aruben yaa cheŋ tattaŋa po a nya ŋanva tii ale a nye ate nipok a baga a yik lɔgi la a jam te wa mawa Leya. Arekel yaa weeni Leya ayen wa nye ŋanvaataŋa ate wa biika a ta jam te wa la maga a te wa.

ref: but Leah said, “It's bad enough that you stole my husband! Now you want my son's love flowers too.” “All right,” Rachel answered. “Let me have the flowers, and you can sleep with Jacob tonight.”
text: Alege wa tulisi wa kama ayen, <<Fi ni vari m chorɔwa a nyini m jigi la an diem a magsi fu? Fi a yaali ayen fi ŋman pai ka m biika ale ta ŋanva tii a jam te mu la mɛ?>> Arekel yaa weeni ayen ku nala kama, <<Wa baga a goa ale fu kama yokku de ate ku magsi chaab ale fi biika ŋanvaataŋa.>>

ref: That evening when Jacob came in from the fields, Leah told him, “You're sleeping with me tonight. I hired you with my son's love flowers.” They slept together that night,
text: Junɔinni ate Jekob a nyini talim a cheena la, Leya yaa cheŋ ga tu wa suik. Wa yaa weeni wa ayen, <<Fi magsi fi goa ale mu kama dii nyiŋla m pai ka m biika ŋanvaata a tuni fi nyiŋ.>> Ate wa sum goa ale wa yok kula.

ref: and God answered Leah's prayers by giving her a fifth son.
text: Wen a jam wom Leya kama, ate wa yik lɔgi a biak nidoabili kaai ale a saari banu la a te Jekob.

ref: Leah shouted, “God has rewarded me for letting Jacob marry my servant,” and she named the boy Issachar.
text: Ate Leya a weeni ayen, <<Wen a te mu m tutuna kama, wa ale pai m dɔkliewa a te m chorɔwa la nyiŋ.>> Ate wa te biika yuei ayen Isaka.

ref: she exclaimed, “God has given me a wonderful gift, and my husband will praise me for giving him six sons.” So she named the boy Zebulun.
text: Ate Leya a weeni ayen, <<Wen a pai ka tiirim maŋ a te mu. M chorɔwa yaa ni pai zula a te mu, dii nyiŋla m biaka bisa bayuebi a te wa.>> Ate wa yaa te biika yuei ayen Zebulun.

ref: Later, Leah had a daughter and named her Dinah.
text: Ku yaa ŋman ga paari diipo ate wa biak lie ate wa te wa yuei ayen Daina.

ref: Finally, God remembered Rachel—he answered her prayer by giving her a son. “God has taken away my disgrace,” she said.
text: Wa jam yik lɔgi a biak nidoabili a yaa weeni ayen, <<Wen a pai m chivieŋa a nyini m zuk.>>

ref: “I'll name the boy Joseph, and I'll pray that the Lord will give me another son.” Jacob and Laban
text: Wa yaa te biika yuei ayen Josefi, a yaa weeni ayen, <<Naawen abe ŋman pai nidoabilik ka choa a gum du a te mu.>>

ref: After Joseph was born, Jacob said to Laban, “Release me from our agreement and let me return to my own country.
text: Ku ŋaaŋnyiŋ ate Arekel a biak Josefi la, Jekob yaa weeni Laban ayen, Basi mu suik ate m ŋman cheŋ m dek tɛŋka.

ref: You know how hard I've worked for you, so let me take my wives and children and leave.”
text: Te mu m pooma ale m bisaŋa, baai nyiŋ ate m tom a te fu la, ate m lɔ suik po. Fi seba mi le tom a soa dii a te fu la kama.>>

ref: But Laban told him, “If you really are my friend, stay on, and I'll pay whatever you ask. I'm sure the Lord has blessed me because of you.”
text: Wa ŋman weeni gum du ayen, <<Weeni fi tutunaŋa ale ka diila ate m tuni fu.>>

ref: Jacob answered: You've seen how hard I've worked for you, and you know how your flocks and herds have grown under my care.
text: Jekob yaa weeni wa ayen, <<Fi seba mi ale tom a soa dii a te fu la kama, ate m nya fi duŋsaŋa zuk ate si chim a soa diila kama.

ref: You didn't have much before I came, but the Lord has blessed everything I have ever done for you. Now it's time for me to start looking out for my own family.
text: Fi ale jam ta mag kaai alege ate mi jam la, yaa le piisi a soa diila, ate Naawen a kasim a niak fu juijui meena ate mi cheŋ la. Alege yɔgyɔgla de, ka din po ate mi ale baga a nye wari a te m dɔkdemma?>>

ref: “How much do you want me to pay you?” Laban asked. Then Jacob told him: I don't want you to pay me anything. Just do one thing, and I'll take care of your sheep and goats.
text: Wa yaa bek wa ayen, <<M te fu boa>> Jekob yaa tulisi wa ayen, <<Kan te mu jaaboa.>> Alege fi dan baga a nye wayeŋka de a te mu, m le kasim a nya fi piisaŋa zuk.

ref: Let me go through your flocks and herds and take the sheep and goats that are either spotted or speckled and the black lambs. That's all you need to give me.
text: Basi ate jinla m va fi duŋsaŋa po a yik pa sobta ale bua ŋaai nyiŋsaŋa ale ŋmirisi la, ate ŋa chim m tutuna.

ref: In the future you can easily find out if I've been honest. Just look and see if my animals are either spotted or speckled, or if the lambs are black. If they aren't, they've been stolen from you.
text: Ate chum po m wensieŋa ale weeni m le ka nuru waai choa la, ate diipo meena ate fi kpesi a nya ŋan baai ate fi pai a tuni mu la. Ate m dan ta buuk ate ku nyiŋka an ŋmirisi, yaase posuk ate ku an sobri ya, nya ku nna ase m zu a nyini ka fi jigi la>>

ref: Before the end of the day, Laban had separated his spotted and speckled animals and the black lambs from the others and had put his sons in charge of them.
text: Di danni dek ate wa yeeri buduktaŋa ale busaaŋa ate ŋa nyiŋsaŋa a ŋmirisi la ale pa sobtaŋa a te wa bisaŋa ayen ba a nya ŋa zuk.

ref: Then Laban made Jacob keep the rest of the sheep and goats at a distance of three days' journey.
text: Ate wa yaa poori wadek nyini Jekob jigi a cheŋ a ga bo jigi yoŋ a paari daa ŋata, alege ate Jekob a boro a nya Laban piisa ŋaai ale tali la zuk.

ref: Jacob cut branches from some poplar trees and from some almond and evergreen trees. He peeled off part of the bark and made the branches look spotted and speckled.
text: Jekob yaa pai niak tiib ale almond tiib ale dapeelita tiib naŋ yɔgsisa ate ba che la a po, a yaa pai daataŋa ate wa poa ate ŋa peenti la a dueni peelim.

ref: Then he put the branches where the sheep and goats would see them while they were drinking from the water trough. The goats mated there
text: Ate wa yaa pai tinaŋsaŋa ate wa poa la a dueni nyaataŋa nɔi, ate ŋa bo piisaŋa niŋ ase ŋa a nin jam ayen ŋa nyu nyiam, diipo ate pasaaŋa a yaali juelika la.

ref: in front of the branches, and their young were spotted and speckled.
text: Ate piisaŋa a jueli chaab tinaŋsaŋa ate wa poa la niŋ, ate ŋa yaa biak pabiik sii nyiŋsa ale sobri yaase si ŋmirisi.

ref: Some of the sheep that Jacob was keeping for Laban were already spotted. And when the others were ready to mate, he made sure that they faced in the direction of the spotted and black ones. In this way, Jacob built up a flock of sheep for himself and did not put them with the other sheep.
text: Jekob yaa gaari piisaŋa ale buo ŋaai ale paala la a nyini Laban duŋsaŋa po, wa yaa nye ate ŋa a nya buo ŋmiŋmiritaŋa ale pa sobtaŋa ate Laban a soa la, ate ŋa biak buduk ŋmiŋmirita ale pabiik sobta. Wa nye dela kama ate wa duŋsaŋa a piisi ate wa gaari si a nyini Laban duŋsaŋa po.

ref: When the stronger sheep were mating near the drinking place, Jacob made sure that the spotted branches were there.
text: Duŋ sii ale pagra la a dan boro a jueli chaab, Jekob nyiem ga pai ka tiisaŋa naŋsaŋa a dueni piisaŋa ale a de juijui la a bo duŋsaŋa niŋ. Ate ŋa jueli chaab a moata tiisaŋa naŋsaŋa,

ref: But he would not put out the branches when the weaker animals were mating. So Jacob got all of the healthy animals, and Laban got what was left.
text: alege duŋsaŋa a dan wobsa, wa kan pai a dueni. Ate duŋ sii ale wobsa la a cheŋ Laban jigi, ate sii ale pagra la a cheŋ Jekob jigi.

ref: Jacob soon became rich and successful. He owned many sheep, goats, camels, and donkeys, as well as a lot of slaves.
text: Nya kude nyiŋ nuruwa a jam jigsi ka nna yegayega, ate wa jam soa duŋsa ale dɔklieba ale nidoaba tomteerɔba ale laalaakomiba ale bonsa.

ref: Jacob heard that Laban's sons were complaining, “Jacob is now a rich man, and he got everything he owns from our father.”
text: Jekob yaa wom ayen Laban bisaŋa a weeni ayen, <<Jekob a tuesi ti kowa ale soa ŋan baai meena la kama, ate wa yaa nya jigsika de a nyini ti kowa ale jam soa ŋanti baai la.>>

ref: Jacob also noticed that Laban was not as friendly as he had been before.
text: Ate Jekob yaa jam miŋ ayen Laban nyeka ale wa a kan nye ase ku nyiem ale ka diila.

ref: One day the Lord said, “Jacob, go back to your relatives in the land of your ancestors, and I will be with you.”
text: Ate Naawen yaa weeni Jekob ayen, <<Ŋman cheŋ fi koma tɛŋka ale fi nuruma jigi, ate mi ale bo ale fu.

ref: Jacob sent for Rachel and Leah to meet him in the pasture where he kept his sheep,
text: Ate Jekob yaa tom wi Arekel ale Leya ayen ba jam talimu po juijui ate wa ta wa duŋsaŋa boro a nya la.

ref: and he told them: Your father isn't as friendly with me as he used to be, but the God my ancestors worshiped has been on my side.
text: Wa yaa weeni ba ayen, <<Mi nya kama ayen ni kowa nyeelaŋa ale mu a kan nye ase ku ale jam ka diila, alege m kowa Wenni a bo ale mu kama.

ref: years to buy your sheep and goats. During that time you kept changing my wages.
text: Ni seba kama ayen mi tom a te ni kowa ale ka m pagrim meena,

ref: and that he keeps cheating me by changing my wages time after time. But God has protected me.
text: alege ni kowa a dɛ gaam mu kama ale wa le yik m tutunaŋa a tagri a paari ku naŋtuok nɔi pi la. Alege Wen abe an te suik ayen wa nye mu wariya.

ref: When your father said the speckled sheep would be my wages, all of them were speckled. And when he said the spotted ones would be mine, all of them were spotted.
text: Wa dan weeni ayen, fi tutuna ale duŋ sii ale a biak ŋan ŋmiŋmirita la, duŋsaŋa mɛ a sum a biak ŋan ŋmiŋmirisa, ate wa dan weeni mɛ ayen fi tutunaŋa a ni ŋaai a ni biak ŋan sobta la, duŋsaŋa mɛ a nyiem sum a biak ŋan sobta.

ref: That's how God has taken sheep and goats from your father and given them to me.
text: Dila nyiŋ Wen a pai ka ni kowa duŋsaŋa a yaa te mi.

ref: Once, when the flocks were mating, I dreamed that all the rams were either spotted or speckled.
text: Va ŋaai po ate duŋsaŋa a jueli chaab la, mi jam nya ka goa daasima, ale ka duŋ doataŋa ale a jueli duŋ sii nyiŋsaŋa ale ŋmirisi la ale sii ale sob la.

ref: and he said, “Notice that all the rams are either spotted or speckled. I know everything Laban is doing to you,
text: Ate wa weeni mi ayen, zak zuk a nya ayen duŋ doataŋa ale a jueli duŋsaŋa la ka ŋan ŋmiŋmirita ale ŋan sobta, diila mi nya dii meena ate Laban a pai a nye fu la kama.

ref: and I am the God you worshiped at Bethel, when you poured olive oil on a rock and made a promise to me. Leave here at once and return to the land where you were born.”
text: Wen dii ate fi nya Betel la ale mi, juijui ate fi ti buntanni a zaani ase zaŋŋi la, a yaa kpiiri kpaamu a basi di zuk, a yaa pɛ te mi la. Yɔgyɔgla de, basi tɛŋka de abe fi cheŋ fidek tɛŋka.>>

ref: He treats us like foreigners and has even cheated us out of the bride price that should have been ours.
text: Wa an pai te a chim daa nyeemjandɔba? Wa an pai ti a da daa daka nyiini, alege wa maa dɛ ba ale tuni ti nyiŋ diila kama.

ref: So do whatever God tells you to do. Even the property God took from our father and gave to you really belongs to us and our children.
text: Chekki paaruŋ kuui meena ate Wen a tuesi a nyini ti kowa jigi la, ka tama ale ti bisaŋa ale soa. Dila nyiŋ nye Wen ale weeni fu diila.>>

ref: Then Jacob, his wives, and his children got on camels and left
text: Jekob yaa pai wa pooma ale wa bisaŋa a kali laalaakomiba zuk,

ref: northern Syria for the home of his father Isaac in Canaan. Jacob took along all his flocks, herds, and other property.
text: ate wa yaa choa wa duŋsaŋa meena a basi wa niŋ, ale wa jaab meena ate wa nya nyini Paddan Araam la, ayen wa cheŋ wa kowa Aizik jigi Keenan tɛŋka po.

ref: Before Rachel left, she stole the household idols while Laban was out shearing his sheep.
text: Laban ale nyini a ga boro a gebi wa piisaŋa kɔbtaŋa la, Arekel yaa zu wa kowa yenni wenaŋa.

ref: When Jacob crossed the Euphrates River and headed for the hill country of Gilead, he took with him everything he owned. Laban Catches Up with Jacob
text: Dila nyiŋ wa jam cheŋ ale ka jaab meena ate wa ta la, a ga lɔ Benni a jueli, a cheŋ tuaga Gilead guuku tɛŋka.

ref: Three days later Laban found out that Jacob had gone.
text: Ku daa ŋatawa danni ate ba weeni Laban ayen Jekob a cheŋ kama.

ref: So he took some of his relatives along and chased after Jacob for seven days, before catching up with him in the hill country of Gilead.
text: Wa yaa pai wa dɔkdemma a gum wa po, ate ba saŋ Jekob ŋaaŋ, ate ku daa ŋayopɔiwa danni, ba ga nya wa Gilead guuku tɛŋka po.

ref: But God appeared to Laban in a dream that night and warned, “Don't say a word to Jacob. Don't make a threat or a promise.”
text: Ate Laban Araam denɔwa yaa gɔdaasim yokku po, ate Wen a jam wa jigi a weeni wa ayen, <<Be seba ate fi kan weeni biem wari yaase nalim wari a te Jekob.>>

ref: Jacob had set up camp in the hill country of Gilead, when Laban and his relatives came and set up camp in another part of the hill country. Laban went to Jacob
text: Jekob yaa jam ti wa viuku ka Gilead guuku tɛŋka po, ate Laban ale wa dɔkdemma a jam gaam wa basi a yaa kali dula dek ale ba.

ref: and said: Look what you've done! You've tricked me and run off with my daughters like a kidnapper.
text: Ate Laban yaa weeni Jekob ayen, <<Ka boa ale la ate fi nye? Fi paasi mi kama, fi pai m liema nna se fi pai nyini ka kpaliŋ po la.

ref: Why did you sneak away without telling me? I would have given you a going-away party with singing and with music on tambourines and harps.
text: Boan nyiŋ ate fi paasi mu ale yiti a chali nna susugi ya? Boan nyiŋ ate fi an weeni sak mi, ate m basi fu suik ale masim ate ti pai kayaksa ale kpanta a yi yiila?

ref: You didn't even give me a chance to kiss my own grandchildren and daughters goodbye. That was really foolish.
text: Fi an te ate m puusi m ŋaaŋbisaŋa ale weeni m liema ayen ba cheŋ nalimnyiini. Fi tom ka betta.

ref: I could easily hurt you, but the God your father worshiped has warned me not to make any threats or promises.
text: M ta pagrim a baga a nye fu wari kama. Alege yokku po, fi kowa Wenni a weeni mu kama ayen. Be seba ate fi kan weeni biem wari yaase nalim wari a te Jekob.

ref: I can understand why you were eager to return to your father, but why did you have to steal my idols?
text: Yɔgyɔgla fi cheŋ kama dii nyiŋla fi a yaali kama ayen fi ŋman cheŋ fi kowa yenni. Alege ka boan nyiŋ ate fi maa zu m wenaŋa?>>

ref: Jacob answered, “I left secretly because I was afraid you would take your daughters from me by force.
text: Jekob yaa tulisi Laban ayen, <<Mi yɔgsa kama, dii nyiŋla m poli kama ayen fi le tuesi fi liema a nyini m jigi ale pagrim.

ref: If you find that any one of us has taken your idols, I'll have that person killed. Let your relatives be witnesses. Show me what belongs to you, and you can take it back.” Jacob did not realize that Rachel had stolen the household idols.
text: Alege fi dan nya ale waai a ta fi wenaŋa, kula nyɔnɔwa a kan vuugi. Ti nuruma niŋ be gisi nya ase fi jaab a bo ale mi dela jigini ya. Fi dan nya jaab ase dila la, be pai a ta cheŋ.>> Yɔgyɔgla Jekob a jam zɛ ayen ka Arekel ale zu wenaŋa.

ref: Laban searched the tents of Jacob, Leah, and the two servant women, but did not find the idols. Then he went to Rachel's tent.
text: Ate Laban yaa jo Jekob viuku po a ga taam jo Leya ale wa dɔkliema bayewa viitaŋa po, alege wa an nya jaaboa. Wa yaa nyini Leya viuku po, a ga jo Arekel viuku po.

ref: She had already hidden them in the cushion she used as a saddle and was sitting on it. Laban searched everywhere and did not find them.
text: Yɔgyɔgla Arekel yaa pai yenni wenaŋa a su ka wa laalaakomi dafieluku po a yaa kali ŋa zuk. Laban yaa gisi jaab meena ale bo viuku po la, alege wa an nya jaaboa.

ref: Rachel said, “Father, please don't be angry with me for not getting up; I'm having my period.” Laban kept on searching, but still did not find the idols.
text: Arekel yaa weeni wa kowa ayen, <<M nyɔnɔ, kan te fi sui a puuri ale mu m ni kan baga yiti a zaani fi niŋ la. M boka m chiak lɔka po.>> Ate wa gisi alege wa an baga a nya yenni wenaŋa.

ref: Jacob became very angry and said to Laban: What have I done wrong? Have I committed some crime? Is that why you hunted me down?
text: Jekob sui yaa puuri ate wa yaa ta chaab ale Laban. Wa yaa bek Laban ayen, <<M kaasuŋku ale boa? Ka boan biem ate m nye ate fi a vi mu?

ref: After searching through everything I have, did you find anything of yours? If so, put it here, where your relatives and mine can see it. Then we can decide what to do.
text: Yɔgyɔgla de ate fi gisi m ŋantaŋa meena po la, ka boan jaab ate fi nya ate ku chim fi yenni po jaaboa? Pai a ta jam dueni mi dɔkdemma ale fi dɔkdemma suŋsuŋ ate ba bo ti nɔi tiye ma buusa.

ref: If a wild animal killed one of your sheep or goats, I paid for it myself. In fact, you demanded the full price, whether the animal was killed during the day or at night.
text: Mi an ta fi duŋ a jam sak fu ayen gɔi duŋ ale yik ka. Jaab a dan bɛ ku a chim ka m zuk po wari. Mi dek ale a tuni. Dan poom ka yok yaase kantueŋ ate ba zu jaab, fi a basi kama ate maa tuni fu.

ref: If the fearsome God worshiped by Abraham and my father Isaac had not been on my side, you would have sent me away without a thing. But God saw my hard work, and he knew the trouble I was in, so he helped me. Then last night he told you how wrong you were. Jacob and Laban Make an Agreement
text: Fi a ti basi kama ate m cheŋ ale nisim kɔta ase m kowa ale Abrayam Wenni, waai ate Aizik a chali yɔgsum la, a nin kan bo ale mu. Alege Wen nya m nyiŋbiiri ale m le pai nisa a tom nam diila kama, ate wa jam a toariŋ fu yokku po.

ref: Laban said to Jacob, “Leah and Rachel are my daughters, and their children belong to me. All these sheep you are taking are really mine too. In fact, everything you have belongs to me. But there is nothing I can do to keep my daughters and their children.
text: Laban yaa tulisi Jekob ayen, <<Nipooma ka mi lieba, bisaŋa mɛ ka mi bisa ale nye ba, duŋsaŋa ka mi ale soa si. Jaab meena ate fi a nya la ka mi ale soa. Alege mi jinla ale pai m liema ale bisaŋa de ate ba biak la a nye ka boa?

ref: So I am ready to make an agreement with you, and we will pile up some large rocks here to remind us of the agreement.”
text: Jam yɔgyɔgla, basi ate ti nye nɔiyeŋ, mi ale fi, ate ku chim teerika wari a bo mi ale fi suŋsuŋ.

ref: After Jacob had set up a large rock,
text: Ate Jekob yaa pai buntain a feri zaani ase zaŋi la.

ref: he told his men to get some more rocks and pile them up next to it. Then Jacob and Laban ate a meal together beside the rocks.
text: Ate wa weeni wa nuruma ayen, <<Ba tigsi tana.>> Ate ba tigsi tanaŋa a pai ŋa a vi chaab zuk ate ŋa jueli ŋmai, ate ba kali dula a dɛ ŋandiinta.

ref: Laban named the pile of rocks Jegar Sahadutha. But Jacob named it Galeed.
text: Laban yaa wi jigini ayen Jegar Sayaduta ate Jekob a wi ku ayen Galeed.

ref: Laban said to Jacob, “This pile of rocks will remind us of our agreement.” That's why the place was named Galeed.
text: Laban yaa weeni ayen, <<Pimpitini de a za kama a chim teerika a te mi ale fi jinla.>> Dila ale soa ate ba a wi ku ayen Galeed.

ref: Laban also said, “This pile of rocks means that the Lord will watch us both while we are apart from each other.” So the place was also named Mizpah.
text: Ba ŋman jam a wi ku kama ayen Mizpa, diila wa jam weeni kama ayen, <<Naawen abe a nya ti zuk ase ti le zak ti basi chaab la.

ref: Then Laban said: If you mistreat my daughters or marry other women, I may not know about it, but remember, God is watching us!
text: Fi dan kan nye m liema a magsi, yaase fi ŋman faari nipok wa choa, waai a ka ti po, alege teeri ayen Wen a nya mi ale fi kama.

ref: Both this pile of rocks and this large rock have been set up between us as a reminder. I must never go past them to attack you, and you must never come past them to attack me.
text: Pimpitini de a chim ka teerika, ate buntanni dɛ a ni feri za ase zaŋi la, a chim ka teerika, fi abe kan gaam pimpitini ale tanni a jam mi jigi a daani mu, ate mi mɛ a kan gaam ŋa a jam a daani fu.

ref: My father Nahor, your grandfather Abraham, and their ancestors all worshiped the same God, and he will make sure that we each keep the agreement. Then Jacob made a promise in the name of the fearsome God his father Isaac had worshiped.
text: Abrayam ale Nayo ale ba kowa Wenni abe bo ti nɔi tiyema buusa.>> Jekob pɛ kama wa Wenni yuei nyiŋ, Wen dii ate Aizik a chali wa Yɔgsum la.

ref: Jacob killed an animal and offered it as a sacrifice there on the mountain, and he invited his men to eat with him. After the meal they spent the night on the mountain.
text: Wa yaa nye kaabka tɛŋka guuku zuk, a yaa wi wa nuruma ate ba jam dɛ ŋandiinta, ate ba le dɛ a nueri la, ba yaa goa dula yok kula.

ref: Early the next morning, Laban kissed his daughters and his grandchildren goodbye, then he left to go back home.
text: Varivientini ku saluik yokyok ate Laban yaa gbaari wa ŋaaŋbisaŋa ale wa liema a yaa niak ba. Alege ŋman cheŋ wa yenni.

ref: As Jacob was on his way back home, some of God's angels came and met him.
text: Jekob mɛ yaa lɔ wa suik po a cheŋ, ate Wen maarɔma yaa tu wa suik.

ref: When Jacob saw them, he said, “This is God's camp.” So he named the place Mahanaim.
text: Jekob ale ga nya ba la wa yaa weeni ayen, <<Ka Wen tigsuŋ ale nna.>> Ate wa te jigini yuei ayen Mayanaim.

ref: Jacob sent messengers on ahead to Esau, who lived in the land of Seir, also known as Edom.
text: Jekob yaa dɛ niŋ a tom nuruba ayen ba cheŋ ga nya wa suoku Iisɔ Seir tɛŋka, ale bo Edom tɛŋka po la.

ref: Jacob told them to say to Esau, “Master, I am your servant! I have lived with Laban all this time,
text: Wa dak ba kama ayen ba weeni ayen. M kpagi Iisɔ. Fi tomteerɔwa Jekob ayen, M bo ale ka Laban a jam paari yɔgyɔgla de.

ref: and now I own cattle, donkeys, and sheep, as well as many slaves. Master, I am sending these messengers in the hope that you will be kind to me.”
text: M ta ka niiga ale bonsa ale piisa ale bua ale nidoa tomteerɔba ale dɔklieba. Yɔgyɔgla, m be tom ka wari ayen ba jam weeni m nyɔnɔwa, ayen m le baga a nya ŋmanteka fi jigi ya.

ref: When the messengers returned, they told Jacob, “We went to your brother Esau, and now he is heading this way with
text: Tintomdɔma de ale ŋman cheŋ Jekob jigi la, ba weeni wa kama ayen. Ti cheŋ fi suoku Iisɔ jigi kama ate wa cheena ale nuruba kobsinaansi ayen ba tu fu suik.

ref: Then Jacob prayed: You, Lord, are the God who was worshiped by my grandfather Abraham and by my father Isaac. You told me to return home to my family, and you promised to be with me and make me successful.
text: Ate Jekob yaa puusi ayen, <<M kowa Abrayam ale m kowa Aizik Wen yaa, Naawen yaa, waai ale weeni mi ayen, <Ŋman cheŋ fi tɛŋka ale fi nuruma jigi, ate m le basi ate fi jigsi.>

ref: Please rescue me from my brother. I am afraid he will come and attack not only me, but my wives and children as well.
text: Mi a puusi kama ayen fi vari mu basi, diila m yɔgsa kama ale m suoku Iisɔ, wa zaan jam nye kpaliŋ ale mu ale m bisaŋa ale ba ma ma.

ref: But you have promised that I would be a success and that someday it will be as hard to count my descendants as it is to count the grains of sand along the seashore.
text: Alege fi weeni kama ayen, <Fi le sum nye ate m jigsi, ate m ŋaaŋsaŋa ale nye ase nyakpiemu tintain busuŋku la, ate waai a kan baga a chiini.> >>

ref: After Jacob had spent the night there, he chose some animals as gifts for Esau:
text: Wa bo ka dula yokku meena, ate wa le ta ŋan baai la, wa yaa yeeri ŋa po ate ku chim tiirim a ta cheŋ ga te wa suoku Iisɔ.

ref: And Jacob told them to be sure to say that he was right behind them. Jacob hoped the gifts would make Esau friendly, so Esau would be glad to see him when they met.
text: Abe ni seba ate ni weeni ayen, <<Fi tomteerɔwa Jekob a bo ŋaaŋ a cheena.>> Diila wa poli kama ayen, <<M le pai tiiritaŋa a dɛ niŋ a tom te wa ate ku nye ate ti niak chaab, la ate ti yaa dan nya chaab, wa yaa tuesi mu.>>

ref: Jacob said, “I have seen God face to face, and I am still alive.” So he named the place Peniel.
text: Ate Jekob yaa te jigini yuei ayen. Peniel, a weeni ayen, <<Ku le ka diila ale nna, m le Wen nya chaab kama alege ate jaab an nye m nyuvunni.

ref: Then he said to the servant in charge of the first herd, “When Esau meets you, he will ask whose servant you are. He will want to know where you are going and who owns those animals in front of you.
text: Wa yaa weeni waai le dɛ niŋ la ayen, <<Ase m suoku Iisɔ a dan tu fu suik a bek fu ayen, <Ka wana ale soa fi, fi be a cheŋ ka bɛ, ka wana ale soa duŋsaŋa de meena ale bo fi niŋ la?>

ref: So tell him, ‘They belong to your servant Jacob, who is coming this way. He is sending them as a gift to his master Esau.’ ”
text: Fi yaa weeni wa ayen, <Ka fi tomteerɔwa Jekob ale soa. Ŋa ka tiirim ate wa pai tom ayen m ta jam te m nyɔnɔwa Iisɔ, wa abe bo ka ti ŋaaŋ a cheena.> >>

ref: Jacob also told the men in charge of the second and third herds and those who followed to say the same thing when they met Esau.
text: Wa ŋman weeni waai ale pai a saŋ buyewa la, ale waai ale a saari butawa, ale baai meena ale a vi duŋsaŋa ba beŋ beŋka la. Ate wa weeni ba ayen ni magsi ni weeni Iisɔ ka wa yeŋ ase nama nin tu wa suik.

ref: Jacob's men took the gifts on ahead of him, but he spent the night in camp. Jacob's Name Is Changed to Israel
text: Dila nyiŋ Jekob tiiritaŋa a jam dɛ ka wa niŋ, alege yokku wadek a jam wari a bo viuku po.

ref: Jacob got up in the middle of the night and took his wives, his eleven children, and everything he owned across to the other side of the Jabbok River for safety.
text: Wa ale ta ba a lɔ beli naŋka a jueli nueri la, wa jam nya kama ate wa ŋantaŋa meena mɛ a lɔ juel.

ref: Afterwards, Jacob went back and spent the rest of the night alone. A man came and fought with Jacob until just before daybreak.
text: Ate ku yaa jam tali Jekob dek wa nyiini, ate nuru yaa jam a jɛ ale wa ate vanni a ga veenti.

ref: When the man saw that he could not win, he struck Jacob on the hip and threw it out of joint.
text: Nuruwa ale ga nya ayen wa an bag wa la, wa yaa tiri Jekob kɔsukku ate wa kɔsukku a choa diipo ate ba boro a jɛ la.

ref: They kept on wrestling until the man said, “Let go of me! It's almost daylight.” “You can't go until you bless me,” Jacob replied.
text: Ate nuruwa yaa weeni wa ayen. <<Basi mu ate m cheŋ, ale ka vari ale a veenti mu>> Alege Jekob a tulisi wa ayen, <<Fi dan kan niak mu, m kan basi fu ate fi a cheŋŋi.>>

ref: Then the man asked, “What is your name?” “Jacob,” he answered.
text: Nuruwa yaa bek wa ayen, <<Fi yuei ale boa?>> Wa yaa tulisi ayen, <<Jekob.>>

ref: The man said, “From now on, your name will no longer be Jacob. You will be called Israel, because you have wrestled with God and with men, and you have won.”
text: Nuruwa yaa ayen, <<Fi yuenni a kan ŋman chim Jekob, alege ka Izirali, dii nyiŋla fi jɛ ale ka Wen ale nuruba a nya nakdeka.>>

ref: Jacob said, “Now tell me your name.” “Don't you know who I am?” he asked. And he blessed Jacob.
text: Jekob yaa weeni wa ayen, <<Mi a saalim fu, wi fu yuei a sak mu.>> Alege wa tulisi wa ayen, <<Ka boan nyiŋ ate fi a bek mi yuei ya?>> Ate wa yaa niak wa dula.

ref: The sun was coming up as Jacob was leaving Peniel. He was limping because he had been struck on the hip,
text: Wenbinni yaa pusi jueli a bo wa zuk ase wa le va Peniel a taam la, ate wa kɔsukku nyiŋ wa yaa cheŋ a duksi.

ref: and the muscle on his hip joint had been injured. That's why even today the people of Israel don't eat the hip muscle of any animal.
text: Dila nyiŋ a jam paari jinla Izirali demma a kan ŋɔbi lam buui ale ka jaab kɔsuk la, diila Jekob kɔsukku ate wa jam tiri la nyiŋ.

ref: As Jacob was on his way back home, some of God's angels came and met him.
text: Jekob mɛ yaa lɔ wa suik po a cheŋ, ate Wen maarɔma yaa tu wa suik.

ref: When Jacob saw them, he said, “This is God's camp.” So he named the place Mahanaim.
text: Jekob ale ga nya ba la wa yaa weeni ayen, <<Ka Wen tigsuŋ ale nna.>> Ate wa te jigini yuei ayen Mayanaim.

ref: Jacob sent messengers on ahead to Esau, who lived in the land of Seir, also known as Edom.
text: Jekob yaa dɛ niŋ a tom nuruba ayen ba cheŋ ga nya wa suoku Iisɔ Seir tɛŋka, ale bo Edom tɛŋka po la.

ref: Jacob told them to say to Esau, “Master, I am your servant! I have lived with Laban all this time,
text: Wa dak ba kama ayen ba weeni ayen. M kpagi Iisɔ. Fi tomteerɔwa Jekob ayen, M bo ale ka Laban a jam paari yɔgyɔgla de.

ref: and now I own cattle, donkeys, and sheep, as well as many slaves. Master, I am sending these messengers in the hope that you will be kind to me.”
text: M ta ka niiga ale bonsa ale piisa ale bua ale nidoa tomteerɔba ale dɔklieba. Yɔgyɔgla, m be tom ka wari ayen ba jam weeni m nyɔnɔwa, ayen m le baga a nya ŋmanteka fi jigi ya.

ref: When the messengers returned, they told Jacob, “We went to your brother Esau, and now he is heading this way with
