ref: Jacob himself walked in front of them all, bowing to the ground seven times as he came near his brother.
text: Wadek yaa ga dɛ niŋ a koori tɛŋ naŋtuok nɔi buyopɔi ase wa ale moata wa suoku la.

ref: But Esau ran toward Jacob and hugged and kissed him. Then the two brothers started crying.
text: Alege Iisɔ a chali kama a ga tu wa suik a yaa gbaari wa ale pai nisa vi wa ŋinni zuk a yaa puusi wa. Ate ba kum.

ref: When Esau noticed the women and children he asked, “Whose children are these?” Jacob answered, “These are the children the Lord has been kind enough to give to me, your servant.”
text: Iisɔ yaa zuk nya nipooma ale bisaŋa. Wa yaa bek ayen, <<Baai ale bo ale fu la a nyini ka bɛ?>> Jekob yaa tulisi wa ayen, <<Ba ka fi tomteerɔwa bisa ate Naawen a pai te wa.>>

ref: Then the two servant women and their children came and bowed down to Esau.
text: Ate dɔkliema ale ba bisaŋa ale moata la, ba yaa koori tɛŋ.

ref: Next, Leah and her children came and bowed down; finally, Joseph and Rachel also came and bowed down.
text: Ate Leya ale wa bisaŋa a jam koori tɛŋ. Ate Josefi ale Arekel a wari ŋaaŋ ate ba mɛ a koori tɛŋ.

ref: Esau asked Jacob, “Why did you send those herds I met along the road?” “Master,” Jacob answered, “I sent them so you would be friendly to me.”
text: Iisɔ yaa bek ayen, <<Alege duŋsaŋa de meena ate ni choa cheena la kiri ale boa?>> Wa yaa tulisi ayen, <<M nyɔnɔ, mi a yaali kama ayen fi ŋman te mu.>>

ref: “But, brother, I already have plenty,” Esau replied. “Keep them for yourself.”
text: Alege Iisɔ yaa weeni wa ayen, <<M suowa tanla fi ŋantaŋa, mi mɛ dek a ta ŋanta kama nna yegayega.

ref: “No!” Jacob said. “Please accept them as a sign of your friendship for me. When you welcomed me and I saw your face, it was like seeing the face of God.
text: Jekob yaa weeni ayen, <<Mi kan siagi, ase fi dan sum a ta ŋmanteka ale mu, be tuesi m tiirimu a nyini m jigi. M le nya fi nimbidiaka la a nye ase ka Wen nimbidiak ate m nya la, fi ŋmanteka nyiŋ ate yɔgyɔgla de fi a tuesi mi la.

ref: Please accept these as gifts I brought to you. God has been good to me, and I have everything I need.” Jacob kept insisting until Esau agreed.
text: Mi a saalim fu kama ayen fi tuesi tiirimu de ate m ta jam ayen m te fu la, diila Wen a nala ka nna yegayega a te mi ate m ta jaab meena ate mi a silim la.>> Jekob ale kasim jam va wa la, Iisɔ yaa tuesi.

ref: “Let's get ready to travel,” Esau said. “I'll go along with you.”
text: Ate Iisɔ yaa weeni ayen, <<Basi ate ti lɔ suik po ate m bani fu ate ti cheŋ.>>

ref: But Jacob answered, “Master, you know traveling is hard on children, and I have to look after the sheep and goats that are nursing their young. If my animals travel too much in one day, they will all die.
text: Alege Jekob a weeni wa ayen, <<M nyɔnɔ, fi seba kama ayen bisaŋa a pɔsima kama, ate m magsi mi a nya pa bisaŋa ale niiŋa ale a ŋoosi la zuk. Fi daa yiak ŋa nwuli nwuli dai yeŋ ate duŋsaŋa meena ale kpi.

ref: Why don't you go on ahead and let me travel along slowly with the children, the herds, and the flocks. We can meet again in the country of Edom.”
text: Dila nyiŋ be dɛ niŋ a cheŋ m nyɔnɔ, ate mi fi tomteerɔwa a vi duŋsaŋa ŋaaŋ maga maga ale bisaŋa ate ti jam paari fu Seir tɛŋka, m dek nyɔnɔ.

ref: Esau replied, “Let me leave some of my men with you.” “You don't have to do that,” Jacob answered. “I am happy, simply knowing that you are friendly to me.”
text: Iisɔ yaa weeni ayen, <<Te m basi m nidoama gela ate ba bo ale fu.>> Jekob yaa bek wa ayen, <<Ka boa ate fi a namsi fi dega? M nyɔnɔ, fidek tin basi ate nya fi ŋmanteka.>>

ref: But Jacob went to Succoth, where he built a house for himself and set up shelters for his animals. That's why the place is called Succoth. Jacob Arrives at Shechem
text: Alege Iisɔ a jam cheŋ ka Sukkot, wa yaa sɛ yeri dula a te wadek ale nye goom jigi a te wa duŋsaŋa. Dila ale soa ate ba a wi jigini ayen Sukkot.

ref: After leaving northern Syria, Jacob arrived safely at Shechem in Canaan and set up camp outside the city.
text: Jekob ale nyini Paddan Araam la, wa ga paari Sikiim tɛŋka ale bo Keenan po la ka nalimnyiini, ate wa yaa kali tɛŋ kpieŋka po jigi.

ref: The land where he camped was owned by the descendants of Hamor, the father of Shechem. So Jacob paid them
text: Jekob a jam da tɛŋka ka kpana kook a nyini, Sikiim biika Ayaamo bisaŋa jigi, tɛŋ kala zuk ate wa jam ti wa viuku.

ref: then he set up his tents and built an altar there to honor the God of Israel.
text: Dula ate wa jam nye kuŋkuŋ a wi ku ayen El Eloye Izirali.

ref: Dinah, the daughter of Jacob and Leah, went to visit some of the women who lived nearby.
text: Yɔgyɔgla Daina, lie waai ate Leya a jam biak a te Jekob la, yaa jam nyini peelim a cheŋ tɛŋka nipooma jigi ayen wa puusi ba.

ref: She was seen by Hamor's son Shechem, the leader of the Hivites, and he grabbed her and raped her.
text: Ayaamo biika Sikiim Yivi denɔwa, waai ale soa kauk kula a nya la, ale ga nya wa la, wa yaa yik wa, a fɛ goa ale wa.

ref: But Shechem was attracted to Dinah, so he told her how much he loved her.
text: Wa suinum yaa jam doa Jekob liewa Daina zuk, ate wa ta yaalika ale nipokbinni, ate wa biisi bi masik a te wa.

ref: Shechem even asked his father to arrange for him to marry her.
text: Ate Sikiim a yaa weeni wa kowa Ayaamo ayen, <<Yaali nipokbinni nna a te mu ate wa chim m pok.>>

ref: Meanwhile, Jacob heard what had happened. But his sons were out in the fields with the cattle, so he did not do anything at the time.
text: Jekob ale ga wom ayen ba taŋ wa liewa la, ale wa bisaŋa a jam ta ka duŋsa a bo vaasaŋa po, ate wa yaa jam sina ate ba ga jam yeri.

ref: just as Jacob's sons were coming in from work. When they learned that their sister had been raped, they became furiously angry, because nothing is more disgraceful than rape, and it must not be tolerated.
text: Yɔgyɔgla Jekob bisaŋa ale ga nyini vaasaŋa po a jam la, ba yaa wom dii ale nye la. Ate ku yaa jam cha ba ate ba sue yaa jaa puuri, dii nyiŋla Sikiim a nye ka chivie wari Izirali po, wa ale goa ale Jekob liewa la, wa dii ale kan magsi ayen di nye.

ref: Hamor said to Jacob and his sons: My son Shechem really loves Dinah. Please let him marry her.
text: Alege Ayaamo a jam weeni ba kama ayen, <<M biika Sikiim suinum ale doa fi liewa zuk. Mi a saalim fu ayen fi pai wa a te wa ate wa chim wa pok.

ref: Why don't you start letting your families marry into our families and ours marry into yours?
text: Ni te ate ti a faari chaab lieba. Ni pai ni liema a te ti, abe tama mɛ a pai ti liema a te ni.

ref: You can share this land with us. Move freely about until you find the property you want; then buy it and settle down here.
text: Ni baga a kali ale ti kama. Tɛŋka ale la a lak doa te ni. Ni kali ka po, ni a nye geruŋ ka po, abe ni ta ŋanta ka po.

ref: Shechem added, “Do this favor for me, and I'll give whatever you want.
text: Ate Sikiim yaa weeni Daina kowa ale wa tuama ayen, <<Ni ŋman te mu, ate ni ale a yaali jaabuuri meena la, m le te ni.

ref: Jacob's sons wanted to get even with Shechem and his father because of what had happened to their sister.
text: Ba le taŋ ba toawa Daina la nyiŋ, Jekob bisaŋa a jam tulisi Ayaamo ale ka Sikiim ko la ale ka pimpaasima ase ba le a biisi ale wa.

ref: So they tricked them by saying: You're not circumcised! It would be a disgrace for us to let you marry Dinah now.
text: Ba jam weeni wa kama ayen, <<Tama kan nye wa dila choa. Tama a kan pai ti toa a te nuru waai ate ba an gebi wa yoari ya. Ku le chim ka chivie a te ti.

ref: But we will let you marry her, if you and the other men in your tribe agree to be circumcised.
text: Tama le pai ti ale a siak wa dii zuk la a dueni ni niŋ. Di be ka wa yeŋ dekki. Ase ni dan siak ayen ni gebi ni nidoama meena yoaŋa ate ni nye ase tama la.

ref: Then your families can marry into ours, and ours can marry into yours, and we can live together like one nation.
text: Dila tɔgni, ti le pai ti liema a te, abe ti tuesi nama mɛ liema a tara. Ate ti le kali ale ni, ate ti chim nuru yeŋsa ale chaab.

ref: But if you don't agree to be circumcised, we'll take Dinah and leave this place.
text: Alege ni dan kan siak ayen ni gebi ni yoaŋa, ti le ta ti toawa a cheŋ.>>

ref: Shechem was the most respected person in his family, and he was so in love with Dinah that he hurried off to get everything done.
text: Duorɔwa, waai le ta zula a gaam ba meena wa ko yenni po la, an poom jam beni ale ba ale weeni wa diila. Dii nyiŋla wa jam ta suipeentik ale Jekob liewa kama.

ref: The two men met with the other leaders of their city and told them:
text: Ate Ayaamo ale wa biika a jam cheŋ tɛŋka nansuiŋku ayen ba biisi a sak tɛŋka nidoama.

ref: These people really are friendly. Why not let them move freely about until they find the property they want? There's enough land here for them and for us. Then our families can marry into theirs, and theirs can marry into ours.
text: Ba yaa weeni ayen, <<Nuruma de ka ti doaba, ni te ti te ba suik ate ba kali ti tɛŋka po a gerim. Tɛŋka doa kama nna yegayega a baga a paari ba. Ti baga a faari ba lieba kama ate ba mɛ a baga a faari tama mɛ lieba.

ref: We have to do only one thing before they will agree to stay here and become one nation with us. Our men will have to be circumcised just like theirs.
text: Alege nuruma ale siak a kali ale ti ase ka tama a nin nye ba le a weeni dii ayen, ti nidoama a gebi ba yoaŋa ase ba ale gebi diila.

ref: Just think! We'll get their property, as well as their flocks and herds. All we have to do is to agree, and they will live here with us.
text: Ba le a wuuk ŋan baai la ale ba paaruŋku ale ba duŋsaŋa meena, a kan chim daa tama ŋanta? Dila nyiŋ ni te ti siak te ba, ate ba kali ale ti.>>

ref: Three days later the men who had been circumcised were still weak from pain. So Simeon and Levi, two of Dinah's brothers, attacked with their swords and killed every man in the town,
text: Ba le gebi ba yoaŋa ate ku paari daa ŋata, ate ba niraŋa a dom la, ate Jekob bisa baye, ba jam ka Simeyon ale Livai, ale ka Daina toaba la, yaa jam pai gebik woŋsa, a ga yik tɛŋ kaai ale kan seba kpaliŋ nɔi po la nidoama a ko nidoama meena.

ref: including Hamor and Shechem. Then they took Dinah and left.
text: Ba jam maa ko Ayaamo ale wa biika Sikiim alege yaa ta Daina a nyini Sikiim yenni po a cheŋ.

ref: Jacob's other sons came and took everything they wanted. All this was done because of the horrible thing that had happened to their sister.
text: Jekob bisaŋa yaa jam kpiema zuk a kɔlim ba ŋantaŋa, ale juijui ate ba taŋ ba toawa la.

ref: They took sheep, goats, donkeys, and everything else that was in the town or the countryside.
text: Ba jam choa ba piisa ale ba niiga ale ba bonsa ale ba ŋanta ale bo diina po ale peelim la kama.

ref: After taking everything of value from the houses, they dragged away the wives and children of their victims.
text: Ba jam kɔlim ba paaruŋku ale ba nipooba ale ba bisa meena ale ba yieŋa po ŋanta meena a jam ta cheŋ kama.

ref: Jacob said to Simeon and Levi, “Look what you've done! Now I'm in real trouble with the Canaanites and Perizzites who live around here. There aren't many of us, and if they attack, they'll kill everyone in my household.”
text: Ate Jekob yaa weeni Simeyon ale Livai ayen, <<Ni ta daanika a jam te mu diila ni nye kama ate m chim ja nyuŋtik ate Keenan demma ale Perizzi demma ale nuru baai ale bo tɛŋka de la. Tama a fiisa kama, ate ba dan gum chaab ayen ba nye kpaliŋ ale mi ale m yenni demma, ba a kaasi ti kama.>>

ref: They answered, “Was it right to let our own sister be treated that way?”
text: Alege ba tulisi wa ayen, <<Ti be basi ate wa nye ti toawa ase waai ale pai wadek a te nidoaba a tuesi ŋanta la ya?>>

ref: God told Jacob, “Return to Bethel, where I appeared to you when you were running from your brother Esau. Make your home there and build an altar for me.”
text: Ate Wen yaa weeni Jekob ayen, <<Yiti cheŋ ga kali Betel, abe fi sɛ kuŋkuŋ dula a te Wen dii ale jam fi jigi diipo ate fi jam chali a nyini fi suoku Iisɔ jigi la.>>

ref: Jacob said to his family and to everyone else who was traveling with him: Get rid of your foreign gods! Then make yourselves acceptable to worship God and put on clean clothes.
text: Ate Jekob yaa weeni wa yenni demma ale baai meena ale bo ale wa la ayen, <<Ni yuk ni wen chaŋsaŋa ale bo ale ni la a basi, abe ni so nidek, abe ni tagri ni gattaŋa.

ref: Afterwards, we'll go to Bethel. I will build an altar there for God, who answered my prayers when I was in trouble and who has always been at my side.
text: Abe ni jam ate ti cheŋ jueli Betel, ate m sɛ kuŋkuŋku a te Wen dii ale tulisi mu diipo ate m bo daanika po la ale kasim a cheŋ ale mu juijui meena ate mi a cheŋ la.>>

ref: So everyone gave Jacob their idols and their earrings, and he buried them under the oak tree near Shechem.
text: Ate ba yaa pai ba wen chaŋsaŋa meena a te Jekob ale ba tukpanaŋa ale bo ba tueŋa po la, ate Jekob yaa pai ŋa a gu Sikiim tiimu tɛŋ.

ref: While Jacob and his family were traveling through Canaan, God terrified the people in the towns so much that no one dared bother them.
text: Ate ba yaa lɔ suik po ate Wen yɔgsum a jam tɛŋ sii ale moata la, ate waai an jam a vi ba.

ref: Jacob built an altar there and called it “God of Bethel,” because that was the place where God had appeared to him when he was running from Esau.
text: Ate wa sɛ kuŋkuŋ dula, ate wa te jigini yuei ayen El Betel, diila ka dula ate Wen a jam yeeri wadek a sak wa diipo ate wa jam chali a nyini wa suoku jigi la.

ref: While they were there, Rebekah's personal servant Deborah died. They buried her under an oak tree and called it “Weeping Oak.” God Blesses Jacob at Bethel
text: Yɔgyɔgla Debora ale ka Aribeka tomteerɔ la, yaa jam kpi ate ba gu wa Betel tiib tɛŋ ate ba a wi jigini yuei ayen Allon Bakut.

ref: After Jacob came back to the land of Canaan, God appeared to him again. This time he gave Jacob a new name and blessed him by saying: I am God All-Powerful, and from now on your name will be Israel instead of Jacob. You will have many children. Your descendants will become nations, and some of the men in your family will even be kings.
text: Ate Wen yaa weeni wa ayen, <<Mi le Wen Dii Ale A Niak La. Be biak a piisi. Fi ka tɛŋ kpieŋ ale tɛŋsa ate nuruba a sueri si po, ate nalima ale nyini fi nyiŋ po a nyini.

ref: I will give you the land that I promised Abraham and Isaac, and it will belong to your family forever.
text: Tɛŋka ate m jam pai te Abrayam ale Aizik la, m le pai a te fi mɛ, ate m le pai te fi ŋaaŋbisa baai ale za ba nyini fi ŋaaŋnyiŋ a jam la.>>

ref: Jacob set up a large rock, so that he would remember what had happened there. Then he poured wine and olive oil on the rock to show that it was dedicated to God,
text: Ate juijui ate Wen a biisi ale Jekob la wa yaa feri buntain ase zaŋi la a zaani, ate wa yaa kpiiri nyiam tiirim di zuk. Wa jam kpiiri kpaam mɛ di zuk.

ref: and he named the place Bethel. Benjamin Is Born
text: Jekob a wi juijui ate Wen a biisi ale wa la ayen Betel.

ref: Jacob and his family had left Bethel and were still a long way from Ephrath, when the time came for Rachel's baby to be born.
text: Ate ba cheŋ nyini Betel. Ba ale jam yalima maga a nyini Efrat la, Arekel yaa jam boro a yaa yen wa biak ate ku jam tua te wa.

ref: She was having a rough time, but the woman who was helping her said, “Don't worry! It's a boy.”
text: Ate ku ale jam tua wa biamu po la, waai ale jam boro yik wa la yaa weeni wa ayen, <<Kan chali yɔgsummoa, fi ale nya ka nidoabili.>>

ref: Rachel was at the point of death, and right before dying, she said, “I'll name him Benoni.” But Jacob called him Benjamin.
text: Wa le vuusi kpeglim la, wa yaa te biika yuei ayen Ben Oni, alege yaa kpi. Alege wa kowa a te wa yuei ayen Benjamin.

ref: Rachel was buried beside the road to Ephrath, which is also called Bethlehem.
text: Ate Arekel a kpi ate ba gu wa ase ba ale jam bo Efrat ale ka Betiliyem suik po la.

ref: Jacob set up a tombstone over her grave, and it is still there.
text: Ate Jekob a feri buntain ase zaŋi la wa boosuku zuk, a jam paari jinla tanni a za kama a teeri Arekel boosuku wari.

ref: Jacob, also known as Israel, traveled to the south of Eder Tower, where he set up camp.
text: Izirali a ŋman cheŋ wa niŋ nyiŋ a gaam Migdal Eder a ga ti wa viuku.

ref: During their time there, Jacob's oldest son Reuben slept with Bilhah, who was one of Jacob's other wives. And Jacob found out about it. Jacob's Twelve Sons
text: Izirali ale jam bo tɛŋ kala po la, Aruben yaa cheŋ a ga bobi Bilya ale ka wa kowa pok la a goa ale wa, ate Izirali a jam wom. Jekob a jam ta ka nidoabilisa pi ale baye.

ref: Jacob went to his father Isaac at Hebron, also called Mamre or Kiriath-Arba, where Isaac's father Abraham had lived as a foreigner.
text: Jekob yaa jam yiti wa kowa Aizik jigi a ga bo Mamre tɛŋka po, a moata Kiriat Arba, Yebron ale la, juijui ate Abrayam ale Aizik a jam kali la.

ref: Esau, also known as Edom, had many descendants.
text: Iisɔ buuni ale nna, Edom ale la.

ref: He married three Canaanite women: The first was Adah, the daughter of Elon the Hittite; the second was Oholibamah, the daughter of Anah and the granddaughter of Zibeon the Hivite;
text: Iisɔ jam faari wa pooma nyini ka Keenan nipooma po. Ba jam le Ada, Elon Yit denɔwa lie ale Oyolibama Anaya liewa wa jam ka Zibeon Yivi denɔwa ŋaaŋ.

ref: the third was Basemath, who was Ishmael's daughter and Nebaioth's sister.
text: Wanyi mɛ jam le Basemat, Ismael lie ale chim Nebaiot toa.

ref: Esau and his three wives had five sons while in Canaan. Adah's son was Eliphaz; Basemath's son was Reuel; Oholibamah's three sons were Jeush, Jalam, and Korah.
text: ate Oyolibama a biak Jeus ale Jalam ale Koora. Iisɔ nidoabilisaŋa a jam le nna ate wa biak Keenan tɛŋka po.

ref: Esau took his children and wives, his relatives and servants, his animals and possessions he had acquired while in Canaan, and moved far from Jacob.
text: Iisɔ yaa pai wa pooma ale wa nidoabilisaŋa ale wa liema ale duŋ yoŋsa meena ale wa kɔlima meena ate wa paari Keenan la, a yaa torisi cheŋ jigi yoŋ ale ge wa suoku Jekob.

ref: He did this because the land was too crowded and could not support him and his brother with their flocks and herds.
text: Ba ŋantaŋa ate ba paari la a jam zuak ka yega ale ba ale bo ale chaab. Tɛŋ kaai ate ba jam boro la an jam baga a maari ba nɔi bayewa, ba wuuni ŋantaŋa nyiŋ.

ref: That's why Esau made his home in the hill country of Seir.
text: Dila nyiŋ Iisɔ, Edom ale la a jam kali moata Seir ale bo guutaŋa la.

ref: Esau and Adah's oldest son was Eliphaz, and the clans that descended from him were Teman, Omar, Zepho, Kenaz,
text: Nalima ŋaai ale jam bo Iisɔ buuni po la ale nna. Iisɔ bikpagni Elifaz nidoabilisaŋa. Teman ale Omar ale Zefo ale Kenaz ale

ref: Korah, Gatam, and Amalek. These and Esau's other descendants lived in the land of Edom.
text: Koora ale Gatam ale Amalek. Nalima ŋaai ale nyini Elifaz po a bo Edom la ale la. Ba jam ka Ada ŋaaŋsa.

ref: The clans that descended from Esau and Basemath's son Reuel were Nahath, Zerah, Shammah, and Mizzah.
text: Iisɔ nidoabinni Areuel nidoabilisaŋa ale nna. Nayat ale Zera ale Sammaya ale Mizza jam ka nalima a nyini Areuel po a bo Edom la. Ba jam ka Iisɔ powa Basemat ŋaaŋsa.

ref: The clans that descended from Esau and Oholibamah the daughter of Anah were Jeush, Jalam, and Korah.
text: Iisɔ powa Oyolibama nidoabilisaŋa ale nna. Jeus ale Jalam ale Koora jam ka nalima a nyini Iisɔ powa Oyolibama waai ale ka Anaya lie la.

ref: All of these clans descended from Esau, who was known as Edom.
text: Iisɔ nidoabilisaŋa jam ale la. Ba nalimaŋa mɛ ale la.

ref: Seir was from the Horite tribe that had lived in Edom before the time of Esau. The clans that had descended from him were Lotan, Shobal, Zibeon, Anah,
text: Yori denɔwa Seir nidoabilisaŋa jam le nna. Ba jam bo ka tɛŋka po. Ba le Lotan ale Sobal ale Zibeon ale Anaya ale

ref: Lotan's sons were Hori and Heman; his sister was Timna.
text: Lotan nidoabilisaŋa jam le nna. Yori ale Yeman. Timna jam ka Lotan toa.

ref: Shobal's sons were Alvan, Manahath, Ebal, Shepho, and Onam.
text: Sobal nidoabilisaŋa jam le nna. Alvan ale Manahat ale Ebal ale Sefo ale Onam.

ref: Zibeon's sons were Aiah and Anah—the same Anah who found an oasis in the desert while taking the donkeys of his father out to pasture.
text: Zibeon nidoabilisaŋa jam le nna. Aiaya ale Anaya. Anaya wa dɛ ale jam nya buli tuilisaŋa gɔnni po wa le jam a nya wa kowa Zibeon bonsaŋa zuk ate si a dɛ la.

ref: Anah's children were Dishon and Oholibamah.
text: Anaya bisaŋa jam le nna. Dison ale Oyolibama Anaya liewa.

ref: Dishon's sons were Hemdan, Eshban, Ithran, and Cheran.
text: Dison nidoabilisaŋa jam le nna. Yemdan ale Esban ale Itran ale Cheran.

ref: Ezer's sons were Bilhan, Zaavan, and Akan.
text: Ezer nidoabilisaŋa jam le nna. Bilyan ale Zaavan ale Akan.

ref: Dishan's sons were Uz and Aran.
text: Disan nidoabilisaŋa jam le nna. Uz ale Aran.

ref: The clans of the Horites were Lotan, Shobal, Zibeon, Anah,
text: Yori demma nalimaŋa jam le nna. Lotan ale Sobal ale Zibeon ale Anaya ale

ref: Dishon, Ezer, and Dishan, and they lived in the land of Seir.
text: Dison ale Ezer ale Disan. Yori demma nalimaŋa jam ale la a va chaab ale ba poori dii Seir tɛŋka po la.

ref: Before there were kings in Israel, the following kings ruled Edom one after another: Bela son of Beor from Dinhabah; Jobab son of Zerah from Bozrah; Husham from the land of Teman; Hadad son of Bedad from Avith ; Samlah from Masrekah; Shaul from the city of Rehoboth on the Euphrates River; Baalhanan son of Achbor; Hadar from the city of Pau .
text: Baal Yanan, Achbor nidoabinni ale kpi la, Yadar ale dɛ naamu. Wa tɛŋka ba jam te ka yuei ayen ka Pau. Wa powa yuei jam le Meyetabel. Wa jam ka Matred lie. Matred mɛ jam ka Mezayab lie.

ref: The clans that descended from Esau took their names from their families and the places where they lived. They are Timna, Alvah, Jetheth,
text: Nalima ŋaai ale jam nyini Iisɔ buuni po ale ba yue a va chaab ale ba diina ale ba tɛŋsa ale nna. Timna ale Alva ale Jetet ale

ref: Oholibamah, Elah, Pinon,
text: Oyolibama ale Elaya ale Pinon ale

ref: Kenaz, Teman, Mibzar,
text: Kenaz ale Teman ale Mibzar ale

ref: Magdiel, and Iram. These clans descended from Esau, who was known as Edom, the father of the Edomites. They took their names from the places where they settled.
text: Magdiel ale Iram. Edom nalimaŋa ale la a va chaab ale ba ale kali dii tɛŋ kaai ate ba jam boro la. Wade jam ka Iisɔ Edom demma kowa.

ref: Jacob lived in the land of Canaan, where his father Isaac had lived,
text: Jekob jam bo ka tɛŋ kaai ate wa kowa jam boro la, Keenan tɛŋka.

ref: “They're not here anymore,” the man replied. “I overheard them say they were going to Dothan.” Joseph left and found his brothers in Dothan.
text: Nuruwa yaa tulisi wa ayen, <<Ba nyini dela a cheŋ kama, mi wom ba weeni ayen, ti cheŋ Dotan.>> Dila Josefi yaa saŋ wa suataŋa ŋaaŋ a ga nya ba Dotan.

ref: Jacob loved Joseph more than he did any of his other sons, because Joseph was born when Jacob was very old. Jacob had even given Joseph a fancy coat
text: Izirali jam yaali Josefi a gaam wa nidoabili sinsila kama dii nyiŋla wa jam biak wa ka wa kpaŋsa po. Wa yaa nye garuk kuui ale nala a te wa.

ref: which showed that Joseph was his favorite son, and so Joseph's brothers hated him and would not be friendly to him.
text: Wa suataŋa ale nya ayen wa yaali wa a gaam ba kama la ba yaa kisi wa a kan biisi wamaŋa a te wa.

ref: One day, Joseph told his brothers what he had dreamed, and they hated him even more.
text: Josefi yaa goa daasim. Wa le weeni sak wa suataŋa la ba yaa ŋmasi kisi wa yega.

ref: Joseph said, “Let me tell you about my dream.
text: Wa weeni ba ayen, <<Ni wom gɔdaasimaŋa de ate mi a gɔdaasim la.

ref: We were out in the field, tying up bundles of wheat. Suddenly my bundle stood up, and your bundles gathered around and bowed down to it.”
text: Ti boro a bobi kpari ŋanta laŋka po ate mi kpari ŋantaŋa deri yiti zaani, alege ate nama bobaŋa a zaani gilim mi koori tɛŋ di niŋ.>>

ref: His brothers asked, “Do you really think you are going to be king and rule over us?” Now they hated Joseph more than ever because of what he had said about his dream.
text: Wa suataŋa yaa weeni ayen, <<Fi a yaali ayen fi a dɛ naam tama zuk kama? Fi le sum a nya tama zuwa? Ate ba yaa ŋmasi kisi wa yega wa gɔdaasimaŋa nyiŋ ale wa le weeni diila nyiŋ.

ref: Joseph later had another dream, and he told his brothers, “Listen to what else I dreamed. The sun, the moon, and eleven stars bowed down to me.”
text: Josefi yaa ŋman gɔdaasim a weeni wa suataŋa. Wa weeni ayen, <<Ni wom mi ŋmana, mi gɔdaasim kama. Yɔgyɔgla de wenbinni ale chiika ale chiŋmarisa pi ale kanyi ale koori a zuli mi.>>

ref: When he told his father about this dream, his father became angry and said, “What's that supposed to mean? Are your mother and I and your brothers all going to come and bow down to you?”
text: Wa le weeni wa kowa ale wa suataŋa la, wa kowa yaa toariŋ wa ale weeni ayen, <<Ka boan gɔdaasima? Fi mawa ale mi ale fi suataŋa ale sum jam koori tɛŋ fi niŋŋa?>>

ref: Joseph's brothers were jealous of him, but his father kept wondering about the dream. Joseph Is Sold and Taken to Egypt
text: Ate wa suataŋa a jaa dɛ nyuri ale wa alege wa kowa a ta ku wa popola po.

ref: One day when Joseph's brothers had taken the sheep to a pasture near Shechem,
text: Josefi suataŋa yaa ta ba kowa piisaŋa a cheŋ ayen ŋa dɛ a moata Sikiim.

ref: his father Jacob said to him, “I want you to go to your brothers. They are with the sheep near Shechem.” “Yes, sir,” Joseph answered.
text: Izirali yaa weeni Josefi ayen, <<Ase fi le poom seba la, fi suataŋa a ta piisaŋa a moata ka Sikiim ate ŋa a dɛ. Jam, mi a yaali ayen m tom fu kama ba jigi.>>

ref: His father said, “Go and find out how your brothers and the sheep are doing. Then come back and let me know.” So he sent him from Hebron Valley. Joseph was near Shechem
text: Wa yaa weeni wa ayen, <<Cheŋ ga nya ase wari meena a cheŋ nalimnyiini ale fi suataŋa ale piisaŋa abe fi jam weeni mu.>> Ate wa yaa tom wa ate wa zaani Yebron Viaka a cheŋ. Josefi ale ga paari Sikiim la,

ref: and wandering through the fields, when a man asked, “What are you looking for?”
text: nuru yaa nya wa bo laŋsaŋa po a nyiem a gilim a yaa bek wa ayen, <<Fi a gisi ka boa?>>

ref: Joseph answered, “I'm looking for my brothers who are watching the sheep. Can you tell me where they are?”
text: Wa yaa weeni wa ayen, <<Mi a gisi ka m suataŋa. Fi baga a dak ba le ta ba piisaŋa a bo juijui ate ŋa a dɛ la?>>

ref: But before he got there, they saw him coming and made plans to kill him.
text: Alege ba zaani niŋ a nya wa. Alege ate wa paari ba la, ba deri zɔk kama ayen ba ko wa.

ref: They said to one another, “Look, here comes the hero of those dreams!
text: Ba weeni chaab ayen, <<Gɔdaasima nyɔnɔwa ale la cheena.>>

ref: Reuben heard this and tried to protect Joseph from them. “Let's not kill him,” he said.
text: Aruben ale wom nna la, wa yaa pii nyiŋ ayen wa vari wa ba nisima po a basi. Wa weeni ayen, <<Ni kan te ti ko wa.>>

ref: “Don't murder him or even harm him. Just throw him into a well out here in the desert.” Reuben planned to rescue Joseph later and take him back to his father.
text: Wa ŋman weeni ayen, <<Ni kan tiri ziimoa. Ni yuk wa basi vinni de po gɔnni po dela. Ni kan pai nisa a tiri wa a ko wa.>> Aruben a weeni nna la ayen ka wa vari wa ba jigi a ta wa a ga te wa kowa.

ref: When Joseph came to his brothers, they pulled off his fancy coat
text: Josefi ale jam wa suataŋa jigi la, ba yaa yeeri wa garuk naliŋka ate wa jo la.

ref: As Joseph's brothers sat down to eat, they looked up and saw a caravan of Ishmaelites coming from Gilead. Their camels were loaded with all kinds of spices that they were taking to Egypt.
text: Ba le kali a dɛ ba ŋandiinta la, ba yaa nya Ismael dem a bo ba toroko po a nyini Gilead a cheena. Ba laalaakomima a ji ka ŋan baai nyum ale masa la, ale ŋan baai ate ba dan pai sa jaab wa kan kaasi la ale tiib sim, a cheŋ Ejipiti.

ref: So Judah said, “What will we gain if we kill our brother and hide his body?
text: Juda yaa weeni wa suataŋa ayen, <<Ka boan nyuenta ate ti le nya ase ti nin ko ti yua baaŋka alege lik wa ziimu?

ref: Let's sell him to the Ishmaelites and not harm him. After all, he is our brother.” And the others agreed.
text: Ni jam, ni te ti pai wa da te Ismael demma abe ti kan pai nisa ko wa, wa ka ti suok, tidek nyiŋ lam ale ziim. Wa suataŋa a yaa siak.

ref: When the Midianite merchants came by, Joseph's brothers took him out of the well, and for
text: Midian geruŋdɔma ale jam paari la, wa suataŋa yaa dari Josefi yeeri vinni po a pai wa da kpana ligbie pisiye a te Ismael demma ate ba ta wa cheŋ.

ref: When Reuben returned to the well and did not find Joseph there, he tore his clothes in sorrow.
text: Aruben ale ŋman cheŋ vinni a nya ale Josefi karo la, wa yaa dari wa gattaŋa cheeri.

ref: Then he went back to his brothers and said, “The boy is gone! What am I going to do?”
text: Wa yaa ŋman cheŋ wa suataŋa jigi a weeni ba ayen, <<Biika karo. Mi m cheem va ka bɛ?>>

ref: Joseph's brothers killed a goat and dipped Joseph's fancy coat in its blood.
text: Ba yaa pai Josefi garukku, a tɔgri buuk a pai garukku a lusi ziimu po.

ref: After this, they took the coat to their father and said, “We found this! Look at it carefully and see if it belongs to your son.”
text: Ba yaa ta Josefi garuk naliŋka a cheŋ wa kowa jigi a weeni wa ayen, <<Tama ale nya diila ale nna. Kpesi nya ase ka fi biika garukku ale la ya.>>

ref: Jacob knew it was Joseph's coat and said, “It's my son's coat! Joseph has been torn to pieces and eaten by some wild animal.”
text: Wa yaa miŋ ku a weeni ayen, <<Ka m biika garukku ale la. Gɔi duŋ ale yik m biika. Ka sum dari Josefi a passi ka zɔna zɔna.>>

ref: Jacob mourned for Joseph a long time, and to show his sorrow he tore his clothes and wore sackcloth.
text: Jekob yaa dari wa gattaŋa cheeri a pai garuk zagsik a jo a kum wa biika da boari.

ref: All of Jacob's children came to comfort him, but he refused to be comforted. “No,” he said, “I will go to my grave, mourning for my son.” So Jacob kept on grieving.
text: Wa nidoabilisaŋa ale wa liema a jam ayen ba yik wa ate wa sui yɔk, alege wa zɛri. Wa weeni ayen, <<Aaya, mi a kum kama ayen m ga siŋ vɔrup a cheŋ m biika jigi.>> Dila wa kowa a yaa kum wa biika.

ref: Meanwhile, the Midianites had sold Joseph in Egypt to a man named Potiphar, who was the king's official in charge of the palace guard.
text: Alege dila meena, Midian demma a pai Josefi a da ka Ejipiti a te Potifar, Faaro niŋdeerɔma wanyi, limisidɔma kpagni.

ref: About that time Judah left his brothers in the hill country and went to live near his friend Hirah in the town of Adullam.
text: Va ŋala po, Juda yaa basi wa suataŋa ale cheŋ a ga bo le nuru ate wa nyini Adullam tɛŋka. Wa yuei jam le Yira.

ref: While there he met the daughter of Shua, a Canaanite man. Judah married her,
text: Dula Juda yaa tu Keenan denɔ lie. Keenan denɔwa yuei jam le Sɛyua. Wa yaa faari wa. Wa yaa goa ale wa,

ref: and they had three sons. He named the first one Er;
text: ate wa yik lɔgi a biak nidoabili ate ba te wa yuei ayen Ɛri.

ref: The third one was born when Judah was in Chezib, and she named him Shelah.
text: Wa ŋman biak nidoabili wa choa ate ba te wa yuei ayen Sɛla. Ba jam biak wa ka Kezib tɛŋka.

ref: Later, Judah chose Tamar as a wife for Er, his oldest son.
text: Juda jam yaali ka nipok a te Ɛri waai ate wa liŋ biak la. Nipowa yuei jam le Tamara.

ref: But Er was very evil, and the Lord took his life.
text: Alege Juda bikpagni Ɛri le jam nye diila a bie kama Naawen nimbie po, dila nyiŋ Naawen yaa ko wa.

ref: So Judah told Onan, “It's your duty to marry Tamar and have a child for your brother.”
text: Juda weeni Onan ayen, <<Goa ale fi suoku powa a tom dii ale magsi la ase fi suok pok la a biak a te fi suoku.>>

ref: Onan knew the child would not be his, and when he had sex with Tamar, he made sure that she would not get pregnant.
text: Alege Onan seba ayen bisaŋa a kan chim wa bisa, dila wa nyiem yaa le boro a goa le wa suoku powa la wa yaa sam basi tɛŋ ate wa kan biak a te wa suoku.

ref: The Lord wasn't pleased with Onan and took his life too.
text: Wa le jam nye diila a bie kama Naawen nimbie po, dila wa yaa ko wa mɛ.

ref: Judah did not want the same thing to happen to his son Shelah, and he told Tamar, “Go home to your father and live there as a widow until my son Shelah is grown.” So Tamar went to live with her father.
text: Juda yaa weeni wa sampowa Tamara ayen, <<Bo fi kowa yenni ase pukɔgi la ate m biika Sɛla pak.>> Diila wa poli kama ayen, <<wa mɛ ale kpi ase wa suataŋa la.>> Dila Tamara yaa cheŋ ga bo wa kowa yenni.

ref: Some years later Judah's wife died, and he mourned for her. He then went with his friend Hirah to the town of Timnah, where his sheep were being sheared.
text: Ku ŋaaŋnyiŋ ku le jam beni la, Juda powa Sɛyua liewa yaa kpi. Juda suinni ale ga yɔk la wa yaa cheŋ Timnaya nuru baai ale jaa che wa piisaŋa kɔbtaŋa la jigi. Wa doawa Adullam denɔwa Yira jam va wa cheŋ kama.

ref: Tamar found out that her father-in-law Judah was going to Timnah to shear his sheep.
text: Ba le weeni Tamara ayen, <<Fi ŋiak nidoawa a bo ka suik a cheŋ Timnaya ayen wa che wa piisaŋa kɔbtaŋa la,

ref: She also realized that Shelah was now a grown man, but she had not been allowed to marry him. So she decided to dress in something other than her widow's clothes and to cover her face with a veil. After this, she sat outside the town of Enaim on the road to Timnah.
text: wa yaa yeeri wa pukɔgi gattaŋa, ale lik wadek ale garuk nyaara nyaara ayen ba kan miŋ wa. Wa yaa kali nansuiŋku ale a cheŋ Enaim la, juijui ale bo Timnaya suiku po. Diila wa nya kama ayen Sɛla a chim kama yɔgla, ba an pai wa te wa ase wa pok la.

ref: When Judah came along, he did not recognize her because of the veil. He thought she was a prostitute
text: Juda ale nya wa la wa pai wa chim ka nidoa yaalik, diila wa lik wa nimbidiaka kama.

text: Wa yaa tulisi ayen, <<M le te fu buuk a nyini m duŋsaŋa po.>> Nipowa yaa ŋman bek wa ayen, <<Fi le te mu jaab ase pumpuulima la ate fi ga tom te?>>

ref: “What do you want?” he asked in return. “The ring on that cord around your neck,” was her reply. “I also want the special walking stick you have with you.” He gave them to her, they slept together, and she became pregnant.
text: Wa yaa weeni ayen, <<M te fu boan jaab pumpuulima? Wa yaa tulisi ayen, <<Fi nifelinni ale di miika ale fi nisa po doani.>> Wa yaa pai te wa ale goa ale wa ate wa yik lɔgi.

ref: After returning home, Tamar took off the veil and dressed in her widow's clothes again.

ref: Judah asked his friend Hirah take a goat to the woman, so he could get back the ring and walking stick, but she wasn't there.
text: Juda a jam tom ka wa doawa Adullam denɔwa ale buuk ayen wa tuesi wa tachekka ate wa pai te wa la, alege wa doawa an jam nya wa.

text: Wa jam bek ka nuru baai ale bo dula la ayen, <<Bɔgluk taabuka a bo ka be waai ale bo Enaim suiku nakpiaka la.>> Ba yaa tulisi ayen, <<Bɔgluk taabuk a ka dela.>>

ref: Hirah went back and told Judah, “I couldn't find the woman, and the people of Enaim said no prostitute had ever been there.”

text: Ate wa suoku waai ale ta garuk miik mɔniŋka wa nisa po la a yaa nyini ate ba te wa yuei ayen Zera.

ref: The Ishmaelites took Joseph to Egypt and sold him to Potiphar, the king's official in charge of the palace guard.
text: Yɔgyɔgla ba jam ta Josefi a cheŋ ka Ejipiti. Potifar Ejipiti denɔ waai ale jam ka Faaro nyaarɔma kpagi la a da wa a ta nyini Ismael demma waai ale ta wa a cheŋ dula la jigi.

ref: So Joseph lived in the home of Potiphar, his Egyptian owner. Soon Potiphar realized that the Lord was helping Joseph to be successful in whatever he did.

text: Wa yaa jam pai garukku a dueni ka wa tɛŋ ate wa kpagni a ga jam yeri.

text: Ate wa yaa weeni wa ayen, <<Yiburu yɔmawa ate fi ta jam a te ti la a jam kama ayen wa lɛ ti.

text: Alege mi ale ga kaari ayen ba maari mu la, wa yaa basi wa garukku a dueni m tɛŋ ale yiti chali nyini yenni de po.>>

ref: and threw Joseph in the same prison where the king's prisoners were kept. While Joseph was in prison,
text: Josefi kpagni yaa jam yik wa a lik juijui ate naawa a lik wa nuruma la. Alege Josefi ale jam bo likka dɔkku po la,

text: Naawen a jam bo ale wa kama. Ate wa sak nyemagsika ale te wa ŋmanteka waai ale a nya ligrɔma zuk la jigi.

ref: While Joseph was in prison, both the king's personal servant and his chief cook made the king angry. So he had them thrown into the same prison with Joseph.

ref: They spent a long time in prison, and the official in charge of the palace guard, made Joseph their servant.

text: Vari ale veenti ate Josefi a jam ba jigi la, wa yaa jam nya ale ba sue a kaasi.

text: Ba yaa tulisi ayen, <<Ti meena a gɔdaasim kama alege nuru karo a weeni ŋa kiriya.>> Josefi yaa weeni ba ayen, <<Gɔdaasima kie miŋka daa Wen ale soa? Ni weeni mu ni gɔdaasimaŋa.>>

ref: The king's personal servant told Joseph, “In my dream I saw a vine
text: Ate daam teerɔ kpagni yaa weeni Josefi wa gɔdaasimaŋa. Wa weeni wa ayen, <<M gɔdaasimaŋa po m nya ka tiib m niŋ,

text: ate tiimu zuk naŋsa sita a boro. Naŋsaŋa ale pusi la si deri chim a yɔn yɔna a muni.

text: Faaro beerika a jam bo ka m nisa po, mi yaa pai yɔnaŋa ŋmiak a nyo Faaro beerika po a te wa.>>

text: Josefi yaa weeni wa ayen. <<Di kiri ale, naŋsa sitawa ka daa ŋata.

text: Daa ŋata po Faaro ale zak fi zuk a zaani a ŋman pai fu a zaani fi naŋkulaŋa po, ate fi ale pai Faaro beerika a nyo wa nisa po ase fi ale jam a nye diila, ase diipo ate fi jam chim daam teerɔ la.

ref: But when these good things happen, please don't forget to tell the king about me, so I can get out of this place.

ref: I was kidnapped from the land of the Hebrews, and here in Egypt I haven't done anything to deserve being thrown in jail.
text: Diila ba fɛ mi kama a ta nyini Yiburu demma tɛŋka a nyini, ate dela mɛ mi an nye wari ya a magsi ba a nyo mu likka dɔk po.>>

ref: When the chief cook saw that Joseph had given a good meaning to the dream, he told Joseph, “I also had a dream. In it I was carrying three breadbaskets stacked on top of my head.
text: Boroborok seerɔma kpagni ale jam nya ayen Josefi a tulisi magsi la, wa yaa weeni Josefi ayen, <<Mi mɛ gɔdaasim kama ayen, mi jika kpanjɔkta tita ale boroborok.

ref: The top basket was full of all kinds of baked things for the king, but birds were eating them.”
text: Ŋmani kpanjɔkku po ba jam sɛka ŋandiinta tɔgatɔga a nyo ku po a te Faaro, alege nuinsaŋa a jaa dɛ ti kama kpanjɔkku ate m ji la po.>>

ref: Joseph said: This is the meaning of your dream. The three baskets are three days,
text: Josefi yaa weeni ayen, <<Kude kiri ale nna, kpanjɔkta titawa ka daa ŋata.

ref: and in three days the king will cut off your head. He will hang your body on a pole, and birds will come and peck at it.
text: Daa ŋata po, Faaro ale gebi fi zukku a pai fi nyiŋka a saŋ tiib nyiŋ. Ate nuinsaŋa ale dɛ ka.>>

ref: Three days later, while the king was celebrating his birthday with a dinner for his officials, he sent for his personal servant and the chief cook.
text: Yɔgyɔgla de daa ŋatawa a jam ka Faaro biam dai, ate wa yaa jam nye tigi a te wa kpaŋŋa meena. Wa jam zak daam teerɔ kpagni ale boroborok seerɔma kpagni zuima a zaani wa kpaŋŋa niŋ.

ref: and had the cook put to death. Everything happened just as Joseph had said it would,
text: alege wa jam pai boroborok seerɔ kpagni a yik ka tiib nyiŋ, ase Josefi ale weeni gɔdaasimaŋa kiri diila.

ref: but the king's personal servant completely forgot about Joseph.
text: Alege daam teerɔ kpagni an jam teeri Josefi wari ya, wa jam baŋ wa wari kama.

ref: Two years later the king of Egypt dreamed he was standing beside the Nile River.
text: Bena ŋaye ale taam la, Faaro yaa gɔdaasim. Wa jam za ka Nile benni nɔi,

ref: Suddenly, seven fat, healthy cows came up from the river and started eating grass along the bank.
text: ate niiga ŋayopɔi yaa nyini benni po a nyaksi ale biigi, ate ŋa yaa bo wuuku po a dɛ.

ref: Then seven ugly, skinny cows came up out of the river and
text: Ŋala ŋaaŋnyiŋ niiga ŋayopɔi a buk a lɔrima a nyini Nile benni po a jueli za naa ŋaai ale za beni nɔi la gauŋ.

ref: The king went back to sleep and had another dream. This time seven full heads of grain were growing on a single stalk.
text: Wa jam a lo ka goom po a gɔdaasim a nye buye. Wa gɔdaasimaŋa po wa yaa nya chɔlomben a pɛ chɔlombena ŋayopɔi ate ŋa nyiŋsa a pagra ale nala.

ref: Later, seven other heads of grain appeared, but they were thin and scorched by a wind from the desert.
text: Ŋan maŋsaŋa ale nye nueri la, ŋan jetisa ŋayopɔi a pusi nyini a nye ase viok tuilik ale wobri ŋa la.

ref: The thin heads of grain swallowed the seven full heads. Again the king woke up, and it had only been a dream.
text: Chɔlomben ŋaai ale jeta la a yaa nɛ chɔlomben ŋaai ale a nyaksi ale biigi la, ate Faaro yaa yiti wa gɔdaasimaŋa po.

ref: The next morning the king was upset. So he called in his magicians and wise men and told them what he had dreamed. None of them could tell him what the dreams meant.
text: Ku saluiku wa popola yaa ta wa a daani, ate wa tom wi sampoporisaŋa ale yam nyama meena Ejipiti tɛŋka po. Faaro yaa weeni ba wa gɔdaasimaŋa alege waai a karo a weeni ŋa kiri a sak wa.

ref: The king's personal servant said: Now I remember what I was supposed to do.
text: Ate daam teerɔ kpagni yaa weeni Faaro ayen, <<Jinla m teeri m kaasuŋku.

ref: When you were angry with me and your chief cook, you threw us both in jail in the house of the captain of the guard.
text: Faaro sui yaa jam puuri ale wa tomteerɔma, ate wa yaa jam yik mi a lik ale boroborok seerɔma kpagni limisidɔma kpagni yenni po.

ref: One night we both had dreams, and each dream had a different meaning.
text: Ti meena a jam gɔdaasim kama yok yeŋ po, ate gɔdaasimaŋa meena a ta ŋa kiri.

ref: A young Hebrew, who was a servant of the captain of the guard, was there with us at the time. When we told him our dreams, he explained what each of them meant,
text: Ate Yiburu bi paalik a yaa jam bo dula ale ti, limisidɔma kpagni tomteerɔ. Ti weeni wa ti gɔdaasimaŋa ate wa weeni ŋa kiri a sak ti.

ref: and everything happened just as he said it would. I got my job back, and the cook was put to death.
text: Ate wie a sum tulim nye a magsi chaab ale wa ale weeni ti diila. Mi ŋman pilim nya m kpaŋtaŋa kama, ate nuru nwala ba pai wa a yik tiib nyiŋ.>>

ref: The king sent for Joseph, who was quickly brought out of jail. He shaved, changed his clothes, and went to the king.
text: Ate Faaro yaa tom wi Josefi, ate ba ta wa nyini likka dɔkku po a nyini nwuli. Ba ale poni wa zukku ale tagri wa kɔlimaŋa la, wa yaa jam Faaro niŋ.

ref: The king said to him, “I had a dream, yet no one can explain what it means. I am told that you can interpret dreams.”
text: Faaro yaa weeni Josefi ayen, <<M gɔdaasim kama ate waai an baga a weeni mu ŋa kiri ya. Alege mi wom kama ale ba weeni ayen, fi dan wom fi baga a weeni ŋa kiri kama.>>

ref: “Your Majesty,” Joseph answered, “I can't do it myself, but God can give a good meaning to your dreams.”
text: Josefi yaa tulisi Faaro ayen, <<Mi an baga a nye, alege Wen ale te Faaro weeni ŋaai ate wa a yaali la.>>

ref: The king told Joseph: I dreamed I was standing on the bank of the Nile River.
text: Ate Faaro yaa weeni Josefi ayen, <<M gɔdaasimaŋa po m jam za ka Nile benni nɔi,

ref: I saw seven fat, healthy cows come up out of the river, and they began feeding on the grass.
text: Ate niiga ŋayopɔi a nyini benni po a jueli a biigi ale a nyaksi a bo wuutaŋa po a dɛ.

ref: Next, seven skinny, bony cows came up out of the river. I have never seen such terrible looking cows anywhere in Egypt.
text: Ŋala ŋaaŋnyiŋ, niiga gela ŋayopɔi ate ŋa jeta ale lɔrima ale buk. Mi an diem nya naa buloatila Ejipiti tɛŋka meena po.

ref: But you couldn't tell it, because these skinny cows were just as skinny as they were before. At once, I woke up.
text: Alege ŋa le poom a nɛ ŋa a nueri la, waai an baga a weeni ayen ŋa nɛ jaaboa. Ŋa lɔrima ase ka ŋa le poom ka diila. Ate mi yaa yiti.

ref: I also dreamed that I saw seven heads of grain growing on one stalk. The heads were full and ripe.
text: <<M gɔdaasimaŋa po mi ŋman nya ka chɔlombena ŋayopɔi a sueri ale nala a bo kinka yeŋ nyiŋ a chim.

ref: Then seven other heads of grain came up. They were thin and scorched by a wind from the desert.
text: Ŋala ŋaaŋnyiŋ chɔlombena ŋayopɔi yaa pusi ŋala nyiŋ a kɔ a jeta ate kori vioku a fuk ŋa a kaasi.

ref: These heads of grain swallowed the full ones. I told my dreams to the magicians, but none of them could tell me the meaning of the dreams.
text: Chɔlomben ŋaai ale jeta la a yaa nɛ chɔlombena ŋayopɔi ŋaai ale nala la. Mi weeni nya kude a sak sampoporisaŋa alege ba waai an baga a tɔk ku kiri a sak mu.>>

ref: Joseph replied: Your Majesty, both of your dreams mean the same thing, and in them God has shown what he is going to do.
text: Ate Josefi yaa weeni Faaro ayen, <<Gɔdaasimaŋa meena ka bunyi. Wen a pai sak Faaro wa le za wa nye diila kama.

ref: The seven good cows stand for seven years, and so do the seven good heads of grain.
text: Naa naliŋsa ŋayopɔiwa kiri ale bena ŋayopɔi, chɔlombena ŋayopɔiwa kiri ale bena ŋayopɔi. Gɔdaasimaŋa meena ka bunyi.

ref: The seven skinny, ugly cows that came up later also stand for seven years, as do the seven bad heads of grain that were scorched by the desert wind. The dreams mean there will be seven years when there won't be enough grain.
text: Niiga ŋayopɔi ŋaai ale bugi a lɔrima la ale jam ŋaaŋ nyiŋla ka bena ŋayopɔi, ku ka dila mɛ ale chɔlombena ŋayopɔi ŋaai ate kori vioku a fuk ŋa a kaasi la. Ku a magsi ka chaab ale bena ŋayopɔi kɔm.

ref: It is just as I said—God has shown what he intends to do.
text: <<Ku ka dii dek ate m weeni sak Faaro la. Wen a sak wa le boro a yaali wa nye diila kama.

ref: For seven years Egypt will have more than enough grain,
text: Bena ŋayopɔi ŋandiinta yegayega ale jam Ejipiti tɛŋka po,

ref: when the king made him governor, and he went everywhere for the king.
text: alege bena ŋayopɔi kɔm ale pai saŋ. Ate ba le baŋ ŋandiinta yegayega wari Ejipiti tɛŋka po, ate kɔm ale bo tɛŋka po.
